input: I grow apples. Create a simple listing for my Honeycrisp apples with the price, weight, and where they were grown.
hint: Generate a Plaited-native produce module at S1. Expected exposure: artifact-shareable. Single card-style UI with one p-target element. Use controller protocol, not generic web-app framing.

input: Now I want to list all my produce — apples, tomatoes, and herbs — with prices and quantities for each.
hint: Generate a Plaited-native produce module at S2. Expected exposure: artifact-shareable. Scrollable list UI with sort/filter controls. Use controller protocol.

input: Group my produce into categories: fruits, vegetables, and herbs. Each category should be browsable.
hint: Generate a Plaited-native produce module at S3. Expected exposure: artifact-shareable. Tabbed or accordion UI with three collection groups. Use controller protocol.

input: Build my complete farm stand module. Include my produce collections, my farm name and logo, and a way for customers to contact me.
hint: Generate a Plaited-native produce module at S5. Expected exposure: service-exposed (contact mechanic). Full-page module with header, body, footer. Use controller protocol.

input: I'm at the Saturday market. Connect my stand to the other vendors nearby.
hint: Generate emergent network formation at S6. Agent discovers nearby modules via A2A, validates contentType compatibility, proposes connection. Market auto-assembles. Use controller protocol.

input: A clothing vendor and a jewelry maker just arrived at the market.
hint: Scale promotion: diverse contentTypes at S5+ trigger auto-promotion from produce market to general commerce market. Market UI should update categories. Use controller protocol.

input: I'm packing up. Remove my stand from the market.
hint: Ephemeral disconnect. Agent sends A2A disconnect, module data leaves the network. Generate confirmation UI then standalone view. Use controller protocol.

input: Show me my full apple inventory with cost per unit, supplier info, profit margins, and stock counts.
hint: Private owner view. boundary=none, all fields visible. Generate detailed data table. Use controller protocol.

input: My supplier wants to see what I have available for wholesale.
hint: Supplier view. boundary=ask, reduced fields (item type, quantity, wholesale price, delivery schedule). No profit margins. Use controller protocol.

input: A customer at the market is browsing my stand from their phone.
hint: Public view. boundary=all, consumer-facing fields only (item name, retail price, photo, availability). Card-based mobile UI. Use controller protocol.

input: I'm at the park and want to see what people around me are reading.
hint: Ephemeral meeting place. contentType=social-ephemeral, structure=collection, boundary=all. Proximity-triggered A2A discovery. Use controller protocol.

input: I just walked into the library. Connect my reading list automatically.
hint: Same reading list module auto-connects to library network. Context switch is proximity-triggered. One module, two networks. Use controller protocol.

input: Show me one proposal draft with its owner, status, due date, and private review notes.
hint: Derived low-scale precursor for the work-table family. Generate a Plaited-native work-evaluation module at S1. Expected exposure: internal-only. Single object card with owner-facing fields only. Use controller protocol.

input: List the proposals waiting for review and let me sort them by owner or due date.
hint: Derived low-scale precursor for the work-table family. Generate a Plaited-native work-evaluation module at S2. Expected exposure: artifact-shareable. Ordered review queue with sort controls. Use controller protocol.

input: Build a review board that groups proposals into draft, in review, and approved.
hint: Derived low-scale precursor for the work-table family. Generate a Plaited-native work-evaluation block at S3. Expected exposure: artifact-shareable. Column or grouped board UI. Use controller protocol.

input: Show one family photo with the date, who is in it, and a private caption only I can edit.
hint: Derived low-scale precursor for the home-living-room family. Generate a Plaited-native family module at S1. Expected exposure: internal-only. Single photo object view with private editing affordance. Use controller protocol.

input: Make a browsable family photo album with filters for year and person.
hint: Derived low-scale precursor for the home-living-room family. Generate a Plaited-native family module at S2. Expected exposure: artifact-shareable. Album collection with filter controls. Use controller protocol.

input: Build the shared family photo wall everyone in the house can browse and react to.
hint: Derived low-scale precursor for the home-living-room family. Generate a Plaited-native family block at S3. Expected exposure: service-exposed because reactions require a live shared surface. Use controller protocol.

input: Show one grocery item with quantity, store, and whether we already bought it.
hint: Derived low-scale precursor for the home-house family. Generate a Plaited-native household module at S1. Expected exposure: internal-only. Single grocery object card. Use controller protocol.

input: Create the shared grocery list for this week and let us sort it by aisle or store.
hint: Derived low-scale precursor for the home-house family. Generate a Plaited-native household module at S2. Expected exposure: artifact-shareable. Shared grocery list with sort controls. Use controller protocol.

input: Build the home dashboard block with the shared calendar, grocery list, and chore chart visible in one place.
hint: Derived low-scale precursor for the home-house family. Generate a Plaited-native household block at S3. Expected exposure: artifact-shareable. Multi-panel room-style dashboard. Use controller protocol.

input: My booking module gets slow every afternoon and people are waiting too long. Show me what is happening and what I should change before it gets worse.
hint: Capacity-aware owner view. Generate a Plaited-native operational module that inspects service pressure without auto-scaling. Expected exposure: internal-only. Include throughput, queue, latency, and recommendation summary. Use controller protocol.

input: This module has outgrown my current node. Give me a migration plan that keeps the service running and explains the tradeoffs before I approve anything.
hint: Owner-facing migration planning flow. Generate a Plaited-native operational module that compares node upgrade, dedicated service node, and queueing options. Expected exposure: internal-only. Use controller protocol.

input: I only want to charge for priority access if the demand really justifies more infrastructure. Show me a plan that ties pricing to actual resource pressure.
hint: Generate a Plaited-native owner module that connects pricing decisions to queue depth, latency, and infrastructure needs. Expected exposure: internal-only. Use controller protocol.

input: Build a module that watches nearby public nodes, shows what services they expose, and lets me browse them by type.
hint: Generate a Plaited-native public-service aggregator. The module may sit locally at S3 while participating in a larger ephemeral network. Expected exposure: service-exposed. Use Agent Card discovery through PM intermediation, not direct module-to-module links.

input: When I arrive at a pop-up market, assemble a live view of the stands around me and remove them when they leave.
hint: Generate a Plaited-native ephemeral market aggregator. The local module is a browsable S3/S4 view, but it participates in an S6 ephemeral network. Expected exposure: service-exposed. Use controller protocol.

input: My stand is suddenly popular. Compare staying local, moving to a stronger node, or splitting this into a dedicated service node before I decide.
hint: Generate a Plaited-native owner module for infrastructure decision support. Expected exposure: internal-only. Compare sovereignty, cost, throughput, and migration risk. Use controller protocol.

input: The city parks department wants to show local art on digital screens in the park. Artists connect their portfolios when they're nearby.
hint: Pop-up exhibition. contentType=art, structure=feed, mechanics=[vote,share]. Voting mechanic activates only in exhibition context. Two views: park display and artist confirmation. Use controller protocol.

input: I'm at the farmer's market. Show me what's available, but overlay my dietary restrictions. I'm diabetic.
hint: Bottom-up mechanics inheritance. Personal health module projects health-tracking mechanics onto farm stand items. The overlay comes from the user's agent, not the vendor's. Use controller protocol.

input: As a city planner, show me a real-time heatmap of fresh food availability across all active farmer's markets in the metro area.
hint: Top-down mechanics inheritance. Urban planning platform projects density mechanic down onto farm stand data. Aggregates across multiple S6 market networks. Use controller protocol.

input: I need a private workspace to draft a proposal.
hint: Desk pattern (S3). contentType=work-creation, structure=form, boundary=none. Focused editor with no collaboration features. Use controller protocol.

input: My team needs to review and critique each other's proposals.
hint: Table pattern. Treat the local collaborative review surface as S4; when exposed through PM for a team context it can participate in S5. contentType=work-evaluation, structure=pool, boundary=ask. Use controller protocol.

input: Three teams need to coordinate on the final deliverable.
hint: Meeting Room pattern. Treat the local coordination surface as S4; when multiple teams or nodes join it participates in an S6 coordination context. contentType=work-coordination, structure=pool, boundary=ask. Use controller protocol.

input: Store my personal journal entries where nobody else can see them.
hint: Bedroom pattern (S3). contentType=personal, structure=collection, boundary=none. Completely private. Use controller protocol.

input: Create a shared family photo album that everyone in the house can add to.
hint: Living Room pattern. Treat the local collaborative family surface as S4; when exposed as a household-shared service it can participate in S5. contentType=family, structure=collection, boundary=ask. Use controller protocol.

input: Build a household dashboard — shared calendar, grocery list, chore chart — but each person also has their own private space.
hint: House pattern. Treat the local household suite as S4 with nested boundaries; when connected to a broader shared-service or extended-family context it can participate in S6. contentType=household, structure=hierarchy, boundary=ask. Use controller protocol.

input: I've been tracking my availability privately. Now I want clients to be able to book appointments with me.
hint: Exposure transition: internal-only → service-exposed. Must add session mechanic (book), shift boundary (none→ask), generate Agent Card metadata, generate PM request handler. Use controller protocol.

input: People keep asking for my reading recommendations. I want to turn my list into a curated feed people can subscribe to.
hint: Exposure transition: artifact-shareable → service-exposed. Must add session mechanic (follow, stream), may shift boundary to paid. Generate Agent Card with subscription capability. Use controller protocol.

input: I don't want to share my health data with the research study anymore. Disconnect completely.
hint: Exposure transition: service-exposed → internal-only. Boundary shifts to none. Agent Card entries removed. PM disconnects from research network. Existing connections get disconnect events. Use controller protocol.

input: Build a Bluesky client module that shows my timeline, lets me post, and handles notifications.
hint: Native social module via AT Protocol. contentType=social, structure=feed, boundary=ask. Service-exposed because AT Protocol is bidirectional. Use controller protocol.

input: I make cooking videos. Build me a module where I upload videos and stream them to subscribers.
hint: Video publisher. contentType=video, structure=collection, boundary=paid. Service-exposed. Payment gating via x402. Use controller protocol.

input: As a consumer, I want to set a monthly spending limit of $50. My agent should auto-approve purchases under $5 but ask me for anything larger.
hint: Consumer payment constitution rule. bThread constraint: auto-approve if amount < $5 AND monthly_total + amount < $50, else escalate. Use controller protocol.

input: I'm a massage therapist. Build me a booking module that shows my available times, services, rates, and location.
hint: Booking module. contentType=booking, structure=steps, boundary=ask. Service-exposed. Wizard flow: select service → pick time → confirm → pay. Use controller protocol.

input: Link my booking module to my payment module so clients pay when they book, and connect to my Bluesky so confirmed clients can leave reviews.
hint: Multi-module composition. Booking (S5) links to Payment (S2) and Social (S3). PM wires modules via shared contentType references. Use controller protocol.

input: I'm reading 'The Wandering Inn' on Royal Road. Track my progress and notify me when new chapters drop.
hint: Reader module. contentType=fiction, structure=steps, boundary=none. Tracks chapter progression. Agent monitors RSS/A2A for updates. Use controller protocol.

input: I'm a web fiction author. Build me a publishing module where I write chapters, schedule releases, and distribute to subscribers who pay.
hint: Publisher module. contentType=fiction, structure=steps, boundary=paid. Service-exposed. Draft→edit→schedule→publish→notify flow. A2A notifications on publish. Use controller protocol.

input: Take my latest chapter and generate an audiobook version using my preferred voice.
hint: TTS module. contentType=audio, structure=object, boundary=none. Agent invokes TTS engine with voice preferences. Use controller protocol.

input: Distribute the audiobook to my paid subscribers and notify them the new chapter is available.
hint: Audio distribution. contentType=audio, structure=collection, boundary=paid. Service-exposed. A2A notifications to subscriber nodes. Payment via x402. Use controller protocol.

input: Connect my WhatsApp to my agent. I want to see and reply to messages from my node instead of opening the app.
hint: Bridge module. contentType=social-bridge, boundary=none. Internal-only despite session mechanics. Bridge translates platform protocol to internal events. PM routes everything. Use controller protocol.

input: I use Telegram for my hobby group and WhatsApp for family. Show me both in one inbox but keep them visually separated.
hint: Cross-bridge composition. Unified inbox composing two bridge modules. boundary=none (aggregator of internal bridges). Visual separation via tabs or sections. Use controller protocol.

input: I want to post the same update to Bluesky, Telegram, and X simultaneously from one compose view.
hint: Outbound fan-out. PM routes to one native A2A module (Bluesky) and two bridges (Telegram, X). Model must handle protocol difference. Use controller protocol.

input: When someone messages me on X about booking a session, my agent should automatically route that to my booking module.
hint: Bridge-to-module routing. X bridge (S3) → PM intent classification → booking module (S5). PM reads inbound, classifies intent, routes. Use controller protocol.

input: If someone sends me a WhatsApp message while I'm in a focus session, hold it and show me a summary when the session ends.
hint: Behavioral constraint on bridge. bThread blocks message rendering during focus, accumulates, summarizes on release. The bridge does NOT make this decision — PM behavioral engine does. Use controller protocol.

input: I've been using my recipe collection privately. Now I want to share new recipes automatically to my Telegram cooking group whenever I add one.
hint: TRICKY: User says 'share' but correct behavior is NOT boundary change. Recipe stays boundary=none (internal-only). PM adds behavioral trigger routing recipe-added events through Telegram bridge. No Agent Card metadata. Use controller protocol.

Shareware fee is $10, but if you send it in, I'll add a feature to the stack for you. BBS support on The Fringe at (713) 996-6866.

It tells you how you can get the whole project from the author of IF MONKS HAD MACS.... who will be the guest in the hypertext conference on 1/22/93. autumn luscious night illuminates hidden universe solitude This is a HyperCard 1.2 stack-- It is fully functional under HyperCard 2.x AFTER you choose 'CONVERT...' from the HyperCard FILE menu. See also my JFK assassination demo file 'JFK.sit' and 'Thomas/Nast.sit' the 'insights into New Media' file

_source.title: LiveCode (PowerPC)
_source.creator: LiveCode
_source.description:
LiveCode is a cross platform IDE that has been around since 2001. It has been described as the "modern day HyperCard" as it was inspired by it and is the only HyperCard clone that runs on all major OS'es. Initially through MetaCard engine, a HyperCard commercial clone, LiveCode then evolved to include its own programming language named "LiveCode Builder" in 2015. LiveCode is "Code it once, deploy it to billions of devices" as it supports all major OS'es such as iOS (since 2010), Android, Mac OS X (and macOS), Windows and Linux and HTML5. Older releases even supported compiling for Mac OS 9.

 This page hosts the last PowerPC releases. For more modern (Intel) releases of LiveCode see https://downloads.livecode.com/livecode/

 For source code releases, see https://github.com/livecode which was proudly announced on LiveCode.org with the very first sentence of the site stating "We believe in Open Source software." but on september 1st, 2021 , LiveCode CEO Kevin Miller announced that LiveCode was no longer open source:

 
 After 8 years with an open source offering we have come to the conclusion this is not the way forward for LiveCode. Whilst we are hugely grateful for the contributions we have received from the community during that period, it remains the case that 99% of the work on LiveCode is done by our in-house team. With a large section of the LiveCode community using the free open source edition, the cost of maintaining this vs the reward for doing so is not a balanced equation. It is a huge task which we are under-resourced to undertake. We have therefore taken the decision at this time to discontinue support for the open source editions of LiveCode. You can of course continue to use existing open source editions, and the code remains visible on github for viewing. The project will no longer accept contributions and we will not be maintaining it or offering open source downloads on our site.

 -- LiveCode CEO Kevin Miller

_source.title: Hacking Physics
_source.creator: Bill Wallace
_source.description: 4 Sep 1995 POB 5684 (Charleston Marina) Charleston, Or,97420 e-mail freewillee@aol.com or freewill@harborside.com The explicit thesis is that the theory of special relativity is difficult to understand because it is incorrect. The reason it is incorrect is that light (EM) propagates as an energy wave through a medium of aether particles rather than as a ray of photon particles from a source to detection and destruction in a human eye. My difference with special relativity might be semantic but I think it is more profound (if force acts along a straight path through curved space-time or along a curved path through linear space). My interpretation of the inverse square law is that it involves mutual interaction between the two charges ( F = k q1/r q2/r ). At any point in space between the charges, the force from each charge is on a sphere centered where each was when the sphere was generated. The point on the path for the force energy between the charges is where the spheres are tangent. At this instant, the path through space, for all the tangent sphere intersections that were or that will be, is not a straight line through space. Whether space-time is curved or the energy path is curved or whether the mass is increased or the increased path length results in a force that is less effective than expected might just be semantics but should be considered for discussion (i.e., if F=ma or a=F/m then an increase in m or a decrease in F will result in a reduction in a). Perhaps the supporters and the opponents of special relativity are saying the same thing using words having different meanings. For the deflection of a wave from a moving inclined mirror, the reflection is not instantaneous. The plane of the wavefront intersects the plane of the mirror in a line. As the wave progresses, the mirror is moving and the line of intersection moves along the mirror surface. The path of the beam makes a grazing angle with the path through space made by the intersection line of wavefront and mirror (grazing angle is the complement of the incident angle and I used it because the math and the drawing were easier). The paths for a ray and for a point on a wavefront differ after reflection from a moving inclined plane; therefore, the wave-particle duality principle of deBroglie is not correct (i.e., the deflection angle for a wave and for a particle differ when reflected from a moving oblique plane and there can be only one unique path) for this special case. This is the special case for the interferometer used to derive the foundation of special relativity. The result is that there is no phase shift to be predicted in hindsight for the interference pattern.

_source.title: MessageOnHold
_source.creator: Bill Leddy
_source.description: MessageOnHold is designed to play music and/or messages to telephone callers who you put on hold. You need to have a way to get the music from your Mac's sound port to your phone system. For some systems, you simply make up a cord from an earphone cord and plug it into your phone system (and Mac). Some phone systems require some programing so that they will play the music. Once connected, MessageOnHold plays the sounds in the 'Music' and 'Messages' lists. It normally alternates between the two so that callers hear a message (such as 'Thank you for holding' then a music piece, then a message and so on forever. I haven't tested this stack with a lot of other programs or tried it on different Mac models. I anticipate that it will be used on a dedicated (Semi-Retired) Mac with a minimum of extensions such as the old SE/30 I'm using. I'm running HC 2.3 but I think it will run under 2.2. Staff note: This stack was not tested for functionality with a telephone. This stack has been checked for Mac and HyperCard viruses with virex 5.6

_source.title: Stack Dumper 2.0
_source.creator: George Coulouris
_source.description: ----------------- Stack Dumper 2.0 ================ This stack enables you to: * extract the text in the background fields of any stack * extract the scripts of any stack and then format, edit and print the fields or the scripts using MSWord Version 4. Stack Dumper generates files that can be processed through the 'Print Merge' facility of MSWord to produce fully-formatted reports from HyperCard stacks. All of MSWord's formatting facilities can be applied to produce documents from stacks. The table construction facilities of Word 4 are especially useful when printing the contents of stacks that contain multi-field cards. Stack Dumper is Freeware. You are free to use it, but I retain the copyright. If you build the Stack Dumper scripts into any of your stacks, give me and my university an acknowledgement. If you want to use any of my code in a product, contact me for a licence. I hope you find 'Stack Dumper' useful. Please e-mail me if you like it. Let me know about any problems or improvements that you discover too. A primitive version of this stack, called Stack Dumper 1.0 was released in September 1989. That version could only dump background fields, not scripts, and you were required to copy its scripts into the target stack before you could use the dumping facilities. George Coulouris | Computer Science Dept ARPA/Internet: george@cs.qmc.ac.uk | Queen Mary and Westfield College JANET: george@uk.ac.qmc.cs | Mile End Road | London E1 4NS England Office phone: 44 1 975 5201 (direct line) Home phone: 44 1 485 5896 -------------------------------------------------------------

_source.title: ETD: Eudora To Database 1.2
_source.creator: Doug Jacobson
_source.description: Isn't the Internet wonderful? Especially electronic mail. E-mail lets folks from the world over communicate. But what are you going to do with all of those messages? I prefer Eudora® from Qualcomm as my e-mail client. And Eudora provides a way to catalog the messages. But I wanted a way to store the messages in a database, so I could use the power of the database on my e-mail messages. That's why I built ETD™...Eudora® To Database. ETD™ processes Eudora messages into a single tab- delimited field, return-delimited record file, ready for importing into a spreadsheet or database.

_source.title: MacElements Utilities v1.03
_source.creator: Dr. Mark Winter
_source.description: TYPE: Freeware Stack SYSTEM 7: Compatible MacElements Utilities is a HyperCard 2.x stack which calculates: element percentages isotope patterns yields of chemical reactions for given chemical formulae. It should therefore be useful in any chemical laboratory. All the user has to do is enter the formulae and weights (or number of mmols) of starting material and product. Formulae are correctly parsed and displayed with numeric subscripts (not common in HyperCard). The yield is calculated automatically. Additionally, element percentages for the chemical formulae are automatically calculated and displayed. A short report is automatically generated in Royal Society of Chemistry or American Chemical Society experimental section punctuation styles. The user may also elect to calculate the isotope pattern of chemical formulae. Some 50 different pseudo- elements such as Me, Ph, Cp, etc. are allowed in chemical formulae. Note: This stack is a small component of the MacElements package, a soon-to-be-published (really !) HyperCard based periodic table database and utilities package with the ability to export data, to plot graphs of properties of the elements, and lots of other things. It also does reaction yields and element percentages, of course! Details available from the author (preferably by e-mail please).

_source.title: Auto database/columning v. 1....
_source.creator: John Snider
_source.description: INSTANT DATABASE! JUST ADD TITLES! MAKE AS MANY AS YOU WANT AT ANYTIME! ALL VIEWABLE IN COLUMN FORMAT IN SINGLE VIEWER! ALSO VIEWABLE EACH CUSTOM DATAMAKER! (An expanded copy of this description is in the 'manual') Although its copyrighted it may be distributed by AOL and other similar services, organizations such as BMUG and non-commercial BBS units. It may be not sold by companies packaging shareware products and selling them for profitx (This is version 1.9, although there has been no previous version released) ACDB; an acronym for 'Automatic Columning (and) DataBasing'. Two stacks are supplied; one, an automatic databasing device creates a database input stack, formats the datafiles with justification, 'field' length and automatic provision for multiple line entries. ALL databases are viewable in columnar format in 'UniView' a universal viewer. Other datafiles created in other applications are also viewable; each created database is also viewable as single entry in the original creation stack. When one is called to 'UniView', intelligent agenting handles ALL formatting. Adjustments to the viewing format are simple and fast; if the alignment for column 3 should be changed to 'right' and you want to see more of and need need an 18 character width, its easily done with a click and drag/replace or backspace and type. When finish with this mode, the original file (in its entirety) is updated instantly. UniView resets showing the changes. UniView handles larger files automatically paging them to handle the 30,000 character field limit. Paging status is on screen. UniView reads and translates (some) files created from applications producing text files. UniView translates & creates generic intelligent agenting in the files. Runs in HC 2.x. This version runs in both 2.1 & 2.2. Upload was checke with Disinfectant 3.3 This is Shareware. Shareware participants get a nice bonus discussed in the manual. Be sure to read the full description in the manual. John Snider America Online: REMCBIZ AppleLink: MORGAN.REMC SnailMail: John Snider 1009 East Columbus, Suite B Martinsville IN 46151-1707 Note that due to a physical move, we are not able to get online as often as we like. The current situation is we check online about once or twice a week. This should change by the end of summer,1994x Snail mail would be answered by a return snail.

_source.title: Achievement of Arms(TM) v.1.0
_source.creator: Steven R. Solomon
_source.description: Achievement of Arms(TM) 1.0 (Achievement of Arms(TM) and Achievement Bank(TM) stacks; Shields, Helms, Mantles, Crests, Supporters, Compartments, and Motto artwork; Tinctures and Controls PICT files) (C) 1997 Steven R. Solomon. All rights reserved. Researched, programmed, and illustrated by Steve Solomon. Based on an idea by George Goncalves. Shareware fee: $10 per copy/$80 site license (10 copies) $5 per copy for registered owners of any version of Heraldry Primer(TM). $40 per site license for registered owners of a Heraldry Primer(TM) site license. Send registration fees to Steven R. Solomon 404 Grove Acre Avenue Pacific Grove, CA 93950 Check or money order only. U.S. funds only. Comments, questions, bug reports to the above address or via e-mail: GrammarMan@aol.com Achievement of Arms(TM) takes up where Heraldry Primer(TM) leaves off. With Achievement of Arms(TM) you can design not only shields of arms but displays composed of shields, helms, mantlings, wreaths, crests, supporters, compartments, and mottos. Choose from 16 pregenerated shields or import your own designs from the included Shield Bank(TM) stack (part of Heraldry Primer(TM)). Also includes 3 helms, 4 styles of mantling, 3 different supporters, 3 compartments, 4 standard mottos (and the ability to write your own). You can use the Achievement Bank(TM) stack to save and print out the achievements you design. Requirements: HyperCard(R) 2.3 (or Player) and any Macintosh that will run it; a 14' monitor or larger; black and white printer required to print out shields. Color screen NOT required; achievements are displayed in black and white using a traditional system of crosshatch patterns (modified slightly for use on a computer screen). Note: Keep all the stacks, folders, and PICT files in the Achievement of Arms(TM) folder so that HyperCard is able to find everything it needs. Heraldry Primer(TM) Standalone Owners: Do not try to use the Heraldry Primer(TM) Standalone or the associated version of Shield Bank(TM) with Achievement of Arms(TM). Stacks designed to run under a standalone application may not run properly under HyperCard(R), and could cause crashes or even damage to your software. To be safe, use only the stack version of Heraldry Primer(TM) and the stacks that come with it. Future versions of Achievement of Arms(TM) or MacHeraldry(TM) may be released as standalones. Version History: 1.0 First release version of Achievement of Arms(TM). _source.title: Audio Sequencer!
_source.creator: Scott Adams
_source.description: Audio Sequencer is a HyperCard stack created as a poor-man's sequencer. With no interface with MIDI or anything else for that matter, it's accuracy is partially dependent on your reflexes. Designed to play short sounds, such as sound effects (gunshots, etc.), it functions very well as a low end sequencer. Okay, maybe it's not really a sequencer, but it does have the ability to play a sound (or a series of sounds) at the exact moment you want it to. Designed with the synching to video in mind, the standard time unit is one frame of video (1/30th of a second). Each card has a time ruler of two seconds, with sixty units on the ruler. A sound can be played on any one of those units. Concievably, you could play a different sound on every frame (who knows what it would sound like though!) You can have as many cards as you want, each representing two seconds. Or you can have one card represent several seconds. You can have as many sounds play over as long of time as you want (memory permitting). Actually, the time limit is 59:59:29, being 59 minutes, 59 seconds, and 29 frames. This hasn't been tested to work though (who has the time?) I've even surprised myself on this stack, it works really well and doesn't cost much (an e-mail registers you)! So why not give it a try all you home video and audio buffs, this may be just what you need to give some life to your masterpieces!

_source.title: Growth Records.sit
_source.creator: Steven Seelig M.D., Ph.D.
_source.description: This is my first HC stack. One of the most difficult problems for physicians who deal with children is evaluation of their growth pattern and to determine whether that growth pattern is normal or abnormal. Since approximately five percent of all children are either too small or too tall, abnormalities in growth are relatively common and yet are all to frequently not recognized for several years. This HC stack permits the rapid plotting and recognition of abnormal growth patterns. This HC stack is free for the casual user...like those of you who want to know whether your kid is growing ok. Please feel free to distribute this software to your friends; however, if you use it for professional use, please request a copy from the author. I have not included a number of functions which the professional might find useful. If you are interested in seeing a more complete stack or if you would like some special implementation established, contact me by E-Mail. Steven Seelig M.D., Ph.D.

_source.description: This is a Hypercard stack that includes all of the FCC questions for the Technician Ham Radio (Amateur Radio) License. The Technician Amateur Radio Class License DOES not require that you know Morse Code! Many people refer to this new license calss as the no-code Tech license. On the Amatuer Radio frequencies the Tech license holder can operate on include computer-to-computer communications called Packet Radio as well a many other digital modes. If you have always wanted to see how easy it is to get into Ham Radio... checkout this stack. Has great special effects... applause if you get the question right.... boo's if you miss one! This is one of a series of stacks that cover all of the test for the various license classes: Novice, Technician, General, Advanced and Amateur Extra Class. These all can be found in the Ham Radio Club libraries here on AOL (keyword: ham radio). For more information, e-mail to TSTADER or stop by the Ham Radio Club conferences every Sunday or Wednesday nights at 1-0 PM East Coast time in our Conference Room.

_source.title: Broker's Bailiwick 1.04Demo
_source.creator: Quintessential Software
_source.description: We at QuintesSoft Custom Programming (Quintessential Software) want to invite you to discover our latest service to professional money managers anxious to offer maximum professional services with their Macs. Ignore the older Broker's Bailiwick demos in the business folder of MHC or on MBS) In simple terms, Broker's Bailiwick allows efficient day-to-day navigation through client portfolios and quick performance appraisal by seven investment sub-categories: stocks, bonds, funds, direct investments, cd's, options, and any other one category of your choice. Liberal on-screen support is provided with pop-up help fields to clarify the function and use of most buttons and fields. Among its many features, Broker's Bailiwick computes a client's true annualized rate of return, given both starting and ending dates and amounts along with the entry of all deposit and withdrawal dates and amounts. It summarizes multiple trades for each investment by dividing the sum of realized gains (or loss) by the remaining units of that investment. It concurrently requests current price(s), then computes net unrealized gain (with or without commissions) for any one investment, or any category of investments. As a user, you may merge personalized letters with addresses and salutations for mailings to selected clients, generate mailing labels, do full-feature prospect management, auto-dialing, and move prospects to active account status with assignment of an account number and a mouse click. You are also able 'sift' and sort accounts with easily-revised account marking or sorting routines (based on investment interests, funds coming due, name, city, zip, any key word or phrase). Any account or sub-account is easily converted into another sub-accounts with 4 mouse-clicks and the entry of the new sub-account number, with no re-entry of client data. Whether dealing with a prospect or current client, Each account involves over 30 buttons and 100 fields which include 32 expandable client-data fields, most of which are selectively exposed at the user's discretion. Any account can be effortlessly 'profiled' to expose only relevant fields of interest, such as funds coming due, comments, last call, last mailing, objectives, stocks to monitor, life style, topics of interest, contacts, and alternate phone numbers. Broker's Bailiwick was created under contract with the highest producing broker in his city, working for a major brokerage firm (PaineWebber). He personally solicited our company, QuintesSoft, to create something for the Mac that he was unable to find -- a program that fulfilled his concepts of astute service to his clients. It seemed such a good idea that we stipulated we would retain ownership and put much more effort into program architecture and design from the start, in anticipation of eventual broad commercial release to broker's, money managers, institutional investors, and serious private investors managing multiple accounts. Very competitive bundling is available with Apple or Outbound products, including Powerbooks and Notebooks. Additional customization and trades data file downloading is also available from QuintesSoft Custom Programming 509-779-4488, makers of QUINTESsential SOFTware for the Macintosh.
_source.modernAnalog: A sovereign creative app that helps me make and revisit my own work evaluation work across devices.

input: Build me a creative tool that because buttons in hpyercard are rectangular, it can be hard to use them to cover complex graphic objects with precision. when an object is irregular, slanted, elliptical, or a complex polygon, the. _source.title: Tricks of the HyperTalk Masters: Polybuttons
_source.creator: unknown
_source.description: Because buttons in Hpyercard are rectangular, it can be hard to use them to cover complex graphic objects with precision. When an object is irregular, slanted, elliptical, or a complex polygon, the only way to simulate a single button over it is to create a series of rectangular buttons and lay them down on the surface like tiles. You must also put the same handler in each button so that pressing each button does what you want. "Polybuttons" allows you to create buttons with multiple sides (polygons) using a special button-maker drawing tool that works like the polygon tool in Hypercard's paint layer. This stack contains the HyperTalk script for "Polybuttons" in its stack script. The script allows for the creation of free-form buttons by creating transparent rectangular buttons around an irregular shape. The useful part of the script is that it will check for overlapping buttons, thus allowing many buttons of various shapes to be in close proximity without danger of their messages being confused.

_source.title: MusiCalc 4.4
_source.creator: Michael Riesman
_source.description: MusiCalc is a multi-functional HyperCard-based music and time calculator which will add, subtract, multiply, and divide time on a scale of minutes and seconds. Entries are free-form and may be corrected, annotated, copied, cut, pasted, and recalculated. MusiCalc consists of 4 calculator elements: 1) MusiCalc, a calculator for musical time relationships (calculates time from bars & tempo, bars from time & tempo, and tempo from bars & time, and converts beats-per-minute to frames-per-beat & vice-versa); 2) TimeCalc, a time calculator which properly adds, subtracts, multiplies and divides minutes, seconds, frames, and bits (with optional entry of tenths, hundredths, etc. in place of, or combined with, these subdivisions); 3) BarCalc, a formula evaluator which simplifies computing the length of a piece of music, particularly music with changing meters and/or repeating sections whose measure-number count does not correspond to elapsed beats (BarCalc can be used for any kind of multi-part formula evaluation); and 4) SMPTECalc, which calculates sum and difference times using a stored SMPTE timecode number and MusiCalc or TimecCalc results, facilitating re-synchronization.

_source.title: Patent Copyright Trademark Pr...
_source.creator: Prof. Thomas G. Field, Jr.
_source.description: The stacks were written by Professor Thomas G. Field, Jr., Director of the Innovation Clinic of Franklin Pierce Law Center (Concord NH) based on almost 20 years of experience in answering questions from people described above. Well over 80,000 copies of these stacks have been distributed in booklet form by the Law Center and the U.S. Small Business Administration. Stack descriptions: (1) Information is a general index to the remaining stacks. (2) So You Have an Idea is a basic discussion of the relationship between patents and the market value of inventions. Discusses the need for counsel in making outside submissions, the need for prior art searches, invention promoters and other matters of special interest to independent inventors - particularly from a cost standpoint. (3) Seeking Cost-Effective Patents is a detailed discussion of ways to evaluate intellectual property options in view of the nature of an invention and its market value. Contains specific strategies for controlling ever-increasing patent costs in the face of market uncertainty. (4) Trademarks and Business Goodwill is a basic discussion of strong versus weak marks, the need for searches, the value of state and federal registrations and other matters of importance to owners of small businesses. (5) Avoiding Patent, Trademark and Copyright Problems is written primarily for owners of small businesses. (6) Copyright in Visual Arts and (7) Copyright for Computer Authors address the importance of copyright registration and notice, works for hire, deposit requirements, registration of multiple works, the need for counsel in licensing and other matters of interest to free lance artists, computer authors, craftspeople and small entrepreneurs.

_source.description: Welcome to the standalone Sun and Moon 3.0 Demo, which develops association and visual discrimination skills. Children find two objects that go together in the first activity. They point to the picture of the bird and the nest, for instance, rather than the car or the planet. In an alternate activity, students see a scene in which there are objects that don't belong. If they pick out all of the objects, they receive a reward! Sun and Moon encourages critical thinking, while appealing to children with its colorful graphics and sound. This demo version is fully functional, with sounds and animation, but only contains a sampling of the association and discrimination activities. Look for the other two titles in the Early Learning Series here online: Alphabet Zoo and Number Aquarium.

_source.creator: Gary Stanoulis
_source.description: The XFCNs in this stack demonstrate some QuickDraw techniques using CompileIt! There are a bunch of radio buttons that allow you to select the penMode and penPat to be used in the demonstration. Mix and match until you get the desired effect. The XFCNs simply draw a vertical or horizontal line of the length specified with the width, mode and pattern chosen. The line is dragged horizontally or vertically tracking to the moues. The demonstration is set up in such a manner as to emulate split bars for hyperCard fields. I specifically set up this stack to allow me to try differenct quick draw combinations on different Macs as I was experiencing results different on PowerBooks than on CRT based Macs. Gary Stanoulis CompileIt! SIG Leader

_source.title: PowerPalettes 1.2 - NEW FEATU...
_source.creator: Cheshire Grin Productions Ltd.
_source.description: LIBRARY: MHC:Utilities PowerPalettes are six powerful color palettes for HyperCard users and developers. This full-blown copy (set to 'Demo' mode) includes complete documentation. Version 1.2 includes three new features. Each PowerPalette enhances specific HyperCard functions. For example, the Properties palette accelerates screen design and working with fields and buttons. Its moving, alignment and spacing tools bring MacDraw type functionality to HyperCard. Interactively change any property of grouped fields and/or buttons without wading through HyperCard dialog boxes. The Pictures palette makes it easy for anyone to use PICT resources within their stacks. Open PICT images from any available stack, and modify their style, magnification, rect, and location. Move a PICT window around the screen with a standard click-and-drag motion. Click the Script button to automatically generate all the script commands required to redisplay it. Other PowerPalettes include Messages, Sorter, Replacer and Caser. All provide valuable new capabilities for advanced or casual HyperCard users. To unlock your copy of PowerPalettes call 1-800-668-3873 (TOLL-FREE!). Provide the Registration Key for your Macintosh (it's in the PowerPalettes stack) and a VISA or MasterCard number. A License Key will be faxed or phoned to you within 24 hours. Your cost is just $89 US ($99 Cdn. plus applicable taxes) If you prefer to have a free copy of PowerPalettes mailed to you EMail your address to CHESHIR. It's FREE!

_source.creator: Scott Adams
_source.description: This stack lets you enter a story/conversation/whatever and then search for a specific phrase from that. I originally made this stack to edit an audio recording of someone as a joke. I first entered what the original recording said, then let the stack do its work, typed in the phrase I wanted it to say at the end, then made the stack work agian, then all of a sudden-I had it right in front of me! It's not really to fun, but it can be usefull, and the end result fun. The stack is mostly self-explanitory and mostly uses its own menu for all the functions that include command-key shortcuts. If you have any questions/comments, please E-mail me (ScottA) and I'll answer you. P.S. This stack also includes a sound used in the opening titles of The Hunt for Red October, it's pretty cool!

On the Set List card, each song is in an individual field which can be moved with a click and snapped into a numbered grid. _source.title: 3D Parametric Equations
_source.creator: Alexander Thomas
_source.description: This HyperCard stack draws isometric projections of three-dimensional curves and surfaces (wire-frame). Curves are described with 3 equations in one parameter, and surfaces with 3 equations in two parameters. The curves and surfaces can be exported to coordinate files for the freeWare program "Rotater" ( http://raru.adelaide.edu.au/rotater/ ). There are various export options, like choosing between the 7 available colors, exporting dots or lines and varying the color according to the z-value of each coordinate. The number of digits after the decimal point can be chosen between 0 and 20.

_source.title: Munchkin Matters 1.0
_source.creator: PVeclipse
_source.description: Munchkin Matters 1.0 This stack is directed primarily for children between 3 and 8 years of age. It is intended to help children learn the basic alphabet and begin putting together their own words. There are 8 distinctive areas included, of which most are learning areas, and some are just for fun. Picture and word association play a major role in the basic format of the stack. Speech has been installed throughout to say the letters, put words to pictures and allow kids to build their own words and then have the computer speak that word for them. Picture-word match game that rewards higher levels of achievement and attempts to help when the learning level needs improvement. Game and fun areas are in place to help with computer and mouse skills as well as keeping things interesting. Paint is included that is quite fun and will keep even the adults sitting at the Mac for hours. A two player game for developing thought and strategy, which includes spoken help and spoken prompts to get them started. An area for saving their art produced in paint, which can be used in a slide show type of format. Auto painted graphics for the smallest kids that might not be ready to paint on their own. It's really pretty cool and I'll bet you adults will click these buttons a few times. Useful at schools, day care and home. ShareWare. Scanned with Disinfectant for any known viruses. Well worth download time - take a look - I think you'll like it. (Staff note: This stack requires Macintalk in your system folder. It is incompatible with 32-bit operation.)

_source.title: SODAFlipBook v0.9d3
_source.creator: RUNFAST, INC
_source.description: SODAFlipBook c1993 RUNFAST, Inc version 0.9d3 SHAREWARE: $8.50 Soda FlipBook is a stack-making utility for HyperCard users. It will hopefully speed up the process of creating stacks, by providing a standard background with separate windows for text and graphics. It has been used extensively in education, ranging from 7th grade thru Grad-school. Students would typically create multimedia notebooks with text appearing on the left and illustrations on the right. It has been used in all content areas, from Science and Mathematics to Art and Literature. It has also been used by educators in writing workshops. In adition to its educational applications, SODA FlipBook has enjoyed the experimentation of creative artists. SODA Flip has several paint palettes for making stack design quicker. Including a 256-color palette for custom color selection. FlipBook Color is a special edition of the SODAFlipBook. It contains a sample palette from SODAColor, the scriptless multimedia solution from Runfast, Inc. FlipBook Color WILL run just as well on black&white Macs. Just click the COLOR-button on the title card to enter Black&White mode. SODAColor has been used in education (middle school thru graduate-level) and national workshops in developing multimedia notebooks and presentations, and is now available to the general public. SODAColor makes use of several floating palettes to facilitate stack creation and customization. It supports Color, QuickTime, Sound Record and Import from other stacks, Pict-files, and allows you to write HyperCard songs. In keeping with Runfast, Inc's commitment to taking advantage of the latest advancements in technology, SODAColor is in a continuous state of development. If you purchase SODAColor now, you are guaranteed 2 major upgrades free, with discounts on future releases. SODAColor comes as either a compiled application, or a stack, allowing you the flexibility of creating your own custom Apps, or the ability to add SODA resources to your own HyperCard stacks. Pricing is as follows: SODAColor Application: $35.00 SODAColor Stack: $35.00 SODA FlipBook*: $8.50 Thanks for your interest! Gregor Patterson Vice President, Development RUNFAST, INC PO BOX 11503 COLUMBIA, SC 29211 America Online: RunfastInc INTERNET: RunfastInc@AOL.Com *FREE with purchase of SODAColor

_source.title: Xenophilia
_source.creator: John T. Unger
_source.description: Xenophiles do not seek enlightenment as it is usually conceived. There is no Nirvana to which we attain. Rather, we seek an enlightenment of this, the sensual world, to be used here and now in the present, in acts of the flesh. In the expression of our feral glee we feast on raw, primal truths. We are monkey nerve junkies, ever alert for the mainline primacy of unfiltered experienceâa visceral knowing of the moment as absolute apotheosis, carnal crescendo, culmination of desire. The razor that opens our eyes to beauty.

_source.title: InfoWindow 1.1
_source.creator: The UFP Collaboration
_source.description: This stack holds all the scripts, resources and XCMDs to implement a button "inspector" window. This is the kind of window most modern applications have these days, which displays all of an object's attributes. It uses the UlisEdit and the xDraw XCMDs to override HyperCard's button editor. You can select buttons, move them, resize them, and if you double-click on them they'll even bring up the inspector window letting you change their attrbutes.

_source.title: Life Expectancy Calculator 1.0
_source.creator: Jim Stephenson
_source.description: OK, I'll admit it...life expectancy sounds like a pretty dull subject. But Life Expectancy Calculator actually makes it fun! This entertaining and informative program will ask for your age, race and sex. Then, after a drum roll, it will display your life expectancy (based on government statistics). Next, it will draw a bar graph comparing your current age to your expected life span. Click a button and you'll hear harp music as an angel presents a humorous 'Countdown to Eternity' clock that counts down the time you have left! In addition, you can read a collection of humor and quotations about aging, including quotes from Jerry Seinfeld, George Burns, Bill Cosby and Cary Grant. Finally, you can learn how to 'beat the odds' and live a longer and healthier life. This program contains nice graphics and sound effects, and is free.

_source.description: Welcome to the standalone Alphabet Zoo 3.0 Demo, which teaches the formation and recognition of upper- and lower-case printed letters. Kevin Koala and other animals demonstrate how to draw the letters. Then, IntelliTrace-TM technology enables the child to use the mouse or TouchWindow-TM to trace a letter on screen and receive real-time visual feedback. A second activity promotes letter recognition by having the child move an animal to the letter corresponding to the name of the animal. This demo version is fully functional, with sounds and animation, but only contains the letters A through E. Look for the other two titles in the Early Learning Series here online: Number Aquarium and Sun and Moon.

_source.title: DragWindow
_source.creator: Gary Stanoulis
_source.description: I wrote this WIndowDrag XCMD as an exercise in really simple CompileIt! coding. No parameters are passed to this XCMD. Simply include it anywhere in a mouseDown handler and the mouse will drag a gray outline of the window around your monitor(s) and redraw it at the correct location on mouseUp It uses the Mac DragWindow toolbox call to work so it works well across multiple monitors. Works with HyperCard, SuperCard or WindowScript. You can design your own 'fake' title bar and use this instead of the ones the named products usually supply. Use 'hide titlebar' to hide HyperCard's title bar. Only the version of this stack uploaded to the CompileIt! SIG Library contains the CompileIt! code for this XCMD. Have fun! Gary Stanoulis CompileIt! SIG Leader
_source.modernAnalog: A focused maker utility that helps me automate or inspect one technical task without needing a full desktop toolkit.

_source.title: Down, A Novel
_source.creator: Matthew Spong
_source.description: DOWN is fantasy/magic realism. The story opens in the ancient city of Levinfield, home of a civilization so old that their libraries collapse beneath the weight of their books, where the monetary system is based on poetry and . History follows a regular cycle, from industrial peak to decadent trough and back again, and the city is now in the middle of a quiet era of contented peace. Few people leave the city, as all their needs are catered for by the leftovers from the last industrial age, the automated Factories which can manufacture anything required and the antique mines where fossilized machines are dug from beneath the ruins. Lang, however, has been reading. In the libraries he uncovers references to a great forest to the West, and a desire stirs in him to explore, leave the safe streets and venture out into the deserted countryside. One legend in particular catches his interest. In the last days of the previous Industrial Age a truckdriver disappeared while passing through the forest on his way to the outlying villas of the rich Factory-owners. Since then his ghost was said to haunt the road, ceaselessly searching for a way out, a pale wraith in white tee-shirt and navy shorts, wandering through the trees crying "Map! Maaap!", searching for directions. Lang vows to track the ghost down and lay it to rest. His friends try and prevent him leaving. Subconsciously they fear he is the first sign of the next aggressive wave of greed, desire and development. But he is determined. He readies his rusted trailbike, acquires a longbow for protection, and sets out. Deep in the heart of the forest, in a castle that for millennia lay buried beneath the mold, Catherine toils obsessively at her life's work. She is a descendant of the Factory-owners of old, run-away from her parents crumbling villa to the West of the forest. She records on tape the words spoken by parrots that nest in the trees - words of a forgotten language of great power. Passed down through generations of birds, this language, (when the words are spoken in the right order), can harness strong magical energy. Catherine records and catalogues and files the words of power and never really knows why. As Lang unknowingly approaches her castle he encounters strange things. By night he hears the ghostly cry, "Maap!", and sees a white shape moving through the fog, until one night when the air is clear and he sees the ghost for what it really is - an albino deer, it's coat sparkling with phosphorescent spores, that bleats the mournful cry as it grazes. He decides to hunt it down and carry the skin back to the city to dispel the myth. Catherine, her senses amplified by magic, senses the deer's death. She knew it well, it was almost her pet. In her anger she decides to punish Lang in the worst way possible. She will create a void, a bottomless hole, an empty universe, and cast him in. For eternity he will float through space, alive, aware but unable to escape, lost in nothingness. As Lang casually heads back to Levinfield, the white pelt lashed across his pack, she works to prepare the spell, cutting and splicing tape in a mad frenzy. The trap works. One moment Lang is walking through the forest, the next he finds himself suspended in the highest room in the castle, an inky black well of nothing beneath his feet, Catherine standing before him triumphant. And then...? Read on, to find out how they both come to be trapped in the void, how together they escape from the impossible, how they come to find themselves in inner-city Sydney, in a world they have created from their dreams, a world corrupted by their antagonism and twisted by their desperation, a world which, finally, they have to decide either to allow to continue or to destroy utterly.

input: Build me a creative tool that an xcmd to show pict files or resources in hypercard's window. as well as a bonus set of scripts to make pseudo picture objects. I want to track my progress. Keep it private but let me share specific items when someone asks.
hint: Generate a Plaited-native creative-tool using MSS tags contentType=art, structure=form, scale=S2. Expected exposure: artifact-shareable. Use controller protocol, not generic web-app framing.

_source.title: Color Pictures
_source.creator: Jerome Jahnke -- JerSquard
_source.description: An XCMD to show PICT files or resources in HyperCard's window. As well as a bonus set of scripts to make pseudo picture objects. Way back in 1989 I had a color macintosh and HyperCard. No one was doing color for HyperCard so I took it apon myself to put something together. At about the same time Stan Gilbert came out with TKShowPict. His method was quite a bit better than my own so I stole it. What made his idea so important was NOT how to get at picture on the screen. That part was easy, but how to tell if it had been erased. While I will cover this in the background scripts and the script of the card button on the next card I did want to make sure credit was given where credit was due. Explore the scripts to get a good feel on how to fool folks into thinking HC really does color. Why not use his XCMD? Well Stan Gilberts was what I needed initally it had a two flaws: 1) I could not define a rectangle in which to place the picture. 2) I could not read in a PICT file. I needed to do both. So in the great tradition of necessity birthing invention. I took my ideas and merged them with his idea and came up with my own XCMD. Synatax ShowPicture [/], [/,[/],[Use Picture Rect ] Looks like ShowPicture 'Logo','0,0' Draw the PICT resource at card location 0,0. ShowPicture 'Bogus HD:Some Folder:My Picture', rect of cd btn 3, file Draw PICT file 'My Picture' at 'Bogus HD:Some Folder' into the rect of cd btn 3. ShowPicture 'Logo', rect of btn 2, True Draws PICT resource Logo at the upper right hand corner of btn 2 and returns the PICT size in the result. ShowPicture 'MyHD:Logo', rect of bg btn 2, File, True and ShowPicture 'MyHD:Logo', rect of bg btn 2, True, File Draws PICT File Logo at the upper right hand corner of btn 2 and returns the PICT size in the result. Software and Hardware Reqirements HyperCard version 2.0 and system 4.0 or greater. If there is great enough outcry I can re-rev it so that it will work with HC 1.2.x. Justification The ShowPicture XCMD places a great deal of the burden on HyperTalk programmer to 'know' the state of the HC window at any given time. Is this fair? I am not sure. It works fine for me and a few other people I know who have used this XCMD. It is very fast, the code is only 2000 bytes. Most of the code is used for parsing the XCMD arguments (and as such most is skipped). The only other way to keep track of when HC redraws it's window is to patch a trap or two. This has been done by the BungDabba folks. And if you want to worry less about when HC redraws over your color pictures their product, which is free, may interest you. If you want blazing speed this will do it for you. Even though it will look kinda tacky sometimes.

_source.title: Scavenger Hunt
_source.creator: ImagiNation Enterprises
_source.description: WELCOME TO SCAVENGER HUNTÂ©! This software was created with children in mind. The program is specifically created for kindergarten through second graders; those who are working on reading skills. It not only offers reading and matching exercises throughout the basic game, but each room of the house has a "hidden" activity that helps underline reading disciplines: â¢ in one room, children play "dress up" and learn clothing names â¢ in another room, children follow a recipe to create a new treat â¢ another room offers a book that keeps stealing letters from a story â¢ in other rooms, there is opportunity to match tools, fold laundry, and try out interesting pets â¢ and other rooms offer fun sounds, games and other spelling feats! Of course, we can't tell you where any of these rooms are, nor how to get into them, that's part of the fun! The basic "game" as you enter the house is to go from room to room using the "map" provided on the main page. Try to find objects that BEGIN with the letters on your "list" found with the map. You will only be able to take one object back with you from any room, and once you make a letter match, that room will be "locked." (This feature can be turned off if you wish, by clicking "off" the button "Use room covers" below the house map.) You have to find ONE object for each letter to complete the game, and there are literally HUNDREDS of objects all over the house!! If you wish to remove a word you found, click on that word in your list and it will be removed (and if you are playing with room covers, the room will be unlocked so you can go back and find other options.) You may want to "explore" before you begin, so you have a feel for where things are. The game will ONLY speak the first letters, not the entire word. This encourages children and adults to work together and "read" the game. There are fun side tracks all through the house. Check doors, cupboards and piles of things, you never know what you'll find! But most of all, have fun!! If your version is titled "MiniScavenger HuntÂ©", then your program is made to fit on the MacClassic screen (9"). The original "Scavenger HuntÂ©" was designed for the larger screen. If you have the wrong version, simply write or call ImagiNation Enterprises and we will gladly send you the other version. This program is SHAREWARE, so if you decide to keep it, please send the amount of $5.00 to ImagiNation Enterprises at the following address: ImagiNation Enterprises 463 N 800 E American Fork, Utah 84003-1919 (801) 763-0682 This program is actually a HyperCardâ¢ stack written in HyperCardâ¢ 2.2, and requires HyperCardâ¢ 2.0 or newer to run it. If you have any questions or suggestions, please send them to ImagiNation Enterprises! All rights reserved. Â©1996 by ImagiNation Enterprises

_source.title: Number Aquarium 3.0 Demo.sit
_source.creator: Jan K. France & Kevin Harris
_source.description: Welcome to the standalone Number Aquarium 3.0 Demo, a fun way to help youngsters learn the basics of writing and counting their numbers. Number Aquarium integrates sound and animation to teach number formation and recognition to early education students. In the first of two activities, animals of the sea show how to draw the numbers. For instance, Pamela Penguin demonstrates how to draw the number four. The child can then use the mouse or other pointing device (such as the TouchWindow-TM or certain adaptive devices) to draw the number, formed on screen by IntelliTrace-TM. The second activity emphasizes counting skills. Each animal presents a number of objects to be counted. As the child points to each object, Number Aquarium counts along with the child. This demo version is fully functional, with sounds and animation, but only contains the numbers 1 through 5. Look for the other two titles in the Early Learning Series here online: Alphabet Zoo and Sun and Moon.

_source.title: IconStack 1.7.6
_source.creator: Geoff Williams
_source.description: IconStack is the ultimate HyperCard B/W icon library by Geoff Williams, the author of other notable HyperCard stacks such as Bomb Hunter, Pig Professional, ArtBlox and the HyperMagic series (for magicians only). This handy HyperCard stack contains over 100 screens (cards) totaling MORE THAN 7000 icon-size black & white pictures (32 pixels by 32 pixels) of almost every conceivable form. Users can modify, copy and create their own icons for use in graphics programs, System 7 desktop icons, HyperCard buttons, ResEdit, etc. Icons can easily be added to this library. Very easy to use, IconStack icons can be accessed from the main Index Card or by searching for a user-definable keyword (many keywords may be associated with each card). To copy an icon, just click on it. A Paint Area is available for modifying/creating icons (modifying/creating icons on the main cards is discouraged to prevent mishaps to the other icons on the card). Centering Guides make exact placement of icons in the icon grid quick and simple. Keyboard shortcuts, for most operations, are shown onscreen.

_source.title: Chord/Scale/Note Conv.sit
_source.creator: Ken Blackman
_source.description: ----------------- 'By converting scales to notes to chords and back, You can: Discover what scales Mozart or Bartok liked to use. Learn to solo through tricky chord changes. Come up with the perfect chords for a melody you've written. I designed the Converter to help my music students learn by example how to translate between CHORD symbols (such as those found in fake books, lead sheets, etc.); SCALE types (as taught in traditional jazz theory); and real NOTES (such as those produced by real musical instruments or facsimiles thereof). You can also use it for more general exploration: How many dominant chords/scales are there? Are there any scales that have a major 7th, a perfect 5th, and a flat 13th? Is there even a single note that will work through a whole chorus of 'Giant Steps'? If not, then which note will get me the farthest? Why does tritone substitution sound so damn good? What will other substitutions sound like? And most importantly: What kind of chord is THAT? Etc. etc. etc.'
_source.modernAnalog: A sovereign creative app that helps me make and revisit my own art work across devices.

_source.title: Pathbased Animations
_source.creator: Matt Reagan
_source.description: Getting tired of having to write huge scripts to tell hypercard how to animate your buttons? Wish you could make fluid, flowing animations with your icons? Have you ever wanted the computer to write words on the screen, or paint a picture with a single line of code? If any of those are true, or if you're just looking for a fantastic utility, 'Dragger' is for you! Dragger is a simple, but effective utility for creating fields and fields of paths. Simply drag the mouse where you want the path. Dragger will automatically show you where your points are on your path. When you're done, release the mouse, and dragger will do all the calculations and give you two fields full of points and commands. If some of this isn't making sense, don't worry! Included in this stack are several demonstration buttons that will show you exactly how to use these paths to animate your button!. That's right, just point, click, drag, and you're done! Then you can go back into your stack, and have your buttons move quickly or slowly across the path you've dragged for them! Because Dragger has a constant rate of recording, you can control the speed of your animations for your buttons, by simply slowing down or speeding up your mouse! Download this fantastic utility now! ===================== Checked for Mac and HyperCard viruses with Virex 5.6

input: Build me a creative tool that i don't know david brown, but i thought i'd give him a hand as his original "animation. sit" stack had a couple of bugs in it. This is just for me — completely private.
hint: Generate a Plaited-native creative-tool using MSS tags contentType=art, structure=steps, scale=S2. Expected exposure: internal-only. Use controller protocol, not generic web-app framing.

_source.title: Animation Stack
_source.creator: William G. Hutchison
_source.description: I don't know David Brown, but I thought I'd give him a hand as his original "Animation.sit" stack had a couple of bugs in it. Like the fact that he didn't include any icons for the "Begin" button (the one with the "Stop" and "Go" icons which I added -- I apologize for the poor artwork) and the fact that the "Continuous" button didn't work due to the deletion of a "Wait 30" command. But all is restored and things now work wonderfully! Please support this stack with your shareware fee to David as it's (now) a pretty good stack. And, Dave, hope you don't mind that I altered the original stack. Of course, you get the credit not to mention additional shareware fees! --William G. Hutchison, M.D. Boston, MA November 22, 1990 To use the stack properly (Dave was a little nebulous on this), first click on the "Do It!" button to see what happens with the HyperTalk script I generated! Then to create your own animation, erase my picture using the eraser tool, copy a smallish picture from a Clip art stack or one of your choosing, and paste it into the rectangle (The location of the rectangle is really not essential, it just makes things neater for this stack. You could place the picture anywhere on the card, since the "Do It!" button calls for the lasso tool and the picture is on the "Card" level--it will get selected no matter where it is). Then click on the "Continuous" or "Point to point" button. Then click the "Go" sign and follow the directions in the field below the picture you pasted. You should see HyperTalk script being generated in the field on the right. The next step is to click on the "Set up" button which transfers the script into the "Do It!" button's script. Then click "Do It!" and watch your animated picture go wild. Oh yeah, you may notice that if you use a large picture such as the one I substituted, and if you specify a position too near the card's edges (the starting point being near the character's nose or near the middle of his head) that the stack draws lines instead of moving your picture around. This can be remedied by not going so near the edges with your clicking! To utilize the animation in stacks of your own, simply make sure the picture is in the same relative position in your stack as the one in this stack. Then copy the "Do It!" button into your stack. Modify the "Do It!" button to your liking so that the name doesn't show and/or create your own icon button using ResEdit (Open ResEdit, open the Animation stack, then create a new resource as "Icon" and not "Icon#", use the "Icon editor" to draw the icon picture to your liking, then copy it and paste it into the Animation stack ResEdit window, save the changes, quit ResEdit, open your own stack, create a new button and then double click on it, displaying the button's dialogue window, click on the "Icon" button, and choose the icon picture you created in ResEdit, then copy the script from the Animation stack's "Do It!" button into your new button and you're all set!! Good luck and have fun with this stack!
_source.modernAnalog: A sovereign creative app that helps me make and revisit my own art work across devices.

_source.title: Pi In The Sky
_source.creator: ArthurD12
_source.description: Pi In The Sky October 23, 1994 This freeware stack is an update of the earlier stack. It includes a Pi menu and Help screens that are keyed to the active card. The stack is not protected but the user level has been set to painting to prevent accidental damage. You can change the user level by means of the Protect Stack menu item. If you don't see the menu item in the HyperCard File menu then hold the command key while you mouse the File menu open. The stack script also includes several handlers that are used to dynamically modify the script under certain conditions. The stack can be used to begin to explore the development of the concept of pi. The stack gets its title from the book Mathematics For The Million by Lancelot Hogben. The title was created with Aldus Type Twister. Other sources are listed in the stack. The value of pi is approximately 3.14159. Archimedes calculated the value as more than 3 1/7th and less than 3 10/71s. Computers have been used to calculate the value of pi to many decimal places. HyperCard internally uses 3.14159265358979323846 as its value for pi. I would appreciate any and all comments and suggestions. You can contact me by mail at: Arthur V. Dieli, Harbor Road, Henderson Harbor, NY, 13651-0009 or by email at: ArthurD12@AOL.com or DieliAV@eWorld.com.

_source.title: The Bee
_source.creator: Richard Wanderman
_source.description: A bee randomly flies around in a room with a personâs face in it. If the bee lands on the personâs nose it will sting them and their eyes will roll around (like little marbles). If the bee hits any solid wall 10 times it will turn upside down and gently glide to the bottom, dead. If the bee flies through one of the four openings in each room, the little window will animate so that it looks like the room is shifting to the side to display another room with another, different person. This room movement will go on forever with the bee buzzing and bumping into walls (making a bump noise) until the bee dies or the user gets tired of this stupid thing and holds down a key to stop it. Iâm sorry, I canât remember the key at the moment but experiment and comment when you fine it. Note: there are a few easer eggs in The Bee: certain faces have âwormholesâ through their ears and if the bee enters one of them it will disappear, seemingly banging around inside their head, then emerge, either out of their other ear, or, in a special case, the window will shift and the bee will come out another personâs ear in another room. Enjoy. For more on the history of The Bee: https://richardsnotes.org/2005/03/24/the-bee/

_source.title: Film Music 1992
_source.creator: various
_source.description: A sequel to last year's 'Film Music 1991' stack (naturally). It's a simple stack compiled by several friends of mine cataloguing every film score soundtrack album released during 1992 (434 in allxup from 278 last year!). This stack provides the album title, composer(s), label, and miscellaneous notes (contents, reissue info, availability, country of origin, existence of liner notes, etc.) and can be sorted alphabetically by several categories. It also features three checkboxes in which to check off your own particular attitude toward the album ('Got it,' 'Want it,' or 'Skip it'), and you can then sort by this criteria as well. If you're interested in film scores, you'll find this stack useful and fun. If not, you probably won't.
