_source.title: Caper in the Castro
_source.creator: CM Ralph
_source.description: Artist Statement, 2023: "I regret the spelling errors found in the game. I am mildly dyslexic and suck at spelling. Back in 1988, I had no one to edit my writing. I also regret some of the more "cringe-worthy" humor that is inappropriate by today's standards. In some older video reviews as well as interviews, people have used the pronouns "she and her" when describing me. I am transgender and my preferred pronouns are "They, Them, and Their." Note: By default, the sound is off on this emulation. When the desktop boots up, click on the Apple icon in the upper left, select CONTROL PANEL, then click on SOUND and bring the volume level from 0 to 9. This will enable sound during the game. Programmed by CM Ralph (https://cmralph.com/) in 1989, Caper in the Castro is likely the first LGBT game. It was distributed via BBS, and is 'Charityware', with the designer asking players to donate to the AIDS Charity of their choice. You are the world famous lesbian private detective, Tracker McDyke. You are searching for a kidnapped drag queen, Tessy LaFemme. What you didn't count on was stumbling onto an even larger and more treacherous crime. The game was changed to different locations and a heterosexual them released under the name "Murder on Main Street". This version is located here . For more information about "Caper in the Castro": https://www.npr.org/2023/01/27/1151702216/how-the-first-lgbtq-video-game-was-given-a-second-life A Q&A With The Designer of the First LGBT Computer Game , by Luke Winkie, Paste Magazine, June 9, 2014. Caper in the Castro in the LGBTQ Video Game Archive. --------The following is taken from the original description / info text file:-------------- About "Caper in the Castro" This game is a Gay and Lesbian based adventure mystery featuring graphics, text, animation and sound. Written with HyperCard® Version 1.2. (Earlier versions will not be compatible) It is the first Gay and Lesbian computer game to be published in the world, as far as we know. In the game you assume the role of a lesbian private detective, Tracker McDyke, in search of a kidnapped drag queen, Tessy LaFemme. What you stumble onto is an even bigger and more hideous crime with a mystery that surrounds it. (Who is Dullagan Straightman? Who is the mysterious "L.L." that Straightman works for? What is the meaning of the cryptograph?) The game has a built-in help/tutorial if you get stuck, the best advice is to try everything and anything during your adventure. You never know what might happen! The game is packed with inside humor, puns and lots of perplexing problems. About the Author C.M. Ralph is a free lance artist living in the San Francisco Bay area. She supports her indulgence in art and computer game developing by working as a Quality Control Technician. Her work as an artist has spanned some 20 years and includes such notables as the design of the AIDS Response Program logo for Southern California's Orange County chapter, along with much donated work for many other nonprofit Gay and Lesbian Organizations. This game, as she describes it is "CharityWare" follows in this tradition of works for the Gay and Lesbian community. It is given out freely, with her request that if you enjoy playing it, please make a donation to an AIDS Related charity of your choice for whatever amount you feel is appropriate. The Inside Poop Caper in the Castro had a creation time of 6 months, It began in HyperCard version 1.0 and then was upgraded during construction. There was an additional 3 months of test playing in it's beta version. The final game version 1.0 was released in May of1989. Thank You's and credits appear when you click on the "About Game" button at the opening of the game. However there are some add-on's to be mentioned here: Thanks go to Diane, the sysop of Alternatives BBS (415) 922-5489 , for putting me in touch with Bill, the sysop of Fog City BBS (415) 863-9697 , (Who was enthusiastic enough to carry this game, thus allowing it nation-wide distribution.) Thank you Bill.

_source.title: CompileIt!
_source.creator: Tom Pittman
_source.description:
CompileIt! is the first true compiler for HyperTalk, the programming language for non-programmers on the Apple Macintosh. Before CompileIt!, HyperTalk scripts were constrained to run slowly in HyperCard - with no protection from prying eyes! The only alternative was to abandon the ease and comfort of HyperTalk for a conventional language such as C or Pascal. CompileIt! makes available the power and speed of these languages, without requiring that you learn a new language and new programming environment.

 CompileIt! translates the HyperTalk language into 68000 machine language, and packages the result as a HyperCard external command (XCMD) or function (XFCN) which can then be used from any HyperCard script.

 CompileIt! also provides many useful extensions to the HyperTalk language which will give you access to features of the Macintosh far beyond what HyperCard alone provides.

 Michel, Steve. (1994). CompileIt! User Manual . Heizer Software. Pleasant Hill, CA.

 
 Compilelt! 2.0 is a significant upgrade to Heizer Software's HyperTalk compiler. This version supports HyperCard 2.0's new XCMD interface and is significantly faster than the previous version. Compilelt! allows anyone familiar with HyperCard's scripting language to write external commands and functions that extend HyperCards capabilities.

 A Stack-Based Compiler

 Since HyperCard was introduced in 1987, its XCMD interface has been its most powerful feature. By writing code that conforms to the XCMD-interface format and installing it in a stack's resource fork, programmers can add commands and functions that customize HyperCard’s scripting language. CompileIt!’s mandate is to give the power of customization to those unfamiliar with conventional programming languages such as Pascal or C.

 Compilelt! is unique in that it is implemented as a HyperCard stack and its programming language is HyperTalk. External commands and functions were given those monikers because Apple programmers expected them to be developed outside of HyperCard, in another programing 1anguage. CompileIt!, however, makes developing externals possible within HyperCard.

 Although it's implemented as a HyperCard stack, Compilelt! isn’t limited to HyperCard. XCMDs developed with Compilelt! can be installed and used in SuperCard projects, for example, or with other products, such as Authorware and MacroMind Director, that support XCMDs.

 To create XCMDs with Compilelt!, you type or paste HyperTalk scripts into fields in the Compilelt! stack and click on a button. Compilelt! subsequently compiles the code, installs it in the resource fork of the target slack, and sends you the stack so that you can test the XCMD.

 CompileIt! provides various indications of its progress during compilation, which can run in the background under MultiFinder. Here's an indication of its compilation speed: A 40-line test script took only about three minutes to compile into a 2,400-byte XFCN when running in the background on an 8-megabyte Macintosh II.

 You'll probably need to revisit the HyperTalk code to debug or improve it. CompileIt!, which has all the basic components of a programming environment, can assist you with all the tools you need to edit and debug code as well as analyze code performance,

 Getting the Bugs Out

 One of these tools, DebugIt!, is new in version 2.0. DebugIt! is a source-level debugger you can install in the external (adding 40K to the external’s size). With DebugIt! installed, you can see (but not modify) the source code, set break points, single-step through the code, and examine and edit the values of variables. You can also halt execution, send HyperCard an arbitrary HyperTalk message, and resume execution.

 According to Heizer, DebugIt! is not dependent on any HyperCard-specific features beyond those in HyperCard 1.0. so it should work with any application that supports externals (Heizer has tested it with all the versions of HyperCard and SuperCard),

 CompileIt! partially executes its translation by looking up symbols in a symbol table. The symbol table contains not only the names of HyperTalk functions but also the names of those unique to SuperTalk and the names of more than 500 Mac ROM Toolbox routines. The latter are gateways to real Mac programming, CompileIt!’s manual gives a good but brief introduction to ROM calls, which are covered in more depth in Inside Macintosh.

 Some programming experience is required to use ROM calls. Not only do they speed up code but the roughly 100 Color QuickDraw routines are also useful for SuperCard developers or for anyone developing external windows.

 Beginners may stumble when it comes to using numerics and string variables from within CompileIt!, which isn't as forgiving as HyperTalk in the use of these data types. Novices also need to be aware that even if a HyperTalk script compiles perfectly in CompileIt!, it may tend to behave in subtly different ways. This happens because the external cannot occupy exactly the same place in the HyperCard message hierarchy as a HyperTalk script does. Also, because an external, unlike a script, is not associated with an object, the HyperTalk term me, which refers to the script’s object, doesn’t make sense in an external and is not supported.

 Although CompileIt!'s manual represents a solid effort (users with some HyperTalk experience will be able to grasp the fundamentals), only programmers with broader experience will he able to make complete sense of it. Even though it acknowledges that it's possible to use CompileIt! to develop other kinds of software, such as Fkeys, the manual doesn't cover such advanced use. The on-line documentation covers most of the manual topics in less depth, and there’s Balloon Help for System 7 users.

 In addition to the compiler and the 200-page manual, the CompileIt! package contains a demo stack, a batch-compiling stack, Steve Mailer’s ResCopy, a folder of alternative symbol tables, and HyperCard. The program requires 750K of memory, but Heizer recommends 2 megabytes. To its credit, CompileIt! is clever with memory. As far back as 1975, when 4K was a lot of memory, Tom Pittman, CompileIt!'s creator, wrote a complete BASIC interpreter and sold it for $5 per copy. He understands how to write memory-efficient code.

 The Bottom Line

 Although the original version of CompileIt! was so slow, that it wasn’t practical for serious development work, version 2.0 features much-improved compilation speed and supports the latest version of HyperCard, With its unique implementation as a HyperCard stack, CompileIt! makes the process of creating XCMDs and XFCNs accessible to anyone who's familiar with HyperTalk.

 Swaine, Michael. (February 1992). CompileIt! 2.0. MacUser . (pgs. 74, 76).

_source.title: Uncle Buddy's Phantom Funhouse
_source.creator: John McDaid
_source.description:
Art "Buddy" Newkirk has disappeared and left you his literary estate. By the looks of it, he and his friends were a very odd bunch.

 You might have enjoyed knowing them. But you don't: why does "Uncle" Buddy think you do? Where is he, anyway? And what does this have to do with Meister Eckhart and the New York City subway?

 To find out, you'll have to pop the floppies into your Mac, drop the tapes into your boombox, and get ready to meet Buddy's friends, read his email, listen to his band, and sort out his (very strange) Tarot deck.

 Surreal and humorous, this is a world you will often return to and long remember.

_source.title: MacRecorder
_source.creator: Farallon Computing, Inc.
_source.description:
Sound recording software for use with the MacRecorder system. The MacRecorder device is a sound input interface for early Macintosh machines that plugged into a serial port (since early machines did not have a microphone port). I believe the SoundEdit software was absorbed by Macromedia in later years.

 The MacRecorder Sound System includes an audio digitizer and three programs: SoundEdit, HyperSound, and the HyperSound Toolkit.

_source.title: +Beverage Bonanza 1.0.1
_source.creator: Eric & Kris Holsen
_source.description: Beverage Bonanza (ver. 1.0.1) c1996 by Eric & Kris Holsen (VeronaEMH@aol.com) Staff Pick: This is a very well done stack, looks like a lot more fun than a social studies textbook : ) Thank you for your interest in this Verona Software product. NOTE: This is a fix of the version of this program uploaded previously. The bug that kept you from buying anything but lemons has been fixed. Beverage Bonanza is an economic simulation game designed to help students understand some basic concepts about economics through a simulated 'real-world', hands-on learning experience. The student gets to be the owner of a lemonade and hot chocolate stand. In order to run this business they will need to make a number of business decisions such as: how many materials to purchase, how much product to make, and what price to sell the product at. The choices the student makes will affect how well she/he does in the game. This program was written by a 7th and 8th grade social studies teacher and his artistic (art teacher) wife. The program has been tested in my classroom, and I found it to be an excellent way to make complex economic concepts come alive for my students. Not only did the students learn a good deal from running the simulation, but they also enjoyed it. (It never hurts to have students actually enjoy something you do in class!) Beverage Bonanza is shareware, IT IS NOT FREE ! If you decide to use the program, you should register it and pay the shareware fee. The cost of registering Beverage Bonanza is $5.00 for a single copy, or $20.00 for a site license. Try to get that kind of value from a commercial software company! * Macintosh and HyperCard are registered trademarks of Apple Computer, Inc.

_source.title: Foggy
_source.creator: John M. Lawler
_source.description: About fifteen years ago a former student sent me [John M. Lawler] a program called foggy that had been circulating underground at IBM which artfully made fun of pompous administrators and their jargon. It was in an internal IBM language, and I translated it into BASIC and Pascal. It was a cute program, but essentially very simple. Somewhat later, I chanced on the Folklore Paper Construction Kit as quoted in Dwight Bolinger's prize-winning Language: The Loaded Weapon, and added its vocabulary of phrases as another option. Finally, I met Anthony Aristar and he shared with me a similar program ( in Lisp) he'd written with phrases collected from the syntactic works of Noam Chomsky. That was the origin of The Chomskybot. It was easy to add these phrases, too. Anthony says he was given these phrases in another program, and didn't know who actually collected them. However, since the Chomskybot has become semi-famous, we have been able to identify the original author as John F. Sowa, who admits putting the phrases together from several of Chomsky's books, including Syntactic Structures, Aspects, and Government and Binding at IBM in the 1980's. Since the real ingenuity in the Chomskybot is the way the phrases fit together, both Anthony and I are delighted to acknowledge Mr. Sowa as The Onlie Begetter of the Chomskybot. (Though we do notice that he hasn't owned up to it on his Web site yet :-) Eventually I got around to making a Macintosh version using HyperCard, to supplement the DOS Version with source code in Pascal. The above description is taken verbatim from http://www-personal.umich.edu/~jlawler/foggy.faq.html . The stack was taken from http://www-personal.umich.edu/~jlawler/ .

_source.title: Esperanto Language Course
_source.creator: Michael Urban
_source.description: A complete course in the international language Esperanto. Esperanto is used regularly for 'personal diplomacy' and correspondence by thousands of people around the world, and is understood passively by hundreds of thousands more. While no self-study course can provide the spoken fluency that comes with practice, this course is a complete treatment of the grammar and many aspects of usage. It uses MacinTalk to synthesize Esperanto speech in several places. A System 7-friendly version of MacinTalk can be purchased as part of the Talking Moose application. This upload supersedes the previous Esperanto Language Course and the EsperantoCourse.sit upload (which is the same thing) in the MED:Reading/Languages library. Downloads of Previous Version: 37

_source.title: HyperPhysQuiz-Nerves 1.0
_source.creator: Ronald L. Girmus
_source.description: Physioquiz 1.O-Nervous Tissue is a simple multimedia application that utilizes graphics, text, and interactivity to form an interactive, computerized study quide for the anatomy and physiology of nervous tissue. This study quide concentrates on the cellular and molecular structures and mechanisms that are responsible for nervous tissue function. Outline and objective sections provide the user with a detailed overview of key concepts. Reviews illustrate and describe molecular/cellular structures and functions. _source.title: Midi Metronome
_source.creator: RScottR
_source.description: Midi Metronome is a Hypercard stack which drives a midi sound generator (Sound Canvas) for the purpose of teaching proper timing to musicians and music students. A simple repeating drum pattern (boom/chuk) allows you to FEEL the beat compared to most metronomes with those wimpy 'clicks' which can never be heard over the sound of the instrument. Tempo's are fully adjustable by the student with a mouse click. If you do not have expensive midi equipment available but would reap the benefits of 'feeling' your metronome, drop me a note (no pun intended). I have created a series of metronome cassette tapes which can be played through any home stereo setup. Not as convenient and flexible as midi, but a lot cheaper! I used a Roland Sound Canvas SC-55, but the stack should work fine with other sound modules as well (kinda). It assumes you are running midi through the modem port at 1mhz. The port is reset when you first launch the stack so don't open the stack if you're connected to an on-line service! Enjoy! CIS: 75046,754

_source.title: Vocabulary Situations Lite
_source.creator: Phil Squared
_source.description: 'Vocabulary Situations Lite' Phil Shapiro posted a text file in this area in February requesting that someone build a stack around it. He described the file as follows: 'This short text file is a series of 'vocabulary situations' ...' '... Each vocabulary situation describes a scenario where students are called upon to choose from one of two options. To choose the correct option, though, students needs to understand the vocabulary used in describing the scenario.' This stack is my response to his request. The stack contains the thirty situations in the list, has room for up to 170 more, and the stack can be adapted for other flash card purposes. See the Help stack for instructions on how to edit the data. A version containing more sound resources was uploaded to AOL earlier. Since the size of the stack with all the extra data might have been off-putting to potential downloaders (there have been only 17 downloads to date - we should have named it Naked Girl Situations), I split the stack and put the sounds and some utilities in a stack called 'VS Sounds'. You might call this version 'Vocabulary Situations Lite'. The functionality of the stack is the same, except that it looks for the stack 'VS Sounds' and starts using the sounds in that stack for transitions. If it doesn't find it the first time, it never looks again. You don't need 'VS Sounds' unless you want a larger variety of sounds or you want to easily record or scrounge or manipulate the sounds to your own taste. As always, response is requested. Fillmore 051695

_source.title: Solving Algabraic Equations
_source.creator: Richard Piotter
_source.description: Equation Solvent was created by me, Richard Piotter. I have been programing since I was 10. I learned BASIC by using the manuals for my OLD TRS-80 Color Computer 2. I learned parts of the HyperTalk scripting language by examining the scripts of stacks and tring things out with the message box. Later, the computer applications teacher at my school, Thomas Hartwig, let me borrow a HyperTalk sripting reference book. Now I work with HyperCard all the time. Now I work with animation, sound, speech synthesis, and color, just to name a few. I developed Equation Solvent to help me with my geometry. The first study hall I got, I wrote out a BASIC program to use with my TRS-80. Soon after, I made a HyperCard stack that could calculate complimentary and supplimentary angles. My next version included the ability to find the value of X when the equations equaled each other. In the next version I made, you could access the Calculator Application with a button, IF it was in the apple menu. It could also find the value of X when the sums of the two equations equal what is entered in the sums box. This version has a built in calculator and is protected so kids can't accidentally mess any thing up. Equation Solvent 2.1 is a must for people who have trouble with Geometry or algebra. *** You'll have to expiriment with the step if increase or decrease number. I can usually get by with .25 in the box. If you don't get an answer with that setting, try smaller numbers. It will take longer, but you should get some kind of answer. If you like it, I would appreciate it if you sent me 5 dollars. I also want your questions and comments. Also, when I create HyperCard stacks, I make them simple and easy to use. I am always trying to make my stacks better and better, and those of you who send in the 5 dollars will be the first to get info about new things. If you can send in your screen name, I might be able to send e-mail that would describe what I have. You can send a check to me at: Richard Piotter 301 Maple SE Sleepy Eye, MN 56085 If you want to send me questions or comments, you can send them to me at the same address or send them through America Online. Use the screenName RichFiles. I also checked the stack for viruses using Sam Intercept.

_source.title: Spell Spell II v1.1 Demo
_source.creator: Steven Rodgers
_source.description: (Previous version had 30 downloads) Spell Spell II is a totally new (Macintosh) version of the original Spell Spell program designed for the Apple II. It helps kids study their spelling words. It displays the word for a short period of time. and speaks it (optional). The child is then prompted to enter the spelling of the word. If the word was spelled correctly, a message is displayed noting that it was correct, plays a sound (also optional) and then continues to the next word. If the word was spelled incorrectly, it displays a message saying so and plays a sound (again, optional). Before continuing to the next word it displays the correct spelling of the word and how the user spelled it so that the user can compare their attempt with the correct spelling. Missed words are presented again until the user spells them correctly. At the end, an analysis of the student's progress is displayed.

_source.title: Paper Chaste 0.7
_source.creator: David Keith Murphy
_source.description: Paper Chaste is a tool to aid teachers in analyzing writing samples in the Roman alphabet. This HyperCard stack calculates the frequency of recorded mistakes so that you can guide students toward correcting the most common mistakes found within their writing as a group, and it does a simple statistical analysis--mean, median, variance, standard deviation, and standardization [z = (X-Mean)/Standard Deviation] of final "gravity of error" totals that are then transformed into grades/marks on an alterable (mean) scale. The mistakes themselves must be entered into a list *by the user*, where they can then be repeatedly loaded into a student report by clicking on the line containing the error. P_C comes with a list of around 90 grammatical errors (in French) and over 20 student reports to serve as data examples. The program is in some ways just a shell that you have to fill in with your own expertise, i.e., P_C does NOT analyze the writing for you; it analyzes the mistakes that you record. Aside from the analysis, the other primary objective of the program is to more actively involve the students in correcting their own mistakes by providing reports to guide them. Documentation is limited to the FAQ file that is bundled with the program.

_source.title: HyperGrapher Calculator 1.0b
_source.creator: Bryan Scott
_source.description: TYPE: Freeware Stack SYSTEM 7: Compatible Author's email address: bscott@east.east-slc.edu I wrote this simple graphic calculator stack because I couldn't afford Mathematica, nor could I buy one of those spiffy PowerMacintoshes with the new 3D graphic calculator on it. So, for those of you who already have one of the above items, then you don't need this stack. However, if you're just an average student-user like myself, read on. Some helpful hints: 1: Type the equations into the fields in HyperCard's standard form. X3 would be typed 'X^3' (minus the apostrophes). 2: All odd exponent powers are rounded off (absolute value)x To make up for this, multiply the variable times itself raised to one less power. 3: The four small fields control the setting of the X-axis and Y-axis, much like the RANGE values found in graphic calculators. The X-off and Y-off are the offset, or how many points (not pixels) to the right, left, up, or down of the origin (the REAL origin). The Xscl and Yscl variables set how many pixels are to be places between each point on the grid. So, if you wanted to 'zoom in', you'd enter in a big number. If you wanted to zoom out, you'd enter in a small number (at least 1). It's not exactly the best screen to zoom in and out of, but it's bigger than some of those hand-held calculator screens, and better resolution too! 4: The Equation Field is merely a field I created to store all my conic section formulas (solved for x). You can modify or copy from it any way you'd like, and I hope you DO personalize it. (Just make sure you put the previous equations BACK if you distribute your working copy.)

_source.creator: Dancing Rabbit Creations
_source.description: Some Features: - Appointments -- an excellent computerized daytimer with detailed scheduling and overview - Substitute Information -- store in-depth information about you and your classroom for guest teachers - Journal -- a place to keep your professional and personal observations and musings - Parent Conferences -- store detailed information about concerns from all sides, and plans for student success - Student Records -- store valuable information about your students, including address, phone numbers, with plenty of room for notes - Optional Hours -- keep track of your beyond-contract hours easily and accurately, then print them out in June - Textbooks -- automate this annual task by keeping information about your books from year to year - Project Plans -- organize and store details of your best projects - Resources -- arrange your materials by theme, project, or unit - Daily Plans -- keep detailed information about your lesson plans for each day of the year - Weekly Plans -- an overview of your week including activities, homework, and work to collect - Report Printing Style -- printouts and forms that don't look like a picture of the screen from most modules - Navigation and Finding tools -- find information quickly and easily ------ Dancing Rabbit Creations produces educational HyperCard programs and games for teachers, students, parents, and learners of all ages. The stacks are affordably priced, and users can try them before they buy - all are released as shareware, or are available in demonstration versions. Dancing Rabbit Creations is the joint (ad)venture of a classroom teacher and a software tester. NOTE: This stack is a demo version of the software and full working copy.

_source.title: 1st. Law of Thermodynamics
_source.creator: H. Allan Peoples
_source.description: Keywords : Freshman Chemistry Chemical Thermodynamics Chemistry lessons This stack is an advertisement for a set of lessons on the first law of thermodynamics (as it is seen from the chemical point of view). This is the first set of lessons in the CheMentor series of software products. The set consists of six lessons and six tests which can be used on individual machines or on a networked system of Macs. They have been tested with Freshman students and received enthusiastic reviews. The basic ideas of system, surroundings, energy transfers (heat and work), internal energy, measurement of internal energy change (or E, if you like), enthalpy, measurement of enthalpy change, relation between enthalpy change and internal energy change, thermochemical equations and Hess's law are all covered. Product will include full documentation. See also the demos 'Chemistry Tutoring' (which outlines the CheMentor series in more detail) and 'The Chemistry Toolkit' - also the Shareware product 'The Molar Mass Calculator' - all of which are available from AOL (use Command-5 or the GoTo menu (File Search)).

_source.title: Softcourse System 1.22 BETA
_source.creator: Captain Megabyte
_source.description: Shareware, $20 Softcourse was originally designed to assist college-level students in learning information presented to them through video telecourses. It affords these students an opportunity to experience a more structured learning environment (which the television courses lacked) without confining a student to a set class schedule or instructor. The technology of television freed the student from having to attend lectures while allowing them to still see them; Computer technology allows the student a more involved environment for learning things with the same flexibility that the television course material offers. Softcourse is a HyperCard stack that is already half-written for you. This half-written stack is laid out in a way so that any information put into it will be in an accessible and learnable format. All of the heavy technical-whizbang stuff has been figured out for you. Softcourse is meant to let you focus on what you do the best: sharing the knowledge you have. In many ways, it is like a form which you can fill your very own information into, doodle a little here and there, and feed it through a machine that makes it a finished product. You are, of course, still very responsible for the content of your material, but the stress of presenting that material in this new way (on the computer) is lessened. The reason for that is (hopefully) because you don't have to worry about much else. What Softcourse offers to you, the instructor, is the freedom to construct a piece of information-rich courseware that not only allows the student to engage in active, self-directed exploration of material, but also automatically remediates. That is, it alters how it presents material to the student as it finds out what portions of the material they are having difficulty with, on the fly. Many courseware program force learners to absorb the material in an almost linear fashion, and become limiting, binding, and impractical. Softcourse attempts to be different. It provides you the opportunity to positively utilize HyperCard without learning all of its intracies. On the other hand, the more you learn about HyperCard, the more you can make your courseware interactive and customized. You want to have a certain piece of material jump to another stack, do something, and jump back? Softcourse allows you to modify and add to any portion of it, until your courseware is just right. In essence, it lets you concentrate on the content of a stack, while Softcourse software concetrates on the function. From that end, your students get your best material, a step-by-step list of things to explore, structured tests set up to your specifications, auto-remediation, and it still lets them access any piece of information, look at any test's results, take any test, or check what their list of things to do it at any time. Pretty Spiffy, eh? Megs
_source.modernAnalog: An interactive study module that adapts lessons and practice around one topic without forcing me into a full LMS.

_source.title: TagNotes 1.0
_source.creator: Peter M. Brigham
_source.description: Text is entered into the main text field. Each card's tags appear in the 'tags' field. A running list of all tags attached to all cards appears in the 'all tags' field. Tags can be created, attached to a card, detached from a card, or deleted from the stack entirely. Any tag can be made the 'active tag.' It is easy to navigate to the next card bearing the active tag, to search for the active tag in the text of any or all cards, and to export the text of the cards with the active tag. In this way, multiple connections may be made between topics, subtopics, related topics, etc. and information may be organized and accessed according to the needs of the moment. If you're a student and you use a PowerBook, try taking lecture notes or reading notes in this stack. If you then attach appropriate tags to the cards, you'll be able to use the tags and searching capabilities to review your notes in a very flexible way. Or if you have a whole bunch of information on some general topic, and you want a way to access it easily, try importing it into TagNotes.

_source.description: This is a very interactive hypercard stack which teaches very young kids math with positive numbers in Spanish. With the random generator there are millions of questions and possible answers. A question is presented visually and spoken in Spanish and three possible choices are give. You have 2 tries. I will download the French and German versions of this program sometime in the near future. This is shareware so please feel free to try it out.

_source.title: HyperCard Desktop Pub. Sample...
_source.creator: Charles Doe/SchoolHouse Mac
_source.description: SchoolHouse Mac usually uploads to the MED forum but we uploaded this sampler here because the stack it represents has material of interest to HyperCard newcomers. Desktop Publishing with HyperCard Sampler contains samples of the graphics, projects, ideas and articles available in the nearly 1500k stack from SchoolHouse Mac. This particular sampler has a little less useful clipart than some of the samplers we've uploaded, but we wanted to give you an idea of topics, projects and tutorials, the larger stack contains. The complete stack incorporates, but goes far beyond, two project ideas we uploaded earlier. They are 'Awesome Tickets' and 'Notes and Memos'. They are still available in the MED forum. Teachers Desktop Publishing with HyperCard came about because of HyperCard's power and flexibility. The combination of graphic and writing tools along with the capability of creating stacks of varying sizes and printing in different sizes make HyperCard an excellent, basic desktop publishing program for teachers. Teachers Desktop Publishing with HyperCard stack is a departure from our other SuperClipArt stacks in that it combines about 400 pieces of clipart, borders, and backgrounds, with approximately 14 simple desktop publishing project ideas for personal use and use with elementary and middle school students. Complete instructions for these projects and working with HyperCard in connection with them are included. We included some simple suggestions for creating your own art and working with HyperCard's graphic tools as well. The complete stack serves as clipart resource, project idea source and HyperCard tutorial all in one. (Note: The intent is not to create a complete HyperCard tutorial, but the stack will help newcomers to HyperCard learn to use some of its features.) The sampler contains complete information about the stack and ordering. If you've any questions or comments, please contact me on AOL or write me at SchoolHouse Mac. Charles Doe SchoolHouse Mac 5236 Coats Grove Rd. Hastings, MI 49058

_source.creator: Paul J. Treffner
_source.description: TreffNet is a simple, Hypercard-based introduction to learning in neural networks. It consists of a tutorial on the simplest of network learning methods called Hebbian learning. The stacks consist of (1) tutorial animations, and (2) a graphical network simulator that can learn to associate two pattern vectors. Based on the first chapter of the classic text by Rumelhart and McClelland (1986), it "brings to life" the basic concepts and mathematics that need to be fully understood before the student tackles more complex systems.

_source.title: House of Multiplication Horro...
_source.creator: Glen L. Bledsoe
_source.description: The House of Multiplication Horror AlphaDog SoftWare by Glen L. Bledsoe Just in time for Halloween! This stack is a big improvement over flashcards -- including my previous 'Flashy Flashcards.' It can used by an individual, but was designed especially for use by a classroom. If the answer is correct a random visual effect will take place and a creepy sound will be heard. If the answer is wrong, the correct answer will be shown in the message box. If a student takes too long to answer the fact, even if his or her answer is correct, they will not get credit for having mastered the fact, but will receive a reward for having gotten the right answer. This stack discards the facts that students already know and proceeds to drill them in groups of four only on the facts that they have shown that they do not know or do not know quickly. After the student demonstrates that he or she knows a given fact well (by getting it correct 4 times while being shuffled in with 3 other facts), that fact is discarded and is replaced by another fact that the student had trouble with previously until all 91 multiplication facts are mastered. Research indicates that this is a very effective way to use flashcards. This stack will not forget where a student left off in his or her practice from day to day. They can come back at any time and pick up where they left off. This stack works best having the PostCrypt Font in your System Folder! It is available elsewhere on AOL. This is Freeware! Share is with anyone who can use it.

_source.title: LinguaFlash - vocab helper
_source.creator: Randy Nargi
_source.description: This is an upgrade of Italiflash -- the HyperCard stack I wrote to help me improve my vocabulary for Elementary Italian class. It can be used to help you learn vocabulary of most any foreign language. It is based on the flash card metaphor, and it's very easy to use. Words are chosen randomly from a list. When all words in the list have been displayed, ItaliFlash will notify you that it is resetting the word list. As your vocabulary improves, you'll want to change the word list by deleting words you know very well and adding new words you don't know. Just click on ADD WORDS at any time. A list on the right side of the screen will display all the words currently loaded into the program. To save your word list to an external file, click on SAVE LIST. To recall a previous list, click on LOAD LIST. These features allow you to have multiple lists of vocabulary words. Shareware: $5. (A stand-alone application is also available; see LinguaFlash read-me, enclosed). Checked for Mac & HC viruses with Virex 5.6.

_source.title: Eng./Spanish HC children's st...
_source.creator: Phil Shapiro & friends
_source.description: Hi folks - This upload includes four original children's stories, presented in both English and Spanish in HyperCard stacks. I originally wrote these stories for my elementary school students. Some of the stories are whimsical and fun. Last month I uploaded the stories to the Macintosh Education Forum library with a suggestion that these stories might be nicely presented in HyperCard or HyperStudio format. Richard Wanderman, a talented HyperCard using educator from Connecticut, did a lovely job of creating these freeware stacks. Sofia Medina, a teacher from Sarasota, Florida, jumped in to assist with some of the translations. Hope these stacks prove useful to other teachers and parents. If there's anyone out there who can assist in some further translation work, I have a few other children's stories I'd like to share as freeware. It's really wonderful when educators are able to collaborate in this way. The fruits of our joint work can be enjoyed by all of our own students, as well as everyone else's students, too. The next challenge is to create some freeware quiz stacks which can quiz students on both the English and Spanish words used in these stories. Any volunteers to assist in creating the quiz stacks? Cheers, - Phil Shapiro Washington DC Internet: pshapiro@aol.com Oh, by the way, also enclosed in this archive are screen shots from these stack, imported into MacWrite II. These can be printed out quite easily from MacWrite II, ClarisWorks, or Microsoft Word. Note: The screen shots print better on laser printers than on inkjet printers. I like having a few copies of the screen shots printed out to show friends and colleagues who are not Mac users.

_source.description: FlashAttack! is a HyperCard stack that allows students to practice multiplication facts. They can choose a variety of facts to practice. The user will also be able to take a timed test. At the end of the test, the date, score, and time it took the user will be displayed and saved. You will be able to view the scores with a password. The password is: FLASH009. Scores can be printed out or cleared. WARNING - the stack automatically deletes scores after 100 names have been entered. FlashAttack! is a shareware program. This version is provided to the user to preview. It can be distributed to anyone as long as a fee is not charged and it is distributed with the read me first file. If you like FlashAttack! and use it, please send in the registration and fee. You will be sent a registration number to enter into the program. The program can then be used on 2 computers. _source.title: EucaQuiz v3.8
_source.creator: John Reed
_source.description: Help and Instructions EucaQuiz has two functions. The first is to provide budding botany and dendrology students with a detailed reference of the common eucalypt species found in Victoria. All reference material has been sourced from "Eucalypts of South Eastern Victoria" by Leon Costermans (See the references page for full details). The second function of Eucaquiz is to encourage learning of the material found within the book "Eucalypts of South Eastern Victoria" as well as to reinforce your field observations. Eucaquiz is fun, challenging and competitive. Not only does the quiz keep a record of your performance, it also records the time you took to complete the quiz! Species reference instructions The species index lists all the species found in EucaQuiz. Simply click a species to go to it. Once there you are able to view detailed information for that species, including drawings of buds, leaves and location of natural stands. Alternatively you can browse through the different species by using the 'next' and 'previous' buttons. Quiz instructions The quiz is really simple to use. Just click on the 'Quiz' button to start the quiz. You will be asked to enter your full name and the type of quiz. When all the questions have been answered the quiz will stop automatically and take you to the score board. If you got any questions wrong then you are able to get a printout of the questions you got wrong, but not the suggested answers. It's up to you to learn the correct answers. Whilst undertaking the quiz if you get a question wrong the correct answer is highlighted temporarily before the quiz moves on to the next question. Good luck and if you have and suggestions or bugs to report please send me a note via email at j.reed@forestry.unimelb.edu.au

_source.title: Pentus
_source.creator: Edouard Albert
_source.description: Who are you ? Where are you ? A game about amnesia in modern suburb environment. In French. Pentus is a student project done between 1996 and 1997. It was a part of my final exam at the fine arts schools, a time where there was no videogame schools a all. The project was really poorly appreciated by my teachers so to say... Hopefully it did not prevent me from working in videogames a few years later. About the game: it's an adventure game which navigation, inventory and dialog systems are inspired by popular 90's games like Myst or LucasArt's. The action takes place in a dirty supermarket of an undefined suburb where you come across strange characters. The game is in French and was published in SVM Mac (CD-Rom No. 34 of the print issue No. 94 in April 1998).

Versions 2.0.1 and 2.0.2 fix bugs that caused the game to be unplayable on machines running System 6 and color not to be recognized on some newer PowerMacs, plus a few other minor improvements.

_source.title: Creepin' Critter Clocks 1.1.1
_source.creator: Bruce A. Pokras
_source.description: Welcome to Creepin' Critter Clocks, the fun way for kids to practice their time-telling skills! Creepin' Critter Clocks is an arcade-style learning game that is both challenging and fun. Kids swat bugs (the "Creepin' Critters") or pop bubbles that correspond to the correct answer to a time-telling problem. Points are earned, in true arcade game fashion, for correct answers. The amount of time available to answer a problem can be adjusted to suit the skill level of the child. The arcade-style scoring provides an incentive for decreasing the amount of time, since lower time settings increase the possible scores.

_source.title: Spy!
_source.creator: Michael Mothner
_source.description:
Short HyperCard adventure game from 1995.

 The setting takes place in Russia. The year is 2003. Two years ago, Boris Yeltsin declared himself dictator of Russia. He soon built the devastated country into a nuclear superpower, with secret missile silos located around the globe. You are a special agent with the CIA. Your specialty is Russian intelligence. Your mission: To infiltrate the top secret Moscow Palace. To kill Boris Yeltsin. To get the locations of the worldwide missile silos. And to escape alive!

_source.title: Stack O' Love version 2.0
_source.creator: Best Brains/Lynn Siprelle
_source.description: What it is: The ultimate database for fans of Mystery Science Theater 3000. It contains information and material original to the stack and unavailable elsewhere, as well as Frequently Asked Questions, a glossary, the MST Poker and Drinking games, a list of Internet sites, and much more. Users will also find a handy episode guide/tape catalog feature, allowing them to keep track of the mountains of video tapes they have lying around. Changes since version 1.0: * Throughout: more coherent design, allowing for easier navigation and a sharper look; no more deadend buttons; additions to virtually every segment, bringing them up to date and correcting errors. * Episode guide: Now complete through season five, with as much information about the upcoming season six as I could scrounge. * Lyrics: Now complete, with a couple of features for easier navigation: buttons allowing quick access to the card of the particular episode the song appears in; and an index page by season. * Internet: A completely redesigned section. * A new section called Oddities, which contains the Drinking Game, an updated Cookbook, the show history, and a new item, the MST Poker Games. Lynsa
_source.modernAnalog: A searchable pocket reference for Stack O' Love version 2.0 that I can browse offline and share specific entries from when needed.

_source.title: +The Lost Pharoah
_source.creator: Daniel Molitor
_source.description: The Lost Pharoah Tuesday, June 23, 1998 'The Lost Pharoah' is a work of hyperfiction set in Egypt. It's not quite a game, but much more than a standard work of fiction. It's got cool interactive stuff, some nifty art, a bunch of pictures from Egypt, information on ancient Egypt and various archeological sites, and some interesting surprises. I know it's a pretty hefty file and takes a while to download, but if you're interested in Egypt at all, or just want a unique hypercard stack, it's worth it. No, really! It's worth the effort! Aw, c'mon! Download it! Pleeeze? Huhhhhh? C'mon!!!! Thanks. And 'hi' to wonko.

_source.title: Cartographer
_source.creator: Mike Bailey
_source.description: Cartographer is an XFCN for HyperCard that draws a grid of cicns VERY fast and easily. This XFCN is perfect for RPGs. Until now color HyperCard RPGs were either black and white or color slow and choppy. I saw some good color HyperCard RPGs but got tired of watching that wave of cicns each time the map was drawn. Well no more wave because cartographer draws ANY size grid with ANY size cicns in just 2 - 3 ticks all at once!!! Best of all if you've been looking for something like this but don't want to spend big bucks then here it is... only $5 to register and gain the ability to use in your own stacks. This XFCN is for anybody who is looking to make a good, smooth, and fast RPG in HyperCard. Staff Notes: - Nice effort for a grid map of ICON, CICN tiles ... many creative possibilities. - All files are checked for known viruses before being made available for downloading

_source.title: Reservoir Simulator
_source.creator: Ken Dragoon
_source.description: This program simulates reservoir operations on the Columbia River system. Several species of salmon have been declared threatened or endangered in the river system. Use this simulator to find a way to better operate the reservoir for the salmon and for the other uses of the reservoir-- flood control, power, resident (non migratory) fish, and recreation. A fun simulation game that lets you operate a hydro power reservoir and try to save the Northwest salmon! See the impacts on recreation, flooding, power, and fish of your own strategy for operating the reservoir. This is a fun learning tool and will give useful insight into how reservoirs operate, and also the kinds of social tradeoffs that have to be made in operating the reservoirs. The issues raised in this program are real life issues being addressed by reservoir operators in the Pacific Northwest. This program is an upgrade of an earlier version. The primary change is you can now keep track of your reservoir operation through the year, and print out a graph of the operation and the relative benefits. It also fixes a few minor bugs, and has greatly reduced memory requirements (found dormant code).
_source.modernAnalog: A self-contained interactive experience I can play or simulate on my phone and share with friends if I want.

_source.title: Creepin' Critter Math 4.0
_source.creator: Bruce A. Pokras
_source.description: Creepin' Critter Math is an arcade-style learning game which provides children with math practice in a way that is both challenging and fun. They swat bugs (the "Creepin' Critters") or pop bubbles that correspond to the correct answer to a math problem. Points are earned, in true arcade game style, for correct answers. The amount of time available to answer a problem can be adjusted to suit the math skill level of the child. The arcade-style scoring provides an incentive for decreasing the amount of time, since lower time settings increase the possible scores. 1) COUNTING objects up to 10; 2) BEGINNER Math addition and subtraction problems involving numbers no higher than 10; 3) BASIC math facts for addition, subtraction, multiplication and division; and 4) ADVANCED two step problems for older children which involve carrying, borrowing and remainders.

_source.description: HyperMemory is a funny game that I have written more than 2 years and a half ago. I was 16 1/2 years old. This stack show how to write a 'Memory' game under HyperCard 2.x. The stack is unlocked, so you show all the code. The only important thing that be aware is that the 'Do not interrupt' button in 'Protect Stack' MUST be checked, because the card mixing algorithm may trash the stack if it is interrupted. This stack is public domain, and may be distributed freely everywhere. AOL, Compu and BBS users, please upload it into the libraries if it is not in.

_source.title: Astrology Treasure Hunt Demo
_source.creator: Gerald CA
_source.description: ASTROLOGY, PLANETS, SIGNS, HOROSCOPE, ZODIAC,TREASURE HUNT GAME, ASTEROIDS, FREEWARE DEMO, MAC ______________________ When is the Leo time to play, the Saturn time to work, the Venus time to love? Just as the Sun rises and sets every day, so do all of the other planets (Sun, Moon through Pluto, and asteroids Ceres, Pallas, Vesta, and Juno) have a 5-10 minute period when they are on the angles (rising, setting, culminating and at the nadir). This program generates a listing of when the planets are on the angles and when the angles change sign FOR YOUR LOCATION AND TIME. Includes interpretation for each planet and sign. I call the first, lowest level of significance, the 'forced' level. For example, if you see that Mars is on the Ascendant, you should look around for sharp or metal objects. You would know what you wanted to find (things, events, and people 'ruled' by Mars), and start looking for them. The next level I call the 'awareness' level. Keeping with the Mars example, you might become aware that you have been sitting on wrought iron chairs for an hour. The last, third, and highest level is the 'event' level. You might see on TV one or two police with all of their Mars equipment (gun, handcuffs, baton, etc.). This demo version has all the features of the commercial version except printing is limited (complete listing displayed on screen). The commercial version may be ordered from the author below for $25. The features new to 'Astrology Treasure Hunt 2.0c' are: => Asteroids Ceres, Pallas, Juno, Vesta and planet Chiron have been added. These symbolize many feminine traits not covered by Moon and Venus: Sexuality leading to spiritual awakening; nuturing all life; the rules necessary for one-to-one relationships; wisdom needed for people to live together cooperatively => Much expanded interpretation of all planets and signs => True type 'Glyphs' font print beautifully -- thanks to Time Cycles Research cooperation in my font development. => Retrogrades shown by 'R' Gerald Baron M.S Professional Astrologer since 1972 POB 426501 San Francisco, CA 94142-6501 (415) 751-4661 voice/eve & weekend Internet: GeraldCA@aol.com 'The years teach things the days never knew.' Checked for Mac and HyperCard viruses with virex 5.6

_source.title: The Macintosh Fanzine (German)
_source.creator: 68kMac
_source.description:
The Macintosh Fanzine is a contemporary and still active German Macintosh fanzine distributed as a HyperCard stack that is released roughly once every 3–4 months. The fanzine puts a focus on the family of Macintosh LC computers and is intended to be read on old Macs, hence the 800k images ready to be copied onto a real floppy.

 The stacks are of high quality, featuring neat b&w graphics and (sometimes) even sounds. Often a "goodie" like a freeware game or a save game is included—just like in the old days! (except this one got newly published in 2019!)

 All of the magazine is produced on authentic early 90s Macintosh hardware (some is written about this in various issues).

_source.title: Cosmic Osmo and the Worlds Beyond the Mackerel (CD version)
_source.creator: Rand and Robyn Miller
_source.description:
Cosmic Osmo is a point-and-click graphical adventure game for kids made by Cyan which would later make the hugely successful Myst series. This is an open-ended exploring game where you can click on various elements on screen to trigger events, and clicking on doors or the edge of the screen will move you to another location.

 You first enter an animated spaceship which later is used to travel between different planets. Shortcuts also exist to other places in small spots like a mouse hole or a water drain. There is no way to die or get hurt in the game and you can't lose either.

 It was made originally on Macintosh using Hypercard, a combined software and run-time environment that mixes database capabilities with a graphical, flexible, user-modifiable interface. The game was one of the first on CD-Rom but a floppy disk version with fewer planets and no background music was also available.

 - ( MobyGames )

 See also: Floppy version
_source.modernAnalog: A self-contained interactive experience I can play on my phone and keep in my own library instead of a game platform.

_source.title: Caper in the Castro (aka Murder on Main Street)
_source.creator: CM Ralph
_source.description:
Caper in the Castro is the first known LGBT video game. The player assumes the role of a lesbian detective investigating the disappearance of a transgender woman in the Castro neighborhood of San Francisco.

 The game was distributed by BBS, with donations requested "to an AIDS-related charity of your choice for whatever amount you feel is appropriate". It was also adapted for mainstream audiences as Murder on Main Street , which was commercially published by Heizer Software. Both versions are included in this download.

 In 2014, the creator was interviewed by Paste Magazine .

input: Build me an interactive experience that myst is an immersive, first-person adventure game of the point-and-click type. it features high-quality graphics and sound and a variety of puzzles and challenges. This is just for me — completely private.
hint: Generate a Plaited-native game-simulation using MSS tags contentType=play-community, structure=collection, scale=S3. Expected exposure: internal-only. Use controller protocol, not generic web-app framing.

_source.title: Myst
_source.creator: Cyan, Inc.
_source.description:
MYST is an immersive, first-person adventure game of the point-and-click type. It features high-quality graphics and sound and a variety of puzzles and challenges. The player is, essentially, a stranger dropped into an unfamiliar world in which clues, writings and functional-but-disabled machines and devices point the way to realizing the storyline. The game presents the player with non-linear gameplay (you can wander around at your whim), puzzles and dilemmas requiring both logical and intuitive thought and presents the player with ethical choices that affect the outcome of the game. The best selling computer adventure game for over 9 years (1993 to 2002), it s a still a thoroughly enjoyable experience a quarter of a century later.

 This is one of the first (and best in its day) of the point-and-click adventure games. It established a new genre of adenture games for computers and set a standard for interactivity that has spawned both sequels and many imitators (called myst-clones"). It was a commercial success from the day it went on sale and was the best-selling adventure-genre computer game from its release in 1993 until 2002 when it was overtaken, in sales, by The Sims. It was also the first game to fully exploit CD-ROM technology and established techniques for effectively managing game content on CD that would be used in thousands of games from then on. It was also pioneering in that it allowed the player to wander freely (non-linear gameplay) and presented the user with ethical choices which created alternative endings to the game. It was also the first to incorporate high-quality graphics with ambient sounds and effects, creating the most immersive environment possible at the time. This game set a pattern for many similar games which followed; that of a player who is essentially a stranger, dropped into a game world, who is presented with puzzles and problems whose solutions are integral to the unraveling of the storyline. Gameplay was remarkably smooth, for that time, as CD-ROM speeds were quite limited. At that time, CD-ROM drives were not a standard system amenity; they were expensive and slow and were just emerging as an important system component. MYST is widely credited with stimulating the sales of CD-ROM drives. MYST started out as a set of HyperCard stacks linked together with commands activated by hot zones in the cards. It was developed on Mac Quadras. It was also one of the first games (If not THE first) to have music engaging enough to be commercially-released on CD as a soundtrack.

 Since MYST was produced at a time when system resources were quite modest in comparison with our own day and is accordingly sparing in its use of resources, it can play smoothly on any system that can run the game. Graphics are rich in quality and gameplay is straightforward. Clues and game elements are scattered throughout the gameworld, so a notebook and pencil are handy during gameplay. The game has a 'zip' mode for fast travel between discovered locations to prevent tedium, as the player may visit the same location many times. The puzzles are clever and engaging, involving both logic and intutive thought. It is, of course, primitive compared to games of the present day but a very enjoyable experience, even now.
_source.modernAnalog: A self-contained interactive experience I can play on my phone and keep in my own library instead of a game platform.

_source.title: The Manhole (HyperCard)
_source.creator: Rand and Robyn Miller
_source.description:
Like Alice in Wonderland , you are a child jumping into a magical world filled with fantastical places and peculiar characters. The world can be navigated screen by screen, by clicking the mouse where you want to go. Many objects on each screen can be clicked to find hidden animations and sounds. Exploring the completely open world, you can listen to and have educational conversations with Mr. Rabbit, Mr. Dragon, and others.

 The Manhole is aimed at children, and focuses on exploration and experimentation. There is no progress to be made, no goal to be reached, and the player can not fail. The entire world is accessible from the start.

 The original Macintosh version consists of six black-and-white stacks for the Macintosh's HyperCard software. Ports to other systems added color graphics. Also, the Macintosh floppy and CD versions are the same except for the cd version adding audio enhancements like voice-overs for the dialogue. This enhancement should not be confused with the remake of this game titled The Manhole: CD-ROM Masterpiece Edition .

 - ( MobyGames )

_source.title: The Ultimate Warrior
_source.creator: Erick Ritchie
_source.description:
The Ultimate Warrior is a HyperCard Dungeon Crawler Game developped by some college student (at that time) named Erick Ritchie, apparently.

It portrays a simple yet addictive RPG where most of the gamplay is about clicking on arrows and pushing the enter key to progress the text (though you can also play the game entirely by mouse clicking, it's just less tedious using enter). I actually have no clue what the story is about, as I have a natural tendency to just spam the enter key to skip through the introduction.

I played this game for the first time in 1996, and it stayed on my mind rent free ever since, so it shouldn't be too bad for a teenager project.

A full playthrough of the demo is actually quite simple (yeah, unfortunately this is just a demo, and my efforts to contact that Erick guy were in vain, as it seems like the mail adress he left in the files to provide him feedback in 95' doesn't work anymore 30 years later, for some reason).

 
 Select a class for your character. Either Babarian, Ninja or Paladin. The two last classes have the advantage to let you cast spells, such as Fireball for the Ninja. 
 Smash that enter key to skip through the story. 
 Go to the temple, say hi, and skip the text. The temple is where you heal and save (note that saving quits the game). 
 Go to the shop, and buy the first weapon useable for your class. Equip that weapon (the name of the weapon must be shown in the character's stats on the left of the screen, in the bottom, or it's not actively equipped). 
 Go back to the town and head to the tunnels. Indeed, you can go to the tavern, but aside from a guy that tells you to go to the tunnels, and the bartender offering you free beverages to get you drunk (which doesn't work though, your character is too much of a badass unfortunately), there isn't anything to do. 
 If you decided to buy a weapon an have it equiped, skip to 7. If you forgot to equip it or just skipped 4, perish on your first monster. You shouldn't have gone bare handed. You get ressurected with 1 HP. Go to the temple and heal before going back. 
 Travel through the dungeon and have fun. 
 

 The game is heavily based on RNG, and any hit, either from the character or a mob can range anywhere from 1 to 200 damage. In other words, while the game is not hard, it is very easy to die miserably on a weak enemy, even with the best gear, just because of bad luck. Just avoid getting there bare handed, and you will mostly be fine.

 The demo dungeon only provides two levels, which will roughtly take you around 2 hours, and when you beat the boss at the end of the second level, all the monsters just stop spawning. You just got your hands on a pretty great treasure and potions, but all of this is useless, since you won't encounter a single monster in this playthrough anymore. Why? Good question. I told you I spent my time skipping the story so, I don't know.

 The only thing you can do now is either start another playthrough with another class, or delete the demo. I would've adored a full game out of this, but I've yet to find the Erick guy. I'll keep you posted.
_source.modernAnalog: A self-contained interactive experience I can play on my phone and keep in my own library instead of a game platform.

_source.creator: Unknown
_source.description:
K'kai Adventure is a game in the form of a Hypercard stack, credited to a class of students at the Kaunakakai School in Hawai'i. The game is a simple point-and-click adventure set off the Hawai'ian coast, beginning with a simple crab dive and leading to exploring a mysterious underwater castle full of treasures and perils.

 The game is short and has cute scenes and a silly sense of humor, with branching paths leading to interesting bad ends, usually in the form of a cruel and unusual death. The short length makes exploring and finding all of them manageable, though, so even finding every room and way to perish won't take too long.

_source.title: Spelunx and the Caves of Mr. Seudo (B&W)
_source.creator: Cyan
_source.description:
If you've never played Yodel Toasters or climbed a Pigtoad tree, it's high lime you tried Spelunx and the Caves of Mr. Seudo, a Brøderbund release for — yes, it's true — kids of all ages.

 Spelunx is the latest production from the Cyan team of Robyn and Rand Miller, who created The Manhole and Cosmic Osmo, Like their earlier efforts, Spelunx is not a game but rather a "noncompetitive exploration environment." There are no points to score, no levels to attain, and no ways to “die" The whole idea is to explore and to learn along the way.

 Kids can play with Spelunx and not realize they’re learning about biology, astronomy, music, chemistry, and so forth, because they're busy having fun. They can mutate wacky trees by splicing genes with the Tree Gene-orator, for example, or they can use Cartesian coordinates to find planets with the P. Seudo Scope and make them spin.

 All these activities occur in Prof. Alexander Spelunx's caverns, a set of rooms and tunnels filled with references to famous children's books as well as to more-obscure items (would you believe a MacWEEK mug?). You can click on virtually everything in the caves, with highly unpredictable results.

 Like previous Cyan productions, Spelunx is HyperCard-based but with a welcome new twist: MacroMind Director Files are integrated within the stack. By combining black-and-white HyperCard graphics with colorful Director inserts, the Millers have created an unusual look and kept the space requirements for the program relatively modest; Spelunx fits on five 800K disks, rather than requiring a CD-ROM. And because the color portions are limited, you can still run Spelunx effectively on a Mac with a black-and-white monitor.

 Although the program's resource needs are carefully contained, its entertainment capabilities are boundless. There's even a secret room with an ingenious entrance that lets you keep modifying the caves with new tunnels and rooms of your own — making Spelunx a game that grows hut doesn't grow old.

 Gruberman, Ken. (November 1992). Spelunx and the Caves of Mr. Seudo. MacUser . (pg. 85).
_source.modernAnalog: A self-contained interactive experience I can play on my phone and keep in my own library instead of a game platform.

_source.title: Flowers of Crystal
_source.creator: Gumboot Software
_source.description:
Flowers of Crystal is an educational game from the author of Granny's Garden. The game was designed to provide for creative activities in the classroom and is built around puzzle-solving. It consists of three parts: an introductory story and an adventure in two parts. A password from the first part is required to start the second. The introductory story is also included recorded on an audio tape as well as printed in a booklet.

 The game takes place on the planet Crystal which once had vast expanses of colourful flowers that are now thought to be extinct. The player is a visitor from Earth who explores the planet in an attempt to find the last remaining flower. In the first part, the player has to find four objects (special water, gold pot, fertiliser and soil). The gameplay involves travelling between seven locations (centre, city, factory, forest, lake, desert and mountains) and choosing which items to carry. The game starts out at the centre where the player chooses four items from three different menus. Items are needed when visiting some of the locations or give some protection when encountering hazards or some of the world's hostile creatures. The second part involves finding six parts of a flower dream to recreate a complete flower picture and then go to a castle. To progress it is necessary to obtain spells and use them at the right location.
_source.modernAnalog: A self-contained interactive experience I can play on my phone and keep in my own library instead of a game platform.

_source.title: Blah Blob!
_source.creator: Brian Kendall
_source.description:
Blah Blob! is a short platformer game made in HyperCard.

 You control the titular Blah Blob as you bounce through twenty levels of increasingly devious obstacles.

 Play the whole game straight through trying your best not to explode, or speedrun the levels individually or even the whole game and try to beat your best time. Perhaps even have some fun while doing so! (Please note the Blah Blob developer disavows all responsibility for excessive fun or frustration experienced as a direct result of or in conjunction with playing the game.)

 A Bit About The Backstory of Blah Blob 

 What is HyperCard, you ask? Why it's an old and beloved app (or "application" to use 1980's Macintosh lingo) that made it fun and easy to create your own software. It was developed by Apple Computers for early Macintosh computers and originally released in 1987, and there are still many who fondly remember it to this day.

 The author of Blah Blob learned HyperCard as a child because "he wanted to make his own Super Mario" and gradually learned how to push it to its very limits. Now, thirty years later, he has returned to HyperCard to reconnect with those earlier days, have some fun making a game again, and seeing what he can manage to get it to do now that he's not constrained by such trifling obstacles as "slow processor speeds" or "software that wasn't meant to do this sort of thing".

 Blah Blob is more than just an anachronism. There is no point at which it made sense to release it.

 It could have been made back in 1990 when HyperCard 2.0 was released, but on any Macintosh at the time it would have run at an unplayable speed of around 2 frames per second.

 In 1999 it could run great on a PowerMac with a G4 processor, but by that point most would have considered it too late to make a game with purely monochrome graphics — HyperCard never had full support for color — on computers that routinely displayed millions of colors, or contemporaneous with game consoles that had full 3D graphics.

 Perhaps it makes the most sense that it was made and released in 2023, roughly twenty five years after HyperCard sadly lost its relevance in the world, at a time when some might appreciate its nostalgic qualities.

 Modern macOS, Windows, and Linux builds of the game are available here: https://bribrikendall.itch.io/blah-blob
_source.modernAnalog: A self-contained interactive experience I can play on my phone and keep in my own library instead of a game platform.

_source.title: Bomber
_source.creator: Rene Vidmer
_source.description:
Bomber is an exciting HyperCard-based game that makes excellent use of HyperCard's graphics and sound abilities. The sounds provide both background ambience and important information. The publisher sensibly includes a set of head-phones so that you can play without disturbing others. The action involves flying a B-17 bomber on raids over Europe during World War II. New pilots are given easy missions; veterans draw challenging long-range raids. Luck plays a part in the outcome, but skill usually prevails. The controls are easy to learn, although some of the cockpit controls are small and somewhat difficult to find when you're fighting off a determined attack — that only adds to the realism.

 Bobker, Steve. (February 1990). Bobker's Dozen. MacUser . (pg. 52).
_source.modernAnalog: A self-contained interactive experience I can play on my phone and keep in my own library instead of a game platform.

_source.title: The Manhole CD-ROM (1989 Activision release)
_source.creator: Cyan
_source.description:
This is the original CD-ROM release of the Manhole (black and white version), published by Activision in 1989. It is identical to the 1988 floppy disk release except for the addition of a musical score, more sound effects and full audio voiceovers for all dialogue.

 The CD has an HFS data partition with the entire game (including the soundtrack and voice audio) contained inside multiple HyperCard stacks. The game therefore runs entirely from the data track of the CD.

 Also included is the full musical score for the game in 16 CD audio tracks which are playable through a sound system or computer-based CD audio player.

 See also: The Manhole CD-ROM Masterpiece Edition

_source.title: BeeHive ADB I/O
_source.creator: Motion Control AB
_source.description:
With ADB I/O and HyperCard, SuperCard, AppleScript, Director or 4th Dimension, it is easy to build customized applications for your control and data acquisition needs. With the example code included in the package you can start controlling and logging data within minutes!

 Functional Features 

 
 Plugs into the Apple Desktop Bus port on the Macintosh. 
 4 ADB I/O units can be connected to one Macintosh. 
 No external power supply needed. 
 4 relay outputs. 
 The relay outputs have a timer function that can be optionally set to, 0 to 25.5 seconds. 
 4 data acquisition channels. Each channel can be configured as an analog input (8 bits resolution), a digital input or a digital output. 
 Maximum sample rate: 90 samples per second.

 4 channels per sample. Max 12 samples per second recommended for smooth simultaneous mouse operation. 
 

 - ( Bzzzzzz.com )

GNC HyperTour 2.1 is a Macintosh HyperCard 2.x tour of my model railroad, the Great Northern Cascade Railway. The GNC is an N-scale layout measuring 11.5 x 14.5 feet overall, and has a 75.5 foot mainline, approximately 2-1/4 scale miles. It represents a freelanced version of the Great Northern's Wenatchee-Oroville branch in North Central Washington state. This file is a StuffIt archive and AOL software should unstuff it for you automatically. It unloads to a 213K HyperCard stack, which requires a Macintosh running HyperCard 2.x. For those not able to view this stack, please keep your eyes open for an updated GIF file, GNC Today.GIF, which presents a simplified trackplan of the GNC. This HyperTour is the _third_ version of the tour I have posted here. This stack has been checked for Mac and HyperCard viruses with Virex 5.6

_source.title: Warp Speed Calculator v1.1 .sit
_source.creator: Peter L. Gingrich II
_source.description: ----------------- Version 1.1 has been updated to calculate either for the original Enterprise (NCC-1701) or the newest Enterprise (NCC-1701-D) using UltraDrive Warp engines. Select the Star Ship; put in the distance (select either Light-years or Parsecs); then click on the desired warp speed. The travel time will then be computed and displayed. Have you ever wondered if Captain Kirk or Captain Picard knows what they are talking about when they quote a certain distance at a certain speed can be covered in a set amount of time????? Example: 'Number One, that's 23 light-years away! Why, that will take 3.5 days at Warp 9!' Well, wonder no more! With the Warp Speed stack you can compute the actual time to cross a given distance at a given speed using the official warp speed factors given in 'The Making of Start Trek' for the old Enterprise (NCC-1701) or the NEW warp speed factors for the new Enterprise (NCC-1701-D). This stack is freeware. Feel free to copy and distribute this stack for non-commercial purposes.

_source.title: Too Much Joy
_source.creator: Clive Young
_source.description: This stack is all about that great 'alternative' rock band you all know and love, Too Much Joy. Celebrated for its crunching power pop-rock riffs and clever, intelligent lyrics, TMJ (as its fans refer to the band) has received rave reviews in Rolling Stone, Spin, Billboard, Entertainment Weekly, People, The New York Times, The Village Voice, Tower Records' Pulse, Request, The Hard Report, CashBox and a ton of other magazines. In fact, CashBox named them one of the top bands of the '90s. Clearly, if you haven't heard of TMJ before, now is the time to catch on to them quick! And this stack can help you do that.... The stack contains a detailed history of the band, (including lots of dirt on its legal problems with Bozo The Clown and being arrested for protesting censorship). There is also an annotated discography, song analysis, soundbites, over 100 pop-up fields, information on how to join JoyBuzzer, TMJ's official fan club, and lots more! This stack is the 'Clean Version,' meaning that some of the -ahem- 'language' has been cleaned up in order to meet AOL's standards; also a soundbite from the rarity, 'Rap Like Mine,' has been removed (a move which saved 51K, incidentally, making this a shorter download). Otherwise, the stack is the same as 'regular' version. This stack is Freeware; it is also Joinware-if you like it, do yourself a favor and join JoyBuzzer (you'll get an fan-club-only, blue-vinyl EP of unreleased TMJ songs and four issues of JoyBuzzer Fanzine, which has gotten rave reviews of its own in Tower Records' Pulse, Factsheet Five and Yellow Pills). Like I said, information on how to join is in the stack. Smash a glass, and en-Joy the stack- Clive Young JoyBuzzer
