_source.description: Cassette Labeler is a stack which prints out labels to replace the 'J' cards in the plastic cases used to hold cassettes. For people with poor handwriting (like myself), it makes the album and song titles neater, and a lot easier to read. This stack contains templates for use with a 'classic' full size cassette case, and the newer 'slimline' cassette cases. Each label has space for both sides of the cassette, including artist, album titles, and song titles. Whatever type of information you wish to include is flexible. The font and size of the text can also be customized. The labels can be printed as a whole stack or individually, and can be sorted and searched for specific text.

What's New on Version 4.7 ------------------------ The following changes appear on SIMM Stack 4.7: * Added new Power Macintosh and PowerBook products. * Corrected LC550 VRAM to 1 MB, not 768k. * Corrected IIvx VRAM configuration depending upon CD ROM installation from Apple. * Corrected maximum supported PowerBook 500 series RAM expansion module. * Corrected Power Macintosh 5200 memory speed from 70 ns to 80 ns. * Added support for 1 and 2 MB SIMMs to the Macintosh 630 family. * Merryxmas virus prevention added.

_source.title: Personal Directory 2.0.1
_source.creator: Tom Rydberg
_source.description: Personal Directory 2.0.1 is a HyperCard stack that provides you with a flexible way to access, organize, and print your personal database of names, addresses, phone numbers, and URLs. It automatically sorts single and multiple name entries, tracks birthdays and anniversaries, and supports drag and drop of information between fields. Use this stack to print mailing labels and desk-size organizer sheets. You can also categorize your entries using one or more categories, copy or print entries based on categories or events, and enter free text notes. Navigation through the stack is easy, too: simply click on letter buttons to access entries via pop-up lists, and use the left and right arrow keys or buttons to move ahead or back one card. If you've got scanned pictures of family and friends, you can use this stack as an online photo album. In addition, Personal Directory automatically formats phone numbers and can autodial them for you. Feel free to try out the demo version of this stack. The demo version is fully functional, but limits you to a maximum of 10 cards. The 10 card restriction can be lifted by registering your Personal Directory and paying the $15 shareware fee. If you have questions or comments, please e-mail me at TomRydberg@aol.com. Thanks for trying it out! ------------------ Scanned for viruses with Disinfectant 3.7.1 and merryXmasVacine.

A user supplied the idea for and part of the implementation of the astrological reading. 3. The journal is new altogether. If it is too large ( >30,000 characters), it will be saved as a text file outside the stack before being overwritten. 4. _source.title: Movies 6.1
_source.creator: Craig Good
_source.description: A HyperCard Home Video Organizer This stack was started a couple of years ago by Andrew Stanton at Pixar. He wanted to be able to catalog and organize his laserdiscs. I started with his basic stack and learned HyperHacking while I added some features. We and some of our friends at Pixar have been using it for over a year (you'll note the Feb 1993 date on the release) and it's been quite useful. Features at a glance: * Keep track of all of your discs and tapes. * Remembers to whom you lent out that video, and when. * Lets you search by criteria such as Director, running time, or by features such as letterboxing, supplements, etc. * Calculates the total value of your collection for insurance purposes. The stack comes with one bogus card which is not a movie, but a set of instructions and examples. Detailed help is available by pulling down the Movies menu and selecting Preferences. There you will find a Help icon. The Movies Stack is c Craig Good. It is FreeWare, but is not in the public domain. It may be freely distributed as long as it is intact and unmodified. If you like it, or if you have suggestions, contact me at good@pixar.com or NM8N GUY (America On Line)

_source.title: Recipe Maker 1.0s demo.sea
_source.creator: Patrick Garrett
_source.description: This is a program that I wrote for my step-mom, who loves recipes, to keep up with them in a database program with HyperCard. This program allows you to store as many recipes as you may need, print them, absolutely do whatever you want to do with them. As a cheap shareware program, costing only $5, it is cheap databasing, without too much effort. It has 4 main fields in it along with an easy interface. It begins with a stack that gives instructions on usage and buying and registering. The registered version will allow you to make new recipe entries. The demo contains one entry that you can use if you want to. It is an example of how it shall work. The first field - Food name. The description of the target food. The second - Ingredients. This is where you put the necessary ingredients. The third - Method. How you use the ingredients. The fourth - Tools. This describes what you will need (crock pot, oven, etc.) If you register the program, you will be sent a cardboard disk mailer with a high-density disk that contains 3 files: a readme, a .sea file for HyperCard 2.x users, and a stack for non-HyperCard users. If you want 2 800k floppies, please specify this when you purchase. Please check it out. Checked for Mac & HC viruses with Virex 5.6.

_source.title: Meal Time 2.3s Recipe Stack
_source.creator: Chris Hostetter
_source.description: ----------------- Meal Time is a HyperCard recipe program which allows you to store your recipes for easy retrieval. It can re-calculate your recipe ingredients if you want to serve fewer or more people than your recipe calls for. You can move recipes to a Shopping List, and/or Guest List. If you are not sure what recipe you want to view, you can generate a list of recipes containing selected Key Words. Eight slots exist for users to add their own Key Words, and users may change existing Key Words. Conditional AND/OR supported. You may even enter one or more ingredients to generate a list of recipes which contain those ingredients, if you desire to find recipes which use food you currently have in your cupboards. Includes a variety of print options. Export your recipes and share them with someone else. Import new recipes that others have exported. Use free for 3 months.

_source.title: LinksBase 1.1
_source.creator: Adam Rice
_source.description: A HyperCard database for web directories. LinksBase allows you to maintain a database of links and HTML code templates, and build specialized pages out of them automatically. It is really only meant for building pages that are, essentially, lists of links, like Yahoo! for example (although it would be utterly innapropriate for Yahoo! itself): it doesn't have much to offer someone posting poetry to the Web. I designed LinksBase mostly for my own benefit, and the cheesy interface should be a dead giveaway that I wasn't thinking too hard about commercial potential. This stack will be of most use to someone who is reasonably comfortable with both HTML and HyperTalk: most people will not want to use the templates I have provided, and in fact, to get the most out of it, you may want to add a few fields, which will necessitate adding some new code. The stack comprises four main parts: the Detail cards, which is where you keep information on individual links, the Categories cards, which is where you set up a page-level and subcategory-level hierarchy, the HTML Code cards, where you insert chunks of HTML code to be used as templates, and the Build card, where you make it all happen.
_source.modernAnalog: A private reference organizer on my phone that keeps everything in one place and lets me share only when I choose.

_source.title: MouseUp 1.5
_source.creator: Mike Stockelman
_source.description: MouseUp is a Hypercard stack to manage data for a transgenic mouse colony. Its particular purpose is to generate an index file of the individual mice in the colony. Each mouse has an index card listing the mouse's ID, sex, birthdate, genotype, parents, and offspring. Also there is a field for entering any text as notes. Other features include ability to search the database for any entry in any field, and automatic updating of the offspring field of parent mice when new information is entered. But the coolest thing is that the mouse database is a hypertext family tree. In other words, there is a link from any mouse's card to the cards of its parents and its offspring. Just point and click the mother listed on a mouse's card (for example) and jump immediately to the card of the mother mouse. Then click the mouse you just came from, listed under offspring, and go back.
_source.modernAnalog: A private science organizer on my phone that keeps everything in one place and lets me share only when I choose.

_source.title: MacChef 2.0.3
_source.creator: Robert Boston
_source.description: I first wrote MacChef in 1995, after becoming frustrated with the limitations of a recipe program I had downloaded a few weeks earlier. Oh, the program worked all right, but it worked the way the writer wanted, not the way I wanted. If I rarely bake bread or pastry, why do I need a Bread & Pastry category of recipes? On the other hand, if I make a lot of pasta, why can't I add a Pasta category? MacChef allows me (and you) to do that kind of thing. I also wanted to make importing text files as easy as possible with a minimum of editing and reformatting. That gives MacChef a plain interface, but it saves time and effort when downloading recipes from the Web and the rec.food.recipes newsgroup, the source of many recipes in this collection. In the four years since I wrote the original MacChef, the number of recipes has grown to more than 500. I haven't tried all of them, but they all looked very promising when I downloaded them from rec.food.recipes and other sources. In each case, I've given credit to the original developer of the recipe if I knew who that person was. Many of the recipes include comments by the person who wrote the recipe. My own comments are attributed to "Bob." And you'll see several recipes I've developed myself. As a cardiac patient, I usually try to come up with recipes that are low in fat and sodium while still tasting great. (See my Low Fat Manicotti for example.)

_source.title: HyperDA
_source.creator: Bill Appleton
_source.description:
To hear everyone tell it, HyperCard is going to change the world. So who am I to disagree? After all, to be colloquial about it, it’s one hell of a product. But how do you reach all that information when you’re in a word processor or spreadsheet? Sure, there’s MultiFinder, but the memory requirements will soon send you shopping for SIMMs. [Editor’s Note: Version 1.0 of HyperDA is MultiFinder-hostile, but Symmetry says that has been fixed with version 1.01 and higher.]

 Enter HyperDA, a desk accessory from Symmetry Corporation that lets you open and browse HyperCard stacks from within another application or from the Finder, even with 512K Macs.

 Call HyperDA from the Apple menu, choose a stack from a standard Open dialog, and you’re in. The stack appears and runs just as it would under HyperCard’s Browse tool. All stack links remain operational, and you can navigate through the information in normal HyperCard fashion. Several of HyperCard s Command-key sequences, including peek at buttons (Command-Option) and peek at FIELDS (Command-Shift-Option) are supported. If a button triggers an action that HyperDA doesn’t support, clicking on it will produce no response.

 HyperDA also lets you search stacks with the find command. You type a character string into the message box and comb your stack for references. Or you can type in HyperTalk commands (HyperDA supports a subset of rhe HyperTalk language) just as in HyperCard. HyperDA also lets you copy graphics or text to the Clipboard and paste it into an application document. The Option key turns the Pointing Hand cursor into a cross hair for deploying a selection rectangle. Move the hand over text dements, and it changes to an I-beam cursor for selecting blocks of text. Need hard copy? You can print Individual Cards directly from HyperDA.

 In the process of making stacks more accessible, HyperDA adds some things that HyperCard left out. First of all, the menu bar is accessible at all times. HyperDA can also open a stack into a resizable window. Command-W toggles from Card to Window mode, which facilitates cutting and pasting operations.

 Stacks behave differently depending on the mode of display. In HyperDA's Card mode, stacks are always active. Click on a button and its function (if possible) is executed. And, even when hidden behind an active application window, the DA intercepts Command-key sequences. So, be aware of possible conflicts, such as COMMAND-F (FIND) and COMMAND-P (print). When a stack is displayed in Window mode, however, the window must be activated before HyperDA commands will work or the stack can be browsed.

 The manual is short, well written, and to the point. Everything you need to use the program is there. If you prefer, keep the stack version online and refer to it with the DA itself. Included is an appendix listing the HyperTalk commands supported by HyperDA. Other technical notes are available from Symmetry as well.

 Of course, HyperDA is not HyperCard. It does not have all the original's features nor its functionality. You cannot modify stacks. Most of HyperCard's visual effects are not supported. Neither can you launch programs or execute functions such as automatic dialing. But then replacing HyperCard is not HyperDA's purpose. Its business is the retrieval of information, such as your appointment schedule or address book, from stacks without having to leave your application. HyperDA is, if you will, a link to HyperCard.

 I tested HyperDA with a variety of popular programs and encountered no difficulties. HyperCard aficionados should find this DA extremely useful. Because it works on 512K machines (it's shipped on an 800K disk, so a double-sided drive is necessary), HyperDA is especially valuable to those Mac users who have not yet upgraded their machine’s memory to the minimum 1 megabyte that HyperCard requires. HyperDA gives these users access to the ever-growing stacks of stackware without having to use HyperCard at all.

 Martinez, Carlos Domingo. (May 1988). HyperDA. MacUser . (pgs. 110, 112).

_source.title: Library Stack 1.2
_source.creator: Paddy O'Connor
_source.description: ----------------- Library Stack 1.2 is a disk cataloger. It will read the Volume Name, File Name, File Type, and File Size from each file on a floppy disk into the proper field in the stack. It has two additional fields for entering Key Words and a Description about the file(s). You can search for a file by its File Name, its File Type, or by a Key Word. You can also search for a disk by its name. It is much improved over version 1.1. It will now put files in a folder onto a single card, regardless of the files' names. It files the cards in alphabetical order by File Name. When you search for a file, you can use more than one word. If you do not exactly remember the name of the file you are looking for, it will find what you type within a longer string of words. When you leave the stack, if the free space is > 10K, it tells you the amount of disk space needed to compact the stack, the amount of disk space available, and lets you decide if you want to do it or not. Shareware. $10.
_source.modernAnalog: A focused maker utility that helps me automate or inspect one technical task without needing a full desktop toolkit.

_source.title: STEROIDS lite
_source.creator: W.A. Hoffman III
_source.description: STEROIDS lite is a 50 structure subset of STEROIDS, a HyperCard stack of more than 650 steroid structures, including anabolics, catabolics, cardenolides, bufadienolides (frog toxins), estrogens and other female and male hormones, steroidal vitamins (D-series), anti-inflammatories, and many more. STEROIDS is designed as a structural reference work for organic, medicinal and biochemists, high school and college instructors and students, as well as others with an interest in an important class of biologically active compounds. STEROIDS can be used as a teaching aid, and can be readily modified for use as the core of a broad information database. STEROIDS lite is a sampler of the larger work, demonstrating its organization and diversity. STEROIDS lite is Copyright 1995, William A. Hoffman III. All rights are reserved. _source.title: The Complete Works of Mozart ...
_source.creator: Kevin Harris
_source.description: The Complete Works of Mozart 1.1 The Complete Works of Mozart is a set of four stacks which contain the entire Kochel catalog online. The Kochel catalog is the listing of Mozart's works as compiled by Ludwig von Kochel in 1869. This stack set contains complete information on the composition, also where and when it was written. This set also includes: - Works that were recently discovered and have not yet been assigned to the catalog. - Works that are still in question, as to whether or not they were indeed works of Mozart, or where they fit into the catalog numbering scheme. - Complete search capabilities. A must for ANY Mozart fan! Shareware, $5. Copyright 1993 Software Perspectives. All Rights Reserved.

_source.creator: Arthur V. Dieli
_source.description: 7/10/95, Global Maps v2.0 This freeware stack lets you explore global views of the continents and oceans. The global maps were copied from the Microsoft Encarta Multimedia Encyclopedia, 1994, with permission from Microsoft for non-commercial use. The names on the maps were converted to hypertext so that they react to a mouse click within HyperCard. The title card text was created with Aldus Type Twister. This version includes a Quiz which selects random questions drawn from the text fields in the stack. The stack was designed and implemented by Arthur V. Dieli. Please send your comments and suggestions to DieliAV@eWorld.com or by using the registration form that is provided. This revision, version 2.0 includes these additions: * a menu to provide alternative navigation * a Quiz generator that builds a ten question true/false quiz * additional political subdivision maps that are accessed from the continental maps * text fields attached to each global map with factual information drawn from Microsoft's Encarta, Grolier's Multimedia Encyclopedia, and The World Almanac. * a registration form * a help field that can be reached from the menu.

_source.title: TOXIX 2.01 Demo
_source.creator: Paul Caluwe
_source.description: TOXIX provides information on toxic properties for nearly a thousand chemical substances. Each compound is characterized by name, structural formula (i.e. chemical structure), CAS (Chemical Abstracts Service) registry number, and by the type of functional groups present. Toxic properties and OSHA air exposure limits - when established - are given for each chemical substance. Hazardous properties were obtained from catalogs of chemical suppliers and from product labels and were placed in eleven different categories : known carcinogen, suspect carcinogen, teratogen, mutagen, highly toxic, toxic, irritant, lachrymator, corrosive, oxidizer, no significant hazardous properties. Exposure limits for air contaminants -current as of October 1993 - are based on the OSHA Safety and Health Standards [1900.1000 : 7-1-91 Edition].

_source.title: PrefsCleaner 1.0.1
_source.creator: Angelo Scicolone
_source.description: PrefsCleaner is a HyperCard stack which helps you to manage your preferences folder. In fact, our Preferences folders are often full of useless preferences files, often created by demo programs or applications used a time only or deleted. Each of these creates his own preferences file in the Preferences folder. At end, our Prefs Folder will be full of useless prefs files, which only occupy disk space and make the MacOS and other applications unstable. PrefsCleaner scans your preferences folder for files which are probably useless, then you can decide which of these you want to delete.

_source.title: HCSC 052295 Text-to-Speech
_source.creator: Mark Klink
_source.description: The enclosed stacks and documents were part of a package that was issued in connection with the 5/22/95 America Online HyperCard Forum Scripting Conference. The HyperCard Scripting Conference is simply an ongoing series of online meetings in the AOL HyperCard conference area (Shortcut: MHC), usually held on Monday evenings. Some of these meetings have formal topics with guest presenters, others are informal get togethers where HyperCard scripting enthusiasts can ask and answer questions on any topic as well as share scripts and tips. Anyone with an AOL account can attend. The subject of the 5/22/95 conference was HyperCard and the text-to-speech capabilities provided by the Speech Manager Extension. Although America Online provides the venue for the conferences, it is in no way responsible for them and does not, in any way, own or endorse the contents of the discussions or the associated stacks. Since the conference there have been a number of changes to the Speech Manager. As of this writing, we are up to version 1.4.1. I have not had a chance to do a comprehensive check of the behavior of the latest versions, so I can't guarantee that the notes about bugs and quirks remain accurate. However, the scripting suggestions and the guide to the use of inline commands and the phonemic symbols should remain generally useful. There are some important differences between the versions of the Speech Manager that were available when the stack was created and the latest version. First of all, it is now possible to set the default voice using the Speech control panel. When the stack was created, the default was restricted to the voice "Fred". The examples in the stack will probably work better if you set the default to "Fred". Since there is no longer a built in default voice or a built in synthesizer, you must, contrary to the information in the stack, have at least one voice installed in the Voices folder in the Extensions folder as well as a synthesizer for text-to-speech to work. Another important difference is that there are now three Speech synthesizers available, Macintalk II, Macintalk III, and Macintalk Pro. Few HyperCard scripters realize that is is possible to embed special commands into text that is to be spoken. These commands allow one to have much greater control over how the synthesizer "speaks" the text. Perhaps the greatest value of this stack is that it documents this feature. Before closing this note, I would especially like to thank to Jeff Crossley, who organized the HyperCard Scripting Conference, and who authored the template for the Script Conference demo stacks. Mark Klink
_source.modernAnalog: A focused maker utility that helps me automate or inspect one technical task without needing a full desktop toolkit.

_source.title: Bibliophile 2.0
_source.creator: Brian Sullivan
_source.description: Bibliophile is a book collector's stack designed for the needs of all libraries, from the casual collector to small book shops. It features the standard functions common to most library stacks (sorting, keyword/phrase searches, etc.) In addition, it also contains more powerful features, such as the ability to keep track of dealer addresses, total inventory costs, and most importantly of all, export stack information in the form of pre-formatted bibiliography or catalog text files which can be read by most word-processing applications. Card information is formatted for those users who have an eye towards the professional or academic market, but is simple enough to be used with the smallest libraries. Its ability to export information makes it useful as a journal citation stack as well. For those of you who have downloaded an earlier version of this stack, the changes contained in this update are very minor. The stack contains an example card which can be deleted when you are tired of looking at it. I have also included an example bibliography and book catalog text file that was made from information on this card. Hope you enjoy this stack. Good book hunting!

_source.title: Culture Interchange Square: Nigiri-zushi (Japan) 1.2
_source.creator: Fuminori Takeda
_source.description: What is "CULTURE INTERCHANGE" Square? Sushi is a typical food in Japanese culture. Although I'm not a professional in the sushi world, I've had sushi in my home and sushi bars. As an typical Japanese native, I decided to introduce this field of my homeland on the Internet as a program. The reason why is printed below. (I had to study this field for content and discovered I knew little of our culture.) If the people of the world could introduce/exchange/learn cultural things to/from others, it's a great thing, I think. Of course, such a thing is possible to do via home pages on the Internet combined with the powerful search engines that have been developed. However, if there were a directory (folder) that controlled by minimum rules, a world wide archive, it would be more useful and helpful for viewers. Much like an encyclopedia. Therefore I named this program's subject line a title that conforms to the following; "the title of my product (My country, province or state) - CULTURE INTERCHANGE Square ." If you're making culture introductory/interchange programs to post on the InfoMac archives, would you name your program's subject line that conforms to the " title (your country, province, or state) - CULTURE INTERCHANGE Square" rule? As the proposer, I had to establish the last notes "CULTURE INTERCHANGE Square." It was absolutely necessary to give it a proper first name for obvious reasons. Fuminori Takeda

_source.description: Key words: Font Catalog Index Collection If the beautiful printouts of The TypeBook (the Cadillac of font cataloging utilities as far as I can tell) are more than you really need, but you would still like to see your fonts both on screen and on paper, then FontIndexer may be just the ticket. This is a plain vanilla stack (no colors or fancy graphics), but it is both useful and easy to use. Care has been taken to make all of the operations clear from the first time you open the stack. If you use a utility like Suitcase or Extensions Manager, you might keep all of your font samples in one stack with labeled dividers between the categories, or you may create separate stacks for each category of your fonts (Serif, Headline, etc.). You can generate printed samples of the fonts in 5 different sizes with 3 font samples printed per page. (Save paper by easily arranging to print on both sides.) By the way, there are several other HC stacks in the libraries that perform much the same task (search by keyword font to locate them). If your font collection has become too large for you to remember what everything looks like, give this stack a try.

_source.title: Cyphercard 1.0a
_source.creator: Chris Westbury
_source.description: Cyphercard is a simple but effective tool (a Hypercard stack) for encoding and decoding text documents. It uses no complex math, but simply encodes one document (the target) by using a second document as a reference document or a 'one time scratch pad', as they call it in the cypher biz. Cyphercard does not just use simple pointers into the reference document, but modulates those pointers using offsets which are also taken from that document. It uses various other encoding techniques as well. None of this is complicated or tricky. It doesn't need to be. The security of the code is not compromised at all by knowing exactly how it was coded. All the security comes from the fact that the code depends on the reference document and the password. The upshot is that anyone who knows which reference document and password to use can decode any document that was encoded with that reference document and password. Anyone who does not know that document and password will not be able to decode the target document. Cyphercard requires a PowerPC Macintosh and Hypercard 2.4.

_source.title: Domestic Violence Assessment
_source.creator: Daniel Jay Sonkin, Ph.D.
_source.description: The Domestic Violence Hypercard stack has been developed to assist mental health professionals, attorneys and investigators working perpetrators and victims of domestic violence and child abuse. The Domestic Violence stack is an overview stack of the assessment and risk determination process. This stack is freeware and is available at no charge. This series also includes three add-on stacks for conducting assessments with either victims or perpetrators; the Domestic Violence Inventory, the Child Abuse Inventory and the Domestic Violence Risk Assessment. The Domestic Violence and Child Abuse Inventories can be utilized to take comprehensive histories with both victims and perpetrators of violence. Summaries can be printed and inserted into the chart for easy review. These stacks also provide a numerical breakdown of the frequency of high, moderate and low level violent acts. The Domestic Violence Risk Assessment stack provides both a summary and recommendations that guide the professional in decision-making with regard to risk prevention and treatment planning. These stacks are shareware and are for sale at $20.00 each or all three for $50.00. These stacks will be available for Windows/DOS users in 1998. Visit my web site for further information at http://members.aol.com/dsonkin/index.html. ------------------------------------------------- Checked for viruses with Disinfectant and Vaccine.

They still want to use it however, and come to me with comments like, 'If it's so great, why can't it build itself!' Well, after reviewing some of the downloads for HC, and (finally) finishing my first reading of Goodman's HC Handbook and the APDA HC book, I decided that a self-building stack was possible. So... here is an early stage attempt at the project.............' 'There are three types of cards currently set up in the Stack Builder. Master Index (Major Topic) card(s), Minor Topic (Sub-topic) card(s) and finally the Info Cards. Major and Minor Topic cards are laid out to hold rows of titles and headings. The Info Cards are where you will put all the information.(and can be cross-referenced by the XREF function).......' 'New features since v3.0 include a couple of bug fixes, menu items, ability to create a 'blank' card for graphics entry, font changing in the text fields, text import and export, text merge, 'automatic' button insertion into the Home Stack, new fonts, capitalization options for Topic titles using modifier keys, addition of 'Mini Help', and some script reduction.' ---D.I. This stack is full of interesting scripting and shows a lot of careful thought.
_source.modernAnalog: A focused maker utility that helps me automate or inspect one technical task without needing a full desktop toolkit.

_source.title: Scientific Bibliography Stack...
_source.creator: Lars Ostergaard
_source.description: OPTIONAL: Access to SilverPlatter Medline SYSTEM 7: Compatible TYPE: Freeware stack Author's email address: lho@biobase.aau.dk Author's description: This stack is my first go at making a HyperCard stack. I have written it, because I needed a database for my scientific articles. The stack may also be used to keep a database of your books, etc. I have written an import function in the stack, this makes it possible to import downloaded from Silverplatter Medline. If you know a little about 'programming' in HyperCard, it is very easy to make it import other formats. Feel free to use this stack and give copies away, but don't charge money for it!!! You may also modify your personal copy, customising it to your needs. Please give the original copy to other interested Mac users. Input: You can either type the references into the correct field yourself or use the import-function. When typing the references yourself, the tab key will move the insertion point to the next field. The import-function is designed for Silverplatter Medline. You will find the function in the menu bar under 'x' (sigma). Each field, in a reference to be imported, is separated by a return and each new reference is separated by three returns. Further help on the format to be found in the stack. I have included a file downloaded from Medline, where you can see the format. You can also try importing this file with the import-function (it should work). If you want to import text from another database, it is very easy to modify the script yourself. If you are not familiar with HyperCard scripting, you are welcome to drop me a line. Output: You can transfer the references to a text file with the export-function in the menu bar under 'x' (sigma). Each field will be separated by a tab and each reference with a return. You can of course also modify this in any way you may like. I think this is very usefull for generating a bibliogaphy, if you are writing articles yourself. You can also print the references, just like any other HyperCard stack, by choosing 'Print report' from the menu bar.

_source.title: ReadCat XFCN.SIT
_source.creator: Oscar Hills
_source.description: ----------------- The ReadCat XFCN will give you handy access to much of the information in the floppy disk catalogs to use as you see fit in your stacks. You may display the catalog brazenly as I do in my stack, Auto Floppy Log 3.5, or you may elect to protect your users from this unpleasantness by using the information as a skeleton for fancy graphic representations. The ReadCat XFCN may be used in several modes. The simplest is a HyperTalk statement such as 'put ReadCat() into catbox.' This will cause HyperCard to prompt the user to insert a floppy disk which will then be read. The catalog will end up in the variable 'catbox' or any other container, in the following format: Line 1: The Volume Name of The Floppy Disk Lines 2 to (The Number of Files and Folders on The Disk): There will be 7 comma delimited items in each line as follows: Item 1: Will be either 'File' or 'Fldr' depending on which it is. Item 2: Will contain the short Date Last Modified of the file. Item 3: The Size of the File in KiloBytes. Item 4: The File Name. Item 5: The Directory ID number (see below). Item 6: The Parent ID number (Yup, parents too, see below). Item 7: The File Type, like 'APPL', 'TEXT', 'STAK'

_source.title: List Disk Directory XFCN 1.21
_source.creator: Bob Sanders
_source.description: ----------------- Version 1.21 - Bug with globals and A4 register fixed. This caused odd problems within Hypercard. If you are using 1.20 please replace it with 1.21. This XFCN will put a sorted list of files contained on a volume with their file info into a global variable in HyperCard. The list can be sorted by file name, file type or creator. This sample stack displays the first 394 file names in a card field. If there are more than that they are in the global variable but one card field can only hold 29996 bytes and that's 394 lines X 76 bytes maximum in this case. The delimiter between fields as well as the delimiter between records can be set in the call to allow for different preferences in how the information is used. Bob

_source.description: This new version fixes a bug that did not allow commas in the file names. In addition, the updated version of Roger Brown's ListDialog XFCN has been installed. What?! Another floppy disk cataloguer? True; however, here are some reasons this stack is worth a look: 1. It's free. 2. It allows for completely HANDS OFF cataloguing of multiple disks. (You only need to type if you want to add comments) 3. The size of each disk, the memory used, the memory left, and the list of files on the disk are automatically read and loaded. 4. It generates disk labels on standard Avery 5196 and even lets you print on a partially used page of labels. 5. It has nice graphics and sound. 6. It has on-line help. 7. It automatically sorts your disks (alphabetically) and creates and index. A few of the resources in the stack (XCMD) are shareware- if you intend to use them for your own projects please see the READ ME file for information. The stack itself is free. I checked it for virii with DI 2.9

_source.title: Disk-it.sit
_source.creator: B. Cherkas
_source.description: This stack is very useful and easy to use. Basically what this stack does is keep a catalog of your floppy disks. All you do is create a new card by pressing the 'New Card' button. The underneath 'Set Disk Size' is there merely to calculate the free size left on your disk. The default setting is for 800k disks. If you want to catalog a 400k disk just press the button next to the field. If you don't know what size disk you are using, leave it set to 800 - no big deal. Next press the 'Catalog Disk' button. This brings up a dialog box requesting you insert a floppy disk - it does the rest! When the catolog is finished your disk will pop out. Just follow the same procedure to catolog your other disks. If your disks change information a lot, go back to the card which you want to re-catalog and start the same procedure over again. The fields will be written-over by the new information, there is no need to erase fields (although you are perfectly welcome to change information by editing fields). The Size of Disk field near the bottom of the card shows your total memory used up on that disk. The Free Size field below it shows the amount of memory left. Also, on the main card, an easy way to go to a particular card or view what cards (disks) you have, just press the 'Table of Contents' button. A dialog will pop up requesting you choose a card to navigate to. If you just want to view the cards (disks) and not go to one then simply choose 'Cancel' and you will be returned to the main card. To print a report with all the contents of your floppies just press the 'Print Report' button. The print dialog box will pop-up and all you have to do is press 'OK'. Normally don't worry about changing anything unless you're not happy with the report.
_source.modernAnalog: A focused maker utility that helps me automate or inspect one technical task without needing a full desktop toolkit.

It allows you to import all kinds of text (testimony, depositions, interrogatory, etc.) into a specific document classification (you can have an unlimited number of user-defined stacks to contain the text). DAMS automatically extracts all found text into a separate file for examination, retaining document, page, and line number references. One of DAMS strongest features is its note system. _source.creator: PlatySoft
_source.description: PlatyKeys Music Glossary by Platypus Software December, 1993 This stack is more or less a text-only pocket music dictionary in machine readable and usable form. The word lists were compiled from a combination of the Harvard Dictionary of music, and the Clark pocket music dictionary. This glossary does not have detailed articles like the Harvard, but it gives you enough of a clue as to the meaning of a word to be able to fake most music exams. There is one hell of a lot of information here. The stack is not flashy like some other stacks I have put here. If you are a student of music, it's probably worth your while to get it. This version is free for personal use. If you're an application developer and you want to use it for anything, either freeware, shareware, or commercialware, you must ask my explicit permission. My answer will most likely be yes, but basically I don't want someone making money off the sweat of my brow and not cutting me in on it. If you wish to show your appreciation, money is a very universally accepted form of gratitude. Any amount. If you're too poor for that, a postcard or an Email would be nice. Believe me, I know what doing $50 gigs sometimes and sometimes not, is like. If I was a rich money grubbing SOB I wouldn't be giving this away, right? Play on into the night dudesx C. R. Dick P.O. _source.creator: PaddyO
_source.description: ----------------- The Library Stack is a disk catalogger. It will read the Volume Name, File Name, File Size, and File Type from your floppy disks and put each file on a card. It also attempts to group files with similar names on the same card. It also has a place for you to write keywords and a description. It has sorting and searching capabilities. Shareware. This is an updated version of Library Stack 1.0. It fixes a bug in the find buttons. If all of the associated files have the same name, they should end up on the same card. If they have different names, then they will end up on different cards and you will have to group them manually. Version 1.0 put each file on a separate card and they all had to be grouped manually. It also ignores the folder names, desktop files, and the information type files on Educorp disks which previously ended up on separate cards. In the future I hope to find a better way to group the files so that whatever files are grouped in a folder will end up on the same card.

_source.title: Cloze Text.sit
_source.creator: Skreyn
_source.description: To aid in correcting the cloze test, only the _exact_ word is considered correct, so if a student answers #23 with 'also' and the original word inthe document is 'and,' it is marked incorrect. To offset this, the grading scale for the test is rather lenient. If most of the students are able to correctly fill in 45%-55% of the blanks, the document is considered to be at the proper reading level for the students. Numbers higher or lower than these percentages may indicate that the document is too easy or too hard for the students. While there is a program similar to this in the AOL archives, that program contains pre-defined text. _This_ program allows you to import a document (such as an essay-test) which has been saved as a TEXT fileand run the cloze text on the document. It creates a new file with the words blanked out (and numbered for easy reference) and a list of the words that were removed (also numbered, for easy grading). This program is handy for teachers that write their own exams and are concerned with the reading level that the test is being written at, or likewise for an assigned book (the instructor would have to type in part of the book's text...). Freeware. If you like it, drop me a line, though!

_source.title: CMU Tunnels
_source.creator: Dennis Moul
_source.description: Newsgroups: alt.college.tunnels Subject: CMU Tunnels Hypercard stack (long) Date: 14 March 1994 Folks, I found something lying around on one of my old Mac disks that may interest some of you: an interactive hypercard map of the CMU steam tunnels. I wrote this stack back in '88 as part of a student application fair called StraightTalk. It's very simple, basically a map with hot spots that take you to pages describing the entrance or tunnel you clicked on. It was intended to demonstrate how you could do neat things with a Mac in a short period of time. It's not up to date, but I thought I would post it just for fun. Those of you who haven't actually been in tunnels can get a sense of how they are sometimes laid out, and I thought ex-CMU people might get kick out of reminiscing about old times. For current CMU people, you'll note the conspicuous absence of Resnik Hall & West Wing, the Parking Garage, and anything else that happened after 1988. Don't take this stack as an authoritative reference, but as a peek into the mid/late 80's CMU culture. The StraightTalk fair was officially sponsored, and no, I didn't get into any trouble for presenting this stack. I thought I would, and held my breath when a couple of Security folks came over, but nothing happened. Apple even had a photographer take pictures of all the presenters, including some of me near a tunnel entrance! I never saw the pictures, though... :( Anyway, here's the stack (called "Tunnels"). Remove everything above the "this file must be converted with binhex 4.0" line and then run it through binhex. Feel free to pass this on to other folks, put it on servers, ftp sites, etc. If you do, please send me a note letting me know so I can have a feel for its distribution. Ideally, I'd like it if the text portion of this message was included as a readme file so new folks would know some of the history. Enjoy, Dennis
_source.modernAnalog: A searchable pocket reference for CMU Tunnels that I can browse offline and share specific entries from when needed.

For those of you who haven't downloaded this yet, it is a simple little stack that allows you to record entries of facts and quotes as you do research from a book or periodical. Its much like the note cards you made as a kid in school when you had a report to write. It provides a place to record bibliographic info (and convienently names the stack after the book in question), provides some user preference settings and a neat search function that is better than HyperCard's standard one. This search doesn't get hung up by only finding the first occurance of your text in a stack; it will search after the current occurence. _source.title: City to City
_source.creator: MediaWorks
_source.description:
City to City is one of the best arguments for a laptop Mac to date. For people traveling the United Slates, Activision's new HyperWare offering gives a pretty thorough traveler's guide to 31 major cities, ranging from Honolulu to Boston and Seattle to Miami. In addition to making a wide range of resources available, City to City features a personal-profile card and customized itinerary planning. It also integrates with Activision's international travel stack, Business Sense.

 Making the best of the HyperCard interface, City to City begins with a map of the U.S. Clicking on a city brings up a card related to that metropolis. The 12 information categories include Business, Dining, Late- Night, Lodging, Market Data, Media, Shopping, Sports, Theaters, Tourism, Transport, and VisitorInfo. Buttons representing each of the categories are lined up along the bottom of each city card, so you can jump quickly from one category to another. You can also pick a different city from a pop-up list box. It's possible to preselect any category and go directly from the map to a specific topic for a certain city.

 Within each of the 12 categories is a wealth of information gleaned from the efforts of more than 30 researchers. The Lodging category, for instance, contains information about hotels, organized into three classes — First Class, Business Class, and Coach. The hotel's telephone number is listed (a toll-free number in many cases), and simply clicking on the Telephone button next to the number initiates a call to the selected hotel. You can also checkmark a hotel to indicate that you have a reservation and fill in the check-in and check-out dates, confirmation number (if any), and the daily rate you were quoted. With a click of the mouse, you can then transfer the necessary information to a current itinerary card. Should you discover a hidden gem of a hotel that isn't currently included in City to City, you can add it easily.

 In addition to the hotel information, each Lodging card contains a button that, when clicked, displays a map of the urban area with its major roads, airports, and the location of each hotel in the list. The hotels are represented by numbered buttons on the map and are also listed, by number, along the side of the map card. Clicking a button on the map takes you back to the main card containing that hotel’s information.

 The other categories contain equally useful features. Restaurants are listed by the kind of food they serve and are rated both by cost and by quality. Phone numbers are also listed, and you can copy restaurant data directly to itineraries. Emergency numbers, freight companies, convention centers, and much more can be found within the City to City stacks.

 I had an urge, on occasion, to see more information about specific items restaurants or hotels, for instance, or airline schedules — but that’s just the magic of HyperCard. You somehow begin to expect to see everything hyper-linked to more information. Obviously, City to City couldn't include a review of each restaurant and each hotel. With more than 10,000 entries and 7,500 phone numbers, the product is already impressive. Speaking as an inveterate telecommunicalor, I would have liked to see Telenet and Tymnet local-access numbers, at least, and even some good local BBS numbers. And the maps should display other special locations such as convention centers.

 The most versatile feature of City to City is the itinerary that you can easily develop as you browse through a city's cards. The itinerary is actually a stack. You tell the program the dates for your trip, and it creates a card for each day of your journey. The daily card has information about flights, lodgings, and car rentals as well as an appointment calendar. Other buttons open additional information about airlines, rental ears, and hotels (including appropriate toll-free numbers) as well as handle HyperCard functions related to itinerary cards and stacks. Finally, you cun add a special note card for any particular dale.

 My only complaint stems from the initial sentence of this review. City to City would be a great product to take along while traveling. Unfortunately, there is no affordable traveling Mac, so this wealth of useful material will have to stay home the next time I travel. I hope to get everything I need from it before I leave.

 DeMaria, Rusel. (September 1988). City to City. MacUser . (pgs. 57, 64).
_source.modernAnalog: A searchable pocket reference for City to City that I can browse offline and share specific entries from when needed.

_source.title: HyperAlgebra
_source.creator: Donald Hazlewood, Carol Hazlewood
_source.description: HyperAlgebra - A HyperCard Stack which generates random tests from a databank of problems on adding/subtracting, multiplying, and factoring algebraic expressions. It uses a HyperCard XFCN by Chris Rogers and Doug Stein that uses MathLinkÂ¨ to communicate with MathematicaÂ¨. Stacks by Carol and Donald Hazlewood MathLinkÂ¨ XFCN by Chris Rogers and Doug Stein Inspired by HyperMathLink, a HyperCard Stack written by Doug Stein and John Bonadies. Â©1993 Redwood Software Permission granted to distribute these files freely provided this notice and all comments stay intact. Version 1.0 (28 May 1993) --------------------------- HYPERALGEBRA TEST GENERATOR HyperAlgebra, by clicking on a button, generates an algebra test of 10 problems in the appropriate area by randomly selecting problems from a databank of problems, allows the user to enter an answer to the problem, and checks to see if the entered answer is correct. If not, a hint is offered as to what might be incorrect and requests the user to try again. If the correct answer is not entered in 3 tries, an offer is made to give the correct solution and the user can then go to the next problem. On completion, a score is tabulated, with 10 points awarded if a problem is corrected answered on the first try, x points if answered on the second try, y points if answered on the third try, and 0 points if missed three times. This score with the users name, the time elapsed, and the particular problems selected randomly from the databank is recorded in a stack in the Resources folder, "Scores." HyperAlgebra uses MathematicaÂ¨ via the MathLink XFCN by Chris Rogers and Doug Stein for the appropriate algebraic manipulations. This means that when you start a HyperAlgebra test, MathematicaÂ¨ is automatically launched. GETTING STARTED The HyperAlgebra window has three buttons, currently, for generating tests on adding/subtracting, multiplying, and factoring algebraic expressions. The user can enter a solution by clicking buttons or directly typing in entry field of the test stack. A Mathematica Kernel is launched initially by using either the "Link to MathematicaÂ¨" button or the "Check" button. TECHNICAL NOTES HyperAlgebra uses an XFCN by Chris Rogers and Doug Stein that in turn uses the MathLinkÂ¨ protocol to talk to a Mathematica Kernel. By editing the script for the "Link to MathematicaÂ¨" button, you can make this stack communicate with a Mathematica Kernel running on another Macintosh (or even another brand of computer)! The folder "Local" contains a MathematicaÂ¨ package "1311.m" that defines two functions "CompareSolution" and "CompareFactors" when a Mathematica Kernel is activated. PACKING LIST About HyperAlgebra - this document HyperAlgebra - a HyperCard Stack Local - a Macintosh folder containing: 1311.m - a MathematicaÂ¨ package defining the functions "CompareSolution" and "CompareFactors" used by the .test Stacks Resources - a Macintosh folder containing: Add_Subtract.test - a HyperCard Stack generating the add/subtract test Factor.test - a HyperCard Stack generating the factor test Multiply.test - a HyperCard Stack generating the multiply test Scores - a HyperCard Stack which collects scores on the various tests ExpBuilder - a HyperCard Stack used by the .test Stacks Frac2Builder - a HyperCard Stack used by the .test Stacks VarChoice - a HyperCard Stack used by the .test Stacks Data - a Macintosh folder containing: Add_Subtract.data - a MicroSoft WordÂ¨ document with test data Factor.data - a MicroSoft WordÂ¨ document with test data Multiply.data - a MicroSoft WordÂ¨ document with test data

_source.title: HyperSavvy HOT for scripters v1.
_source.creator: J.R. Mooneyham
_source.description: Many of the scripts in HyperSavvy have already proven themselves out in the field, in other stacks, enjoying a minimum of a year's worth of debugging and polishing before ever appearing in HyperSavvy; so it's highly unlikely you'll encounter a problem with them. This stack is NOT PROTECTED, and is set to USERLEVEL 5. So you have full access to everything (so long as you possess the full-blown HyperCard application). This allows you to use HyperSavvy as a 'template' stack- transform it directly into your own custom stacks- or borrow bits and pieces instead, as preferred. Anyone may use these scripts (AND the custom navigation palette) in their own shareware and commercial stacks, with no more obligation than plainly stating within those stacks the source of the code and palette. Major scripting techniques presented in HyperSavvy (there's lots of minor ones not listed here) include the following: 1. Central control over stack navigation that ties together the cursor/arrow keys, Go menu commands, custom menu commands, keyboard menu shortcuts, and an attractive custom navigation palette into a single system. This allows you to change all stack navigation operations with just the tweak of a few central script handlers. 2. Your own 'About [filename]...' under the Apple menu, and 'Quit [filename]' under the File menu, and a custom '[filename]' menu, easily customized within MINUTES for your own stacks. 3. Comprehensive modifications of HyperCard menus on demand- menu AND menu item deletions, disablings, enablings, breaks, and redirections of commands- whatever you require for your own project. 4. A fast and elegant PopUp menu (in pure HyperTalk) which changes 'on-the-fly' to present different options for different cards. 5. An exhaustive trap script for all HyperCard menu calls- simply insert your own custom scripts for any menu item you desire. This gives you absolute control of every menu command in a stack (and so is great when you want to protect your stack from hackers with a few special tricks of your own). 6. Full custom menu and palette wrap, and transparent userLevel changes, within multi-stack environments. This greatly reduces the chance of script errors, while also making your own stacks behave much more professionally under complex conditions. You (and your stack's users) will be delighted. 7. A wide range of ask and answer dialog boxes, from simple to complex. 8. Three different styles of progress gauge dialogs, including the standard bar, and new choices of page and circular styles, all equipped with Abort buttons (pure HyperTalk). 9. A versatile scrolling list dialog with click-to-choose and multiple function buttons. 10. 'Find...' and 'Find Next' scripts complete with a full range of contingency dialogs already built-in, ready and waiting to be pasted into your own stack. 11. Background processing for HyperCard, with examples providing time and date readouts for users, and a trap script that'll tell you everytime a user begins or quits typing into a field. 12. Ways to keep tabs on the size of any script or text field in a stack, and intercept and deal with HyperCard script errors in a way invisible to end-users. Why spend months trying to gather up and comprehend all these techniques separately, with little or no help, and then spend more months attempting to make them all work together, when you can just download HyperSavvy today? Become an overnight expert in HyperCard- by downloading HyperSavvy HOT today.

_source.title: ResGenie 2.2.1
_source.creator: Mark D. Clark
_source.description: ResGenie 2.2 is my first major revision to the original ResGenie of last year. I now support the addcolor external, full easy FX, multiple FX on opencard, new spreadhseet style windows for viewing resources, a ppat tool for easy use of ppat's with addcolor, new and improved control palettes, a new dialog for importing PICT files (allows previews-and corrects a problem where i forgot to write a renumber resource routine last time around-ouch), creates color ready stacks with superior scripts that don't blitz regular fx throughout hypercard, doesn't mess any of your menus up, doesn't take you into a 'color mode' that stops everything else. And more, much more, such as a 'link' tool that creates dynamic links between color cards and stacks--writing the correct scripts and giving you a pop up menu of color fx to choose from; a color object report tool that gives you access to all of addcolor's database info at the click of a mouse ( card or bg id's, and object ids in the case of buttons and fields). ResGenie objects no longer contain the full scripts for changing effects, etc., but still *do provide useful info* in their script headers. Includes handy 'Object' tools for listing, aligning, editing objects (a modeless real Mac window). An 'Objects' window that gives you a list of all objects, card and bg levels, lets you rename, edit contents, nudge point by point, change the visible, edit the script--all without putting you in a modal dialog (of course not). Locate hidden fields and buttons, or those dreaded '0,0,0,0' coordinate objects that you didn't even realize were there. Grab the text of locked fields without having to putter with the field tool. Get info on externals in your stacks, read the hex or ascii output from addcolor database, preview or view pictures, play sounds, record sounds (in your stack or any other file)--with more compression available than through audio palette, and more... ResGenie installs *nothing* into your home stack, doesn't work unless you tell it to, and doesn't care where it is--you push the activate button and it's ready. ResGenie adds *no* background noise to the message hierarchy (i.e., doesn't have an on openstack handler that runs every time you open any stack)... Color authoring has never been so easy or painless. Resource importing has never been so modeless. Try it. I think you'll like what you see. A small 'getting started' stack has been included to help you see the full potential of ResGenie. Import PICT files, Import resources, record sounds (any file), play sounds, get info on externals, delete resources, move resources between files, modeless operation. Create custom icons (thumbnails) for PICT and MooV files, create FX tools for addcolor, a linking tool for addcolor navigation buttons with color FX (similar to the old 'link' tool, but writes scripts for color effects as you link). Report tool for getting full addcolor database info on any color object. Much, much more. *ResGenie 2.2.1 contains some routines for helping out in low memory situations. I also included some notes on recommended RAM settings for HyperCard. Still $10. MC ------------------------ The Macintosh HyperCard Forum thanks this uploader for including Keywords at the top of the file description. For more info on uploading files and for tips on increasing the number of folks who download your files - see the document '*** Uploaders please read this' at the top of the New Files library.

_source.title: RunTime Revolution
_source.creator: Runtime Revolution Ltd.
_source.description:
I can hear you now. "Oh, goodie. Yet another programming language. Just what we need. What do I need with another language? Isn't [insert your personal favorite language du jour here] good enough?" Leaving aside for the moment the question of why any serious software developer would confine him or herself to a single programming language, let's just say that if you want to develop Mac Classic and OS X applications that also happen to run virtually unchanged on Windows and *nix platforms, Revolution may just be the sweetest thing that's happened to you in a long, long time.

 If you're a seasoned professional programmer who writes only for the Macintosh, you may still find Revolution a useful tool. You can use it to prototype user interfaces quickly, turn those prototypes into demos for users to evaluate as you rapidly modify the UI in response to user feedback.

 If you're one of perhaps hundreds of thousands of people I call Inventive Users, you're going to love the ease of programming and application design in Revolution as well as the way it gets along with corporate databases, the Web, and rich media. (Inventive Users are people who don't program for a living but who are willing to take time to learn an accessible scripting language and use it to create applications they or their departments need or want.)

 What Is Revolution?

 Revolution, a product of Edinburgh, Scotland-based Runtime Revolution Ltd., grows out of HyperCard, a much-beloved and widely used Apple Computer product that the company abandoned a few years ago, much to the chagrin of tens of thousands of its customers, including some of the biggest companies in the world. HyperCard ran only on Macintoshes and was a strictly black-and-white application for most of its life. When color was added, near the end of the HyperCard life cycle, it was not well thought out and never worked very well.

 Scott Raney loved HyperCard but he needed something like it to run on Unix. So he set about to develop a HyperCard-like product for his platform of choice and wound up creating the MetaCard engine, about 10 years ago. Kevin Miller and his Scottish cohorts licensed the MetaCard engine, put a more robust and attractive programming UI on it and extended it in some other crucial ways, marketing the result as Revolution. Recently, Runtime Revolution acquired MetaCard and Raney now works for the engine's new owners.

 Revolution combines a powerful, graphical IDE (integrated development environment) with a highly accessible programming language called Transcript to create a platform for developing a broad range of software applications that will run virtually unchanged on Mac Classic, OS X, every flavor of Windows currently supported by Microsoft (and some that aren't) and many versions of *nix, including the widely popular Linux. Applications are in color, take full advantage of native UI widgets as much as possible, and offer performance comparable to Java and Basic applications.

 In fact, I like to call Revolution Java without the Java. Revolution delivers on the promise of true cross-platform development better than Java in my experience, and does so at a fraction of the programming time and effort.

 Shafer, Dan. (2004). Review: Runtime Revolution, Programming for Mere Mortals. MacTech. preserve.mactech.com/articles/mactech/Vol.20/ 20.05/RuntimeRevolution/index.html

_source.title: MegaHome II 3.01:New Home Stack
_source.creator: Roger Plyer (SirDeath Y)
_source.description: MegaHome II 3.01 is a fully-documented, full-featured replacement for the HyperCard 2.x 'Home' stack. Released on January 1, 1992, it reflects exactly thirteen months' hard work on improving the stack since the release of version 1.1 on December 1, 1990. It is offered as $15 shareware from Earth Systems. MegaHome II provides numerous features not found in the Home stack shipped with HyperCard. It features a complete Finder-like interface featuring dragging, selecting and double-clicking. It also includes a moderately secure 'safe' card, known as Ft. Knox, to guard your confidential material. Another useful feature is a built-in screen-saver. All features are documented in the included MegaHome HELP stack. NOTE: Version 3.01 is functionally equivalent to version 3.0. If you are currently using version 3.0, there is NO NEED to download this archive. The only changes made in 3.01 were the addition of custom icons and the updating of the version number to reflect a final release rather than the beta-test version incorrectly indicated in 3.0. MegaHomex II's third release boasts a number of new features not found in versions 1.1 and 2.0.: -The Diagnostics stack has been incorporated into the home stack; -The confusing and virtually useless Prefs stack has been eliminated; -F15 now activates the Screen Saver, and the Screen Saver has been rewritten to resize itself to the size of the current screen, as well as other new features; -'Power Commands' are now standard; -Displays can now be temporarily hidden; -'Atheist Mode' has been added for those who do not celebrate December 25; -Script Checksums have been added to protect some code from hazardous modixcation; -User-dexnable Auto-Compacting Thresholds have been added; -A message is shown in the message box during Auto-Compacting; -A few handlers have been compiled into XCMDs and XFCNs for greater speed; -A new Intercept, compactMe (for programmers), has been added; -A 'Shrink' button has been added for users of small screens and MultiFinder or System 7; -MegaHome's menus are hidden when in other stacks; -Ft. Knox displays the stopwatch cursor properly, and security has been greatly improved; -Level 4 Support for the soon-to-be-released Module Manager has been added; -The Icon Picker is finally here--you no longer need to worry about stupid ID numbers!; -You are no longer prompted upon quitting; the quit command functions normally. 'Installing MegaHome is brief and simple. From the Finder, simply get rid of your old Home stack, and rename MegaHome, 'Home'. The next time you start up HyperCard, MegaHome will be installed.' MegaHome *must* be installed as the current Home stack. If one attempts to open the MegaHome stack when it is not installed as the current home stack, MegaHome will search for global variables that it assumed were installed upon startup time. Errors will result when it discovers that such variables were not initialized as they should have been.

_source.title: Rites of Passage v2.2
_source.creator: John Hughes (and Paul Gronemeyer)
_source.description: READ ME! RITES OF PASSAGE A SCIENCE FICTION ROLEPLAYING MODULE ON HYPERCARD Rites of Passage is a series of HyperCard 2 stacks that comprise a systemless science fiction roleplaying module. You have just downloaded the Menu and Introduction/Character stack. The aim of the Rites Project is to release the entire module (including printable character sheets and GM notes) on HyperCard for free distribution and play. THE NATURE OF THE STACKS The two introductory stacks are large, each over 500K when decompressed. They together comprise 85 cards giving a fifteen minute sound, text and graphic introduction to the module followed by selections from the Characters� stack. The introductory stack does NOT contain the full module: it will be released after convention play in early 1993. Rites of Passage is written and scripted by John Hughes of The Wyrm�s Footprint. It is an experiment in electronic module design and distribution. A convention version of the module will run at CanCon (Canberra, Australia) in January 1993. The completed stacks will be available through the Net shortly thereafter. The stack is postcardware, and may be freely distributed, as long as it is not altered in any way. If you have any suggestions for improvement � scientific, programming, dramatic or jest plain old english expression then PLEASE let me know. The introductory stack contains full information on the module and on the further stacks. E-mail me through InterNet at john.hughes@anu.edu.au Rites of Passage is a systemless and thematic module � a module designed to explore a number of themes or moral quandaries using mood, atmosphere and depth of characterisation. Set in the 24th Century, it is designed to explore the cultural and psychological impact of computer implants and virtual reality. While focussing on characterisation and drama, the module also features a murder mystery, a black hole, possible alien contact... oh, just download and explore the stack. HOW TO USE THE STACK Rites of Passage requires HyperCard 2 and System 6.07 or higher. It will run on any Mac with the necessary memory. As it uses a large number of digitised sounds, a minimum of 4 Meg RAM is recommended. The Introduction (and subsequent stacks) use resources stored in the Menu stack, so they should not be separated. VERSION 2.1 Versions 1.x of these stacks used MacIntalk, a voice generating utility no longer supported by Apple. The MacIntalk scripts caused system crashes in certain Mac II machines, and they have been deleted in the current version. In addition, several cards have been added or clarified in the current version. The original large Introduction has been split into two stacks. Oh yeah, and the spelling... I'd be grateful for comments or reviews. REALLY. Please mail me at the address above. Free Information! Free Imagination! John Hughes

_source.title: EZ Bike Gear Calculator
_source.creator: Durer Shomer
_source.description: An attractive, fun, and very easy to use and understand program, THE GEAR/INCH CALCULATOR will automatically figure out the 'gear/inches' for any bicycle's front chainwheel/rear cog combinations. It also contains a complete easy to read description and history of just what 'gear/inches' are (and why you should care), for those who aren't familiar with the term. Determining and installing the proper selection of gears on your bike will enable you to cope with the enormous variations you may encounter in your riding, not only in road and wind conditions, but also in your body's ability to produce power. THE GEAR/INCH CALCULATOR is necessary because, unfortunately, the selection of gears on most 'factory-built' bikes is chosen mostly in terms of the manufacturer's convenience, and also, of course, must necessarily reflect an 'average' of all the thousands of riders who buy that brand and model of bike. However, in real life, a dedicated rider with years of experience is going to require a different set-up from a casual novice, and, of course, a rider in a flat area like Florida, for instance, will require a totally different range of gears from a rider in a mountainous area like, say, Colorado. I'm sure that by now you've 'personalized' your bike, choosing gadgets and accessories that reflect your own personal taste. Well, taking it one step further, and insuring that the selection of gears installed on your bike is the selection that's exactly right for you and your riding style, is where THE GEAR/INCH CALCULATOR comes in. Suitable for both road and mountain bikes, THE GEAR/INCH CALCULATOR can process data for up to 3 front chainwheels and 8 rear cogs. Using THE GEAR/INCH CALCULATOR to figure out the 'gear/inches' of your present set-up will help you easily determine whether any of your present chainwheel/cog combinations are duplicates, whether your high gear is high enough, your low gear low enough, or if any of the combinations fall outside the range of gearing that's suitable for your riding style and conditions, as well as the best shift pattern to use to 'step' through your range of gears. And, racers and tri-athletes can also use THE GEAR/INCH CALCULATOR to 'fine-tune' the selection of gears they might use for a particular event. A long, hilly race would require a totally different set of gears from, say, a flat time-trial, or short, tight-cornered criterium on city streets. So, give THE GEAR/INCH CALCULATOR a try. It's a great looking, fun and easy to use program that's well worth the download for experienced and novice cyclists alike. And, since it's 'postcard-ware,' it's 'almost' free!

_source.title: doTitles & curlyQuotes XFCNs
_source.creator: RobtBass
_source.description: doTitles & curlyQuotes XFCNs Sunday, June 21, 1998 -------------------------------------------------------------------- doTitles XFCN (v. 1.0) and curlyQuotes XFCN (v. 1.0) -------------------------------------------------------------------- Size, expanded: 65K. Public domain XFCNs. -------------------------------------------------------------------- doTitles() XFCN id #31611 v. 1.0 This external function capitalizes words in titles. Unlike other functions and scripts I've seen, it doesn't just capitalize the first letter of each word indiscriminately. Instead, it capitalizes the first and last words of the title and all nouns, pronouns, adjectives, verbs, adverbs, and subordinate conjunctions; it does not capitalize articles, coordinate conjunctions, or prepositions, regardless of length, unless they are the first or last words of the title. If the title contains a subtitle preceded by a colon, it capitalizes the first word of the subtitle too. If the title contains only capital letters or has otherwise been capitalized improperly, it recapitalizes according to the criteria above. If there are straight up-and-down quotation marks around the title (') or odd substitutions for quotation marks (`` or ''), it converts them to curved, left- and right-handed marks. It also converts single quotation marks and apostrophes. It's smart enough to recognize some contractions ('til, 'twas, and so forth), but not all possible contractions. It capitalizes abbreviations if they contain periods (U.S.A. or Ph.D.), but not if they don't (ACLU or SJ). (That's because it doesn't know whether ACLU is a word or an abbreviation.) It converts double hyphens (--) into real em dashes, and hyphens between dates and numbers in series (1901-76) into real en dashes. Finally, it converts double or multiple spaces into single spaces. curlyQuotes() XFCN id #19501 v. 1.0 This function also converts quotation marks and apostrophes to the curved variety, but it does nothing else. ------------------------------------------------------------------- Syntax: put doTitles(container) into container put curlyQuotes(container) into container -where container is any HyperCard container, such as a field or variable. ------------------------------------------------------------------- doTitles and curlyQuotes come installed in a demonstration stack. To install these external functions in another stack, you must use a resource-copying utility, such as ResCopy, ResEdit, or ResMover. -------------------------------------------------------------------

_source.title: Waldo v4.1.1
_source.creator: John J. Gaudreault
_source.description: WALDO 4.1.1 (SM) is a HyperCard HyperText program. It requires HC v 2.0. WALDO turns plain text files into HyperText documents. There are many HyperText indexing and multimedia features in WALDO. Waldos may include graphics, sound and QuickTime Movies. Waldo is ShareWare. Waldo creates Digital books, magazines and documents. Waldo 4.1.1 is a bug fix for HC v2.1. users only. XHome (SM) v1.2 is a bug fix. Waldo includes XHome 1.2 & Waldex (SM). Waldo and Soto (SM) allow the creation of very sophisticated HyperMedia Documents. You may embed graphics, sound, QuickTime movies and text in words. You may use Soto to import any text document. Soto may also be used to import text in a variety of structured formats including; SeText, Dart, Waldo, MacWeek, NewsBytes. While Soto may be used as a stand alone text reader for these formats it becomes a way of 'importing' prestructured text into Waldo. Text in Soto can be exported into Waldo format. When your text is in Waldo HyperText links and tables may be easily constructed by clicking on text. These links may have attached graphics, sound, QuickTime, or text. The links or spots are assembled into HyperQueues. These Queues may easily be restructured by clicking in list views. There may be up to 10 Queues in a document. Queues may be erased or saved to disk and later reloaded. New Queues may be created at anytime. Registered users will be given a 'KEY' to unlock advanced 'OpenBook' features including; CD-ROM support, locking selected features out from reader access, further customazation of locked books, creating an integrated book with all media locked inside of book. While Waldos in the 'OpenBook' state are locked the reader can still create HyperQueues that utilize all of the HyperMedia features--Queues that have been locked by the author are protected from reader modification or erasure. XHome is an enviroment for MultiMedia creation and structuring. Graphics and sound created in XHome are saved as 'resources' in the XHome stack. Media, including QuickTime movies, created in other programs can be pasted or integrated into XHome. When ready the user compiles the media into an new XRes (SM) stack combining all media together with an automatically created script. This new XRes stack can be accessed from within the Waldo by simply calling up a list and clicking on a selection. There is too much more to include in this discription. Waldo is ShareWare and the registration fee is a $10.
