_source.title: Project Management
_source.creator: Edward Nutter
_source.description: ----------------- 'Projects' is an incomplete stack. It's a demonstration of a HyperTalk technique that relys on the presence of three externals by various authors. The three stacks that contain those externals may also be down loaded from AOL. The names of those files (on AOL) are: 1) 'FileName 1.3.2.sit' by Steve Maller 2) 'XNET PowerList' distributed by XNET or 'HyperList 3.2' by James L. Paul (on CIS only so far as I know) 3) 'PopUpMenu example.sit' by Andrew Gilmartin PowerList can be downloaded from AOL, and HyperList 3.2 is available on CIS. The scripting for the two is different, but both versions are in the 'Projects' button script. The PowerList version is functioning and the HyperList version is commented out. I use HyperList myself because it can return an index number directly rather than requiring another couple of lines of code to calculate it. Qualifications aside, here is what 'Projects' does. It enables a pop up menu with a list of 'projects', ie. any stack that you may be working on or that you use frequently. This helps me get to the stuff I'm working on without cluttering up my Home screen with a lot of buttons. You access this menu by pressing down on the 'Projects' button. The empty popup contains two selections and a disable line. The selections are 'New Project' and 'Delete Project. When you select 'New Project' you get an open file dialog (courtesy of the fileName XFCN) that will show only stacks. If you select one, you will be asked to provide a name for the menu name of the project. The stacks name will be the default answer. When you click the OK button on that dialog, that stack will be added to the popup menu. At that point you can pull down that menu, select the stack you want, and you're there. It's not as versitile as HyperCards built in button link facility in that you can't specify a card within the stack. If you need that feature, use a regular button. This is much more compact as I noted above, and more dynamic. Deleting projects is just as easy as adding them. Select the 'Delete Project' option and you'll get a list dialog of the current active projects. Select one, and it's gone from the menu. You can Cancel of course, or add the stack again if you change your mind. Download the stacks listed above and paste their externals into your Home stack. I think you HyperHackers out there find this as useful as I have. Enjoy. Ed Nutter - AOL EdNutter - CIS 76167,102

_source.title: ShowDialog XCMD 1.6.7r
_source.creator: Jay Hodgdon
_source.description: ShowDialog XCMD 1.6.7r Sunday, June 21, 1998 A revised version of the late Jay Hodgdon's ShowDialog 1.6.7 stack, with updated instructions and registration information. The ResEdit instructions have been rewritten for 2.1.1. THE EXTERNALS THEMSELVES HAVE NOT BEEN CHANGED. Includes several bonus externals. From the stack: 'ShowDialog XCMD is an external command that will allow you to display ANY type of dialog or alert that can contain ANY number of buttons, ANY amount of text, and ANY number of icons or PICT resources. You decide whether the dialog or alert is to have a default (outlined) button. You can have as many fields of static text as you wish and can modify any two of these on the fly (in the XCMD parameter list). You can have any number of editable text fields that the user can type into. Any of these fields can contain a default text string (that you specify in the parameter list). Your dialog can have as many check boxes or radio buttons as you want or can have a popup menu or a scrolling list from which the user can select items. You can also create specialized dialogs for the display of interactive help screens, color icon bars, or Macintosh 'splash screens.' You use ResEdit to create the necessary DLOG/DITL resources that allow ShowDialog to function. ShowDialog XCMD takes seven forms: Type 1, Type 2, Type 3, Type 4, Type 5, Type 6, and Type 7. Type 1 allows for 'regular' buttons, icons, PICTs, and static text only. You can modify the text as you wish in the parameter list. Type 2 can have these and ALSO any number of editable text fields that the user can type into and an unlimited number of check boxes. Any of these fields can also contain default text (provided in the parameters). Type 3 shares the same attributes as Type 2 with the exception that you can have as many radio buttons as you want instead of the check boxes. Type 4 is also similar to Type 2 with the exception that this type contains a popup menu. Types 5 and 6 are used to display 'help' screens consisting of multiple PICTs. Type 7 displays a scrolling field from which the user can select lines in a fashion similar to the Font/DA Mover. You specify the font, pt. size, and default line of the list in the parameter list. This type can also respond to any number of 'regular' buttons. (Editor's note, Type 7 ShowDialogs do not work properly under System 7.)' Edited and originally uploaded by Perry Barton (PBARTON).

_source.title: GraphiCalc
_source.creator: John E. Parsons
_source.description:
The marriage of traditional and desktop publishing methods is not without conflict, confusion—and lots of calculation. To ease some of the problems, a graphic designer for American Demographics , a 95 percent desktop-published magazine, has developed a HyperTalk-based program for calculating enlargements and reductions of photographs and illustrations. Designer John Parsons wrote GraphiCalc so that he could translate traditional printer's picas into PostScript picas online.

 Before writing GraphiCalc, Parsons struggled through the following steps: (1) measuring photos and illustrations in printer's picas; (2) converting printer's picas to decimals of inches; (3) switching the PostScript pica default on his page layout program to inches; (4) entering the decimals of inches; (5) switching back to the PostScript default on his page layout program; (6) entering the percentage enlargement or reduction desired.

 GraphiCalc has enabled Parsons to convert from original measurements to final reductions or enlargements—using only one HyperCard screen. He estimates that he can now do ten such conversions in the time he formerly spent doing one. GraphiCalc also lets him enter original and final measurements first, and then calculate the percentage of enlargement or reduction required.

 A third GraphiCalc calculation compensates for the size distortion that often occurs with plain-paper laser printers. If your printer is not perfectly accurate, you can print a document, measure its inaccuracy, and then type the name of the printer and the inaccuracy measurement into your GraphiCalc stack. From then on, you can select Find Input, and the program will compensate to produce WYSIWYG output.

 GraphiCalc will also create a file that logs all of your calculations, so you won't have to constantly move back and forth between computer and paper. Then you can save the file and print it out later when it's time to jot down the new measurements on the back of your photos or illustrations.

 GraphiCalc requires HyperCard and will run on any Mac with 1MB of RAM, but running it in tandem with another application requires at least 2MB. (Parsons has 5MB and uses GraphiCalc with PageMaker and Adobe Illustrator running all at once under MultiFinder.)

 - ( Macworld , April 1989)

_source.title: BasicDB XCMDs 1.1
_source.creator: Brian Blood
_source.description: This is a fully functioning demo of version 1.1 of my database external set called BasicDB. It is a demo because write access to database files will expire 14 days after it was created. Notice I said, write access. If you wish to purchase this product, please read the information on the first card. BasicDB is basic. A BasicDB file can contain any number of records. Each record, however, has a fixed structure of the following: 1. A Numeric key (32 bit long integer) 2. A String key of up to 63 characters 3. Textual data limited only by diskspace. (You must also have enough RAM to work with that data [read/write]) 4. Record ID that is unique to that item and is totally controlled by the system. Changes from version 1.0 to 1.1: - Expanded the string key for each database item from 31 characters to 63 characters. This change will make BasicDB 1.0 files incompatible with 1.1 files. You must reload your data through some sort of scripting. I'm sorry about this change, but it was necessary for my project. I'm also sorry that there is no conversion external. - Modified the return value of BasicDBNewItem to return the same full line of data that is returned by the GetIndex call. This will help in maintaining external indexes. - Added user stored long integer and Str127 to the header block. Also created appropriate externals to get/set these values. - Added field to the header block that is the last time that the database was compacted. - Changed all the FlushVol calls to PBFlushFile. Should improve performance a bit. - Created a BasicDBVerify external to check the integrity of the database. - Changed the error string handling to look for the STR# resource by name instead of ID. BasicDB does not have any indexing or anything fancy. It does have searching. You can search for an equality on the numeric key or the string key or a 'contains' search on the text data. Here is a list of the calls. BasicDBCreate BasicDBNewItem BasicDBSetItem BasicDBGetItem BasicDBFindItem BasicDBCompact BasicDBNumItems BasicDBGetIndex I developed BasicDB becuase I wanted to store the data I was working with outside of HyperCard, but I didn't need any sort of fancy stuff and I wanted to store lots of data inside a single record. Brian Blood Maverick Enterprises

_source.title: HyperTinker(TM) 1; RollOff
_source.creator: Bob West (BobWest1)
_source.description: --------------------------------------- RollOff -- Scripting that scrolls text horizontally to allow you to see the contents of a one-line field that are hidden due to a field's small size. --------------------------------------- Free! From... The Western Hemisphere Dog & Pony Company (TM) ------------------------------------- Don't you just hate it when you have a million fields you need to place on a card or background and you have a postage-stamp-size-area to work in? The fields are too small to make effective scrolling fields, so the text ends up to be 6-point Tiny Helvetica. Yeah, I hate it, too. So, I came up with a few tricks to avoid pulling out any more hair than necessary. One of them is RollOff. When Apple's Human Interface Guidelines are followed, Mac applications provide a way to edit the text that is hidden off one end of a field that's too short to show it all: the text automatically scrolls as you drag the mouse pointer past the right side of the field. Unfortunately, HyperCard doesn't provide that ability, so I sighed, sat down, and came up with a cheat. When the various parts of RollOff are installed in the appropriate handlers in your stack, you are permitted to see any text that's hidden off one side of a field. (Note that the dontWrap of the field need not be 'true', though it does look a bit better when it is). --- Version 1 of RollOff uses a global variable to store 'Rolled-Off' characters, so data could be lost if the machine dies while RollOff is working. Rare, but... it could happen. --- Version 2 uses fields to store the characters, as well as the id of the field they belong in; if the power goes out whilst you type, don't worry. When you come back to the card, the data will be restored from the field. This version is safer, but it isn't as fast. RollOff may be used free of charge in any private or commercial product, as long as the proper credit is displayed (noted in the card scripts). I hope this works for you. It may not mesh with what you're doing right now, but keep it around somewhere just in case... Ya never know when it might just work perfectly with some fancy project! Have fun! ...Bob West, The Western Hemisphere Dog & Pony Company (TM)

_source.title: EmailLog 2.3.1 -- UPDATE
_source.creator: Peter M. Brigham
_source.description: Use this stack to save your email correspondence and to compose email offline. Your AOL mailbox only saves messages for a limited time, so you can't just let old messages sit there indefinitely and still have them when you need them. This way you have all your correspondence at your fingertips - and you can use hypercard's find routines to locate text. EmailLog allows you to save email text files, sort and print them, find text, format text (one keystroke removes EOL's and extra spaces), compose messages offline, and open AOL with the message automatically put on the clipboard. Use EmailLog if you subscribe to any mailing lists - use a separate copy for each mailing list or correspondent, and you won't have to search through zillions of little text files to find what you are looking for. Or save all messages in one stack and sort them by correspondent. EmailLog also works for messages on FirstClass BBS's. Features include: compose multiple messages offline, select correspondents from a pop-up button, store often-used email addresses, auto-quote and auto-reply to a message, add a signature with one click. Multiple-card printing and exporting to file are supported. You can (if you choose) automatically mark for printing when importing and automatically log messages after sending; sort chronologically, or by correspondent or subject (message threads are grouped together); and import a whole folder of saved messages at once. As always, if you've been using an earlier version of EmailLog, you can import the present contents of your EmailLog stacks into a copy of this latest version. This is a very useful stack if you're carrying on any significant amount of email correspondence. If you subscribe to any mailing lists, as I do, and want to archive messages, try this out! (I have an archive of over 700 messages from one list which allows me to find and export/print old postings on a given topic within minutes.) Note: An XCMD was inadvertently not attached to version 2.3 of EmailLog. Please use this version -- 2.3.1. My apologies for any problems the defective stack caused. This stack has been checked for Mac and HyperCard viruses with Virex 5.6 Previous DL count: 38

_source.title: HyperMIDI
_source.creator: Nigel J. Redmon
_source.description:
The HyperMIDI Toolkit is a collection of routines (XCMDs and XFNCs) for MIDI programming in HyperTalk, the language of HyperCard. These routines include utilities for transmitting and receiving data over MIDI, and for implementing active MIDI controls.

 HyperMIDI is well suited for:

 Ugly little one-time tasks:

 A real-life situation: Yesterday I got around to looking at the MIDI implementation manual for my Roland D-50. The manual is not the most lucid I've seen, abounding with grammatical atrocities (an obvious translation from Japanese). In the dump format, the checksum is described as the sum of "address, size, and that checksum" being zero (modulo 128). Since size is not otherwise mentioned, I wonder if they mean address + data + checksum, address + size of record + data + checksum, etc. So, I do a bulk dump from the D-50 front panel, read it into a text field with RxMIDI, edit out all but one block by mouse selection, and write a 6 line script to add the 259 address and data bytes up and see what I get. Less than 5 minutes to do all of the above, a job which would otherwise would have been a pain even for an experienced programmer.

 Orphaned MIDI instruments:

 Have we seen these before?

 Custom solutions:

 We don't always like the way XYZ, Inc's patch editor for the XDD750+ synth is laid out in certain respects (you can't please everybody). Well, from MultiFinder you can run their patch editor, and switch to HyperMIDI when you want to use your custom oscillator/filter/envelope panel WITH RANDOM PATCH CAPABILITIES, etc.

 Experimental:

 Explore the Wonderful World of MIDI (hey, we'll get Marlin Perkins to host...).

 MIDI Macros:

 Set up your MIDI system from HyperMIDI, switch back to your megabuck sequencer program.

 Become a professional MIDI programmer:

 Programs using the HyperMIDI Toolkit routines may be distributed freely in free or shareware products (contact me about commercial applications), as long as you are a registered owner and adhere to any restrictions in the licensing agreement.

_source.title: TextMuncher 0.93
_source.creator: Peter M. Brigham
_source.description: From the Help field: TextMuncher is a text processor. I created it because I found that I sometimes needed to edit or format text in ways not easy in a word processor. TextMuncher not only cleans up raw ASCII text, but it also allows you to do such things as: change all words which begin with 'pre' to upper case, or delete any lines which contain 'http://', or set each word which immediately precedes 'Instructions:' to a given textFont, textSize, and textStyle. Capabilities: *** Operations: clean up ASCII text by taking out EOL's, replacing double en-dashes with em-dashes, smart-quoting, eliminating superfluous hyphens, etc. Also delete, replace, sort, change case, set text styles, and extract specified chunks of text to the clipboard. *** Operate on a selected block of text or on all lines/words/characters/strings which contain/don't contain/equal/begin with/end with/follow/precede a specified string, in the entire text or in any selected block of text. *** Define any combination of textFont, textSize, and textStyle as a named style, and apply it, via a pop-up list, to selected text or to all lines/words/... etc., which contain/don't contain/... etc. Like a limited version of word-processor styling, but with much more flexibility in applying styles automatically to specified pieces of text. *** Undo any of the above operations. *** Export the formatted text as an RTF file, which can be opened by most word processors. Limitations: *** Only works (for now) on text files less than 30K. *** Very slow on large searches. (That's Hypercard - powerful but slow.) *** Somewhat fussy about its operations. However, for certain procedures, it's still lots faster than doing it by hand in a word processor! DL this stack and keep it on hand. If in two months you haven't used it, then trash it. But I bet in that time you'll have come across at least one situation when you'll find it very handy...

_source.title: MiniTool Palette
_source.creator: HyperSTUff
_source.description: The MiniTool is a small 2 1/2' by 1/2' palette with nine icons. Combining modifier keys with single clicks allows you to perform over 50 different navigation and authoring tasks. The palette is completely 'self-contained' and requires no additional stack scripting; just copy its three resources into your Home stack, type 'palette miniTool' in the Message Box, and away you go. Using combinations of modifier keys and single clicks, MiniTool can take you to the 1st card, previous card, recent card, 1st bkgnd, previous bkgnd, next card, last card, next bkgnd, last bkgnd, or any stack window. It can open a stack, open the Recent display, push a card, pop a card, start using a stack, stop using a stack, close open windows, hide all HyperCard windows, open ResCopy, open ResEdit, open any other application, open any stack's script, rush you Home, open Home's script, call GetHomeInfo, and do several other things. Stealth mode lets you set lockMessages to true via a modifier key, while you click any palette icon; a real lifesaver when a resumeStack or openCard handler has run amok, or when you're checking out someone else's stack and want to avoid transition effect scripts, 'security' handlers, etc. Of course, if you don't mind adding about five lines of code to your Home stack script, you can also use the MiniShow XCMD, which keeps the palette 'pinned' above the center of the current stack. (Of course, it's smart enough to take the title bar and menu bar into account, and it never lets the palette become lost chasing off-screen card window locations). Complete documentation is in the stack, and the palette displays a comprehensive Help window with a single click. The stack, palette, and MiniShow external require HC 2.0 or higher. A couple of bonus externals that work with HC 1.2.2 or higher are also included. Grokware -- if you 'get' it, you can keep it.

_source.title: Space-Aligned Columns
_source.creator: Michael Riesman
_source.description: This utility converts tab-delimited text from a database or word processor into space-separated columns, with each tab replaced by the correct number of (monospace-font) spaces to align the columns. The original purpose of this utility was to be able to use the "PRE" HTML tag to properly display columns of tab-delimited data on a web browser, but it is useful for any situation where tabs must be replaced. This is freeware. First, paste the tabbed text into this field (the tabs will show as spaces, but they're still there). Then click the "No. of Columns" button and enter the number, then click the "Widths" button which will ask you for column widths (number of characters), which should be entered separated by commas. Then click "Justify" if you want anything other than left justified columns. Each column can have its own justification; left, center, or right. Then click "Add space" if you need to insert extra space between any columns (such as a left-justified column next to a right-justified column). Finally "Convert" will do the conversion, take you to the next page, and select it all in preparation for copying and pasting. If the total number of characters in each row is wider than the window, it won't look good yet - you have to paste it into a wide-enough window to see the true results. As is often the case, creating this solution took longer than putting in spaces by hand in the original situation. But, if I ever have to do it again, now I have a tool. And so do you.

_source.title: HTML Optimizer 3.0.3
_source.creator: Ton Brand
_source.description: HTML Optimizer offers FOUR functions: 1. HTML Optimizer saves 10 to 15% of space on your web pages by removing unnecessary characters and tags. Though this may seem a small improvement, load time savings is much higher as your client's Internet browser parses the pages more efficient. Embedded script code is not ruined by HTML Optimizer! Your original pages remain untouched for later updating. New text files are created with same Creator as original and are put in a folder named "Optimizer Output". Take care that you do not replace your original HTML pages with optimized ones because you may lose your nicely indented layout. Keep the originals in a safe place. 2. Another useful function of HTML Optimizer is checking the socalled tagged pairs, i.e. the tags that always have to appear together with their end tags, for example .... All dangling tags are reported. Further, the IMG tags are checked for the presence of the Width, Height and Alt attributes. The Width and Height attributes help the browser to build up the page faster because it can reserve the necessary room for the image while placing the text. The Alt attributes make your pages more user-friendly. 3. The Mark function does the same as the Check function, but produces new files with the incomplete tags marked with the character •, so that you can easily find them for fixing. 4. The Convert function takes care that special characters between ASCII 128 and 255 are converted to the appropriate &.....; notation.

_source.title: Timed-Out v1.11
_source.creator: Bob Hangebrauck
_source.description: Version1.11.....January 11, 1993 ___________________ Even though HyperCard is a handy, simple-to-work-with database, it cannot be shared by multiple users on a network. When the standard System 7 sharing is used, I believe a stack open on one computer can be accessed as read only by another computer. Worse yet, using Appleshare on a server, a person trying to access a stack on the server that is already in use gets a message something like 'This stack is busy.' I hope this can be remedied in the next update of HyperCard. One way to help work around this problem, when you need to have multiple stack users, is to have the database stack automatically quit afer a defined period of time. This stack contains a simple script at the stack level to do just that. After 5 minutes, it will show a hidden field stating...... 'If you need another 5 minutes use-time, hit the 'Reset Use-time' button, otherwise this stack will quit in 1 minute to make it available for other users.' The stack's 'Reset Use-time' button can be used to respond to or preempt the inevitable warning. Copy the script, button, and hidden background field (named 'Warning') to your HyperCard database stack, and change the timing to whatever is reasonable for typical use of your database. If you have other solutions, I think a lot of Mac users would be appreciative. Robert Hangebrauck...RobertPH on American Online....Internet: robertph@aol.com

_source.title: Pediatric Diagnosis #2(PEDDX2...
_source.creator: Clarens
_source.description: These Hypercard stacks require Hypercard v. 2 and because of their size must be run from either a hard disk or a 1.4 MB floppy drive. The hard disk is the preferred storage medium since they run v-e-r-y s-l-o-w-l-y on a floppy drive. This is the second of a series of interactive Hypercard stacks designed to test one's ability to approach the diagnosis of a pediatric patient's illness in a logical and thoughtful fashion. The stacks are designed so as to be as close to 'real life' as is possible, including the element of time. You are given the history of three patients' illnesses and pertinent physical findings. After that you may request those laboratory studies you deem necessary until you feel certain of the diagnosis and proper therapy for each patient. The 'grades' you receive are based upon the cost of the studies and hospitalization (if needed) to the patient; it is hoped that this method will give the 'testee' an appreciation of the high cost of medical care and the necessity for being thoughful in the way he/she orders laboratory studies. The denouement of each case discusses the differential diagnosis as well as a logical approach to the diagnosis. Each case has with it a current (1992) bibliography. The stacks are designed for medical students, house officers and physicians but may be of interest to others. Clare N. Shumway, M.D. (Clarens)

_source.title: MerryXmas Virus
_source.creator: Unknown
_source.description: MerryXmas was the first documented HyperCard virus, and was the only prevalent HyperCard virus for many years. When an infected stack is opened, the virus script copies itself to the Home stack. From there, it spreads to every other stack that is opened. Unopened stacks are not affected, but it still does not take long for most of the stacks on a hard drive to contract the virus. The original intent of MerryXmas was to simply propagate, but because of a typing error, the virus can sometimes copy parts of other stack scripts along with its own virus code. This causes stacks to behave unexpectedly or sometimes to error. In certain circumstances, MerryXmas also causes HyperCard to quit (but not crash) without any notification. (from https://www.hyperactivesw.com/hypercard/virus1.html ) This stack includes both a stack to infect the Home stack (in the disk 'Virus') and the tool 'MerryXmas Vaccine' (in the disk 'disk') that can detect and disinfect the virus. Please note that the emulator is an isolated environment and therefore all changes (and infected stacks) are lost when the emulator is closed. The came cannot be said if you download this stack to run elsewhere - extreme caution is advised! Particular thanks to Space Rogue of The Whacked Mac Archives for tracking down this virus so it can be preserved in the HyperCard collection.

_source.title: Hackintosh 1.7
_source.creator: T. James Vano
_source.description: Hackintosh is a cool program I wrote whilst completely toasted on caffeine. (Come to think of it, most of my software is like that. You don't think 10-12 cups of coffee a day is too much, do you?) I designed Hackintosh for a friend of mine. He had an old laptop (5300c@117MHz) and the Finder really crawled. So, he asked me if I would write a command-line for him so he could work with his files more quickly. I picked Linux as my model, and HyperCard as my platform. For the file access, I chose to use an adaptation of some HyperTalk/AppleScript modules I'd written (which eventually became Hackintosh Core Services). Although the first version used the shell commands directly via 'start using,' subsequent versions have used a Virtual Memory Module, to load an almost unlimited number of shell commands. What started out as a quick hack for navigating and manipulating files quickly turned into something far more powerful. Because of the inherent extendability of the HyperTalk scripting language, plus the OSA and XCMD/XFCN interface, Hackintosh can be programmed to do just about anything, completely via shell commands. You can write shell commands for complex file management, resource control, even control over the internet. In fact, if you can find/write/hack a way to do something in HyperCard, you can probably make Hackintosh do it.

_source.title: HyperTrax Converter.SIT
_source.creator: Lance Havoc
_source.description: ----------------- What is this thing? This is a utility stack which converts most HyperTrax,SoundWave, SoundCap and other digital sound files that are a.k.a. 'FSSD',into 'Snd' resources, which can be used for HyperCard stacks, Mac2 beeps or anywhere else you can call up the resource. It is input sensitive, but it is not output sensitive...so be careful! This is a resource editor, so handle it like a gun. I've seen grown men cry because they had thought it would be funny to put the theme to the 'Munsters' into their only copy of PageMaker. It is important that you keep in mind that digital sounds are RAM eaters. They do not get along well with other RAM dwellers such as talking mooses (meese?), screen savers, RAM cache's, trick clocks, etc...See what works with your system. Operation: The purpose of this program is to convert a sound file into a resource, and then install it on to a stack or application of your choice. Clicking within either the Source Sound icons or on the Source Path field, will prompt you to open a sound file. After selecting your sound, the Source Path will indicate the path to your selection. Target selection works the same way, only it will let you select ANYTHING you want to send the sound to.

_source.title: +Living with HIV Disease
_source.creator: Nursing Masters Candidates
_source.description: This Hypercard Stack has been created by a group of Graduate Nursing Students at the University of Massachusetts at Boston. We all have been involved in healthcare for many years. We all share a strong interest in facilitating and improving the quality of life of people living with chronic HIV disease and in providing the best form of resource advocacy that we can. People coping with HIV disease have very unique and special needs, and these needs are the focus of this stack. We've created this stack as a seed to be planted in Cyberspace. We hope that you will add resources which you think might be of value. Please continue to pass the stack along. Please add your name to our list, provide some information about yourself, if you choose, and include your Email address if you'd be willing to act as a resource person, or simply if you'd like to keep in touch. We ask that you leave the name of the stack unchanged, this will enable us to locate the stack in the future. Thanks for your help. Be happy.... (Staff note: Good info, well done. Simple interface, but effective and moving.)

_source.title: Alert Manager
_source.creator: Dean Wette
_source.description: ----------------- Alert Manager is a development utility that implements standard type alert dialogs (OK response, CONTINUE response, & OK/CANCEL response with STOP, CAUTION, and NOTE alert types) in HyperCard stacks using Jay Hodgdon's ShowDialog XCMD. To call any type of alert from your scripts, you need only provide the alert text strings and the call to Alert Manager. The Alert Manager scripts handle everything else. As an added feature, you can control the alert dialog display and system beep in varying ways at three different user-configurable help levels. Any required conditional branching is handled automatically. The Alert Manager is very flexible (18 different alert dialogs possible) and easy to use and script. This demo stack provides complete examples, instructons, a tutorial., and automated installation of scripts and resources. Alert Manager is designed to work with version 1.5 of ShowDialog (included). The Alert Manager 1.1.6 Upgrade Script--included with this file--contains the handlers necessary to make Alert Manager compatible with ShowDialog version 1.6.

_source.title: Mental Giant
_source.creator: Glen L. Bledsoe
_source.description: Mental Giant is a HyperCard stack which develops one's ability to perform mental math. Click the 'Begin' button to begin (what else?). After determining your choice of either 'Silent' or 'Speaking' version, your gender, and asking your name, Mental Giant will then ask a series of twenty-five mental math problems. Type in the answer when presented with a dialog box and either push return, enter, or click OK. Players are timed how long it takes them to finish. The smaller the amount of time it takes a player to finish, the higher his or her standing in the 'Masters' listing; only the lowest ten are listed. (Hint: the silent version runs a little faster). You may check the listings before you begin by clicking the 'Scores' button. These listings will also be shown at the end should your score be listed. A 'thermometer' (Mental Measure) along the right hand side of the card registers your progress. The rest is practice, and a little 'evolution.' If you enjoyed my stack, 'The House of Multiplication Horror,' you will enjoy this stack as well.
_source.modernAnalog: A focused maker utility that helps me automate or inspect one technical task without needing a full desktop toolkit.

_source.description: This edition of recursion engine fixes a few very embarassing bugs which appeared in the original. these include: - would crash if userlevel was not set to 5. - was system seven dependent. - one of the cards was dysfunctional. - misspellings and other small errors. what it does: recursion engine generates recursion fractals - the koch curve, snowflakes, dragons, the maltese cross, etc - in outline form. it does this very quickly and smoothly, and does not depend on XCMD's for execution. it is designed with an eye towards experimentation, and is rather heavily documented in the scripts as well as within the cards. it is a bit fangled, and has lots of bells and whistles, even though i have really TRIED to keep the thing as simple as possible without hurting performance. it comes ready with several cards already built to plot recursion fractals, as well as a pi lesson, logarythm chart, and a title page packed with help. you'll be laughing in the face of infinity in no time.

Keep it private but let me share specific items when someone asks.

_source.title: Hanrek XCMD Shell 1.2
_source.creator: Mark Hanrek
_source.description: This XCMD shell is unique, in that it makes it possible for one to be immediately successful creating XCMDs in C without having to know anything about the inner workings and mechanisms of HyperCard externals. If you have, say, a function in C that you would like to call as an XCMD or XFCN, all you have to do is to open a single source file, paste the function in, copy and paste in a couple of statements and adjust them slightly (to hook up with any parameters and return result), compile it, and you're outta there! If you do not have version 7 of Symantec C, (Think C) then no problem. A HyperCard stack shows you visually the settings you need to make for a Think C project of any version. Things are structured so that you can get in and get out quickly, yet you also can get in to everything if you want. In addition, there are extensive notes to help with the commonly confusing things, such as working with handles, pointers, strings, etc.
_source.modernAnalog: A focused maker utility that helps me automate or inspect one technical task without needing a full desktop toolkit.

_source.title: ChangeObjectLayer.SIT
_source.creator: Dean Wette
_source.description: While developing a stack containing numerous fields and buttons, I found myself constantly using the Send Farther and Bring Closer commands in the Objects menu in order to move objects (buttons and fields) through different card and background layers. In some cases I wanted to obscure other objects, and in other cases I wanted to prevent objects from being obscured. Well, when you have 20 or more each of background and card buttons and fields, this menu selection (or its command key equivalent) business to change object layers can become quite tedious, boring, and unfriendly. Therefore, I developed 'ChangeObjectLayer' to automate and speed-up the process of moving objects through different layers. 'ChangeObjectLayer' works with both card and background buttons and fields. Try it out! On this card are six each of card and background buttons and fields. Click on any one of these objects to change its order in the object layers.

_source.description: ----------------- This is the new and MUCH improved Sorter Stack, the older versions having several bugs, and the documentation being vague. Although originally created to assist in formatting footnotes created from medical literature searches, this stack is for anyone with data to sort and alphabetize (one field per card--no tab delim fields). Will import a TEXT file, delete any numbers, alphabetize it, renumber, and export to a text file. Improvements include several new buttons, a much faster interface, a scanner button to prescan literature searches downloaded from a medical online service such as MedLine (through Colleague or PaperChase), and a SUPER button which automates the Import through Export functions. Multiple files can be stacked. There is now a find feature, much better online help, and new musical sounds plus a button to turn the music off. Extensive documentation provided.
